```mermaid
    H2 --> N
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
*Distinguish between **form drag** and **skin-friction drag** with a suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Form (Pressure) Drag** arises from the imbalance of surface pressure when the flow **separates** from the body, creating a low-pressure wake. *Example:* Drag on a flat plate held normal to the flow, or a blunt-nosed re-entry capsule.
- **Skin-Friction Drag** arises from **viscous shear stresses** in the thin boundary layer adhering to the wetted surface, with *no* flow separation. *Example:* Drag on a long, slender, **streamlined** body at low angle of attack.
- The key distinction is the **dominant physical mechanism**: pressure imbalance vs. wall shear. (1 mark each mechanism + 1 mark example) → **3/3**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
*Define the **aspect ratio** of a wing and state its effect on **induced drag**.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** $AR = b^{2}/S$ where $b$ is wing span and $S$ is wing planform area. (1 mark)
- **Effect on $C_{D_i}$:** From $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi e\, AR)$, induced drag is **inversely proportional** to $AR$. (1 mark)
- **Practical implication:** Doubling $AR$ *halves* $C_{D_i}$ — hence long, slender, high-AR wings (gliders, U-2, airliners) are used for **efficient cruise**. (1 mark) → **3/3**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the **fundamental drag equation** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$ starting from the surface integral of pressure and shear. **State clearly the physical meaning of every term.** (7 marks)

**(b)** A small UAV has the following flight-test data at sea level: $V = 60\ \text{m/s}$, $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $S = 0.8\ \text{m}^{2}$, $C_L = 0.6$, $C_{D_0} = 0.022$, $AR = 8$, $e = 0.85$.
Compute: **(i)** Induced drag coefficient, **(ii)** Total drag coefficient, **(iii)** Total drag force in Newtons. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Q A (a):** *(7 marks)*

1. *Surface-integral starting point* (1 mark):

$$F_D = \oint_{S_{wet}} \bigl[(p_\infty - p)\cos\theta + \tau_w \sin\theta\bigr]\, dS$$

2. *Non-dimensionalization by $q_\infty S$* (1 mark) — leading to $C_D$ definition.

3. *Final rearranged form* (1 mark):

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$$

4. *Term meanings* (2 marks):
- $\rho$ → freestream density,
- $V$ → true airspeed,
- $S$ → reference area (wing planform),
- $C_D$ → drag coefficient (geometry + flow physics).

5. *Significance* (2 marks): $F_D \propto V^{2}$, which is why doubling the speed *quadruples* the drag — a critical insight for fuel-burn and stall-speed analysis.

---

**Model Solution — Q A (b):** *(7 marks)*

**(i) Induced drag coefficient** (2 marks):

$$
C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_L^{2}}{\pi e\, AR} = \dfrac{(0.6)^{2}}{\pi (0.85)(8)} = \dfrac{0.36}{21.363} \approx 0.0169
$$

**(ii) Total drag coefficient** (2 marks):

$$C_D = C_{D_0} + C_{D_i} = 0.022 + 0.0169 = 0.0389$$

**(iii) Total drag force** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(60)^{2}(0.8)(0.0389) \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 0.8 \times 0.0389 \\
    &= 68.61\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

✔ *Result:* $F_D \approx \mathbf{68.6\ N}$.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **physical origin of induced drag** on a finite wing. Show mathematically that $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi e\, AR)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **wave drag**? Explain the concept of the **critical Mach number $M_{cr}$** and describe the **area rule** as a means of reducing wave drag. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Q B (a):** *(7 marks)*

1. *Finite-wing explanation* (2 marks): Air spills from below to above the wing at the tips, forming **trailing vortices** with strength $\Gamma$.
2. *Downwash $w$* (1 mark) — these vortices induce a downward velocity behind the wing.
3. *Lifting-line derivation* (3 marks): Following Prandtl's lifting-line analysis, $w = L/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty \pi b^{2})$, substituting and resolving the tilted lift vector gives the in-class derivation shown in **Section 3.2** above, leading to:

$$C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_L^{2}}{\pi e\, AR}$$

4. *Oswald factor $e$* (1 mark): empirical correction (0.7–0.95) for non-elliptic lift distribution.

---

**Model Solution — Q B (b):** *(7 marks)*

1. **Wave drag definition** (1 mark): A drag component that appears in **compressible flow** due to the formation of **shock waves**, across which kinetic energy is irreversibly lost to heat.
2. **Critical Mach number $M_{cr}$** (2 marks): The freestream Mach number at which the *local* Mach number first reaches 1 somewhere on the body, marking the onset of transonic drag divergence. For thin airfoils, $M_{cr} \approx 1 - \tau$.
3. **Why it happens** (1 mark): At higher $M$, local flow acceleration over the body crest reaches sonic; further increase produces a normal shock.
4. **Whitcomb Area Rule** (2 marks): For a given volume, the *longitudinal cross-sectional area distribution* should vary as smoothly as possible. Abrupt changes in area (e.g., wing-fuselage junction) concentrate shock strength; smoothly waisting the fuselage (coke-bottle shape) reduces wave drag. Examples: Concorde, F-102, SR-71 Blackbird.
5. **Engineering takeaway** (1 mark): Modern transport aircraft operate at $M \approx 0.82$–$0.85$ to remain safely below $M_{cr}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on "Types of Drag" questions:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Forgetting to convert $V$ from km/h to m/s in $F_D$ calculations. *Examiner will deduct 2 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/2$ factor** in the dynamic-pressure term. *Very common; loses the method mark.*
> 3. **Conflating form drag and skin-friction drag** in a 14-mark essay — examiners award marks for *contrast*. Always end with a one-line comparison.
> 4. **Drawing the lifting-line figure wrong:** Wingtip vortices should *trail behind* the wing and *rotate outward-on-top / inward-on-bottom* (right wing). Drawing them rotating the opposite way is a **-1 mark** deduction.
> 5. **Mis-stating $AR$:** Writing $AR = b/S$ instead of $b^{2}/S$ is a unit-error and forfeits the formula mark.
> 6. **In wave-drag questions**, students often forget to mention the *irreversible* nature of the energy loss across a shock — a key 1-mark point.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Drag = resistance parallel to freestream**, defined as $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$.
- [x] **Parasitic drag** ($C_{D_0}$) is **lift-independent**; dominated by form + skin friction.
- [x] **Form drag** ⇔ pressure imbalance from **flow separation**.
- [x] **Skin-friction drag** ⇔ viscous **shear stress** in the boundary layer.
- [x] **Interference drag** ⇔ non-linear addition of body fields at junctions.
- [x] **Leakage drag** ⇔ unintended internal flow through gaps/seals.
- [x] **Induced drag** ⇔ tilted lift vector due to **wingtip vortices & downwash**.
- [x] Induced drag: $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi e\, AR)$ — **inversely proportional to AR**.
- [x] **Wave drag** ⇔ **shock-wave losses**, dominant in **transonic/supersonic** flight.
- [x] **Whitcomb area rule** ⇔ smooth longitudinal area distribution reduces wave drag.
- [x] **Total drag polar:** $C_D = C_{D_0} + C_L^{2}/(\pi e\, AR)$.
- [x] **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V c/\mu$ governs boundary-layer state.
- [x] **Mach number** $M = V/a$ governs compressibility & wave-drag onset.
- [x] **Instrumentation link:** Drag is *measured* via pressure probes, wake rakes, strain-gauge balances, and pitot-static systems — all feeding the $F_D$ equation.
- [x] **Key trick for KTU:** Remember the **drag-divergence Mach number** $M_{dd} \approx M_{cr} + 0.05$–$0.08$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite Wings and Swept Wings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Finite Wings and Swept Wings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Finite Wing?

A **Finite Wing** is a wing having a finite span (finite Aspect Ratio), with distinct wing tips where the high-pressure air on the lower surface can leak around to the low-pressure region on the upper surface. This tip leakage creates **trailing vortices**, **downwash**, and **induced (drag) forces** — phenomena that are completely absent in the idealized "infinite wing" (2-D) case studied via airfoil theory.

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S} = \frac{b}{c_{\text{avg}}}$$

where **b** is the wing span and **S** is the planform area. The Aspect Ratio (AR) is the single most important geometric parameter governing finite-wing aerodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A finite wing is one in which the flow at the wing tip is three-dimensional, producing induced drag and reducing the lift curve slope from its 2-D (infinite wing) value.

## 1.2 What is a Swept Wing?

A **Swept Wing** is a wing whose leading edge (and usually also the quarter-chord line) is inclined at an angle **Λ (Lambda)** to the direction perpendicular to the aircraft's longitudinal axis. The leading-edge sweep angle Λ is measured from the lateral (perpendicular-to-fuselage) direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sweep Angle Λ (Lambda):** The angle between the wing's leading edge (or reference line) and a line perpendicular to the aircraft's longitudinal axis. Expressed in degrees.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Finite Wing Analogy — The "Row of Pushers" :**
Imagine a single person pushing a large board through still water. They can push it with full force. Now imagine a row of 10 people pushing the same board edge-to-edge. The people at the ends (tips) have no one beside them, so the water at the edges of the board can curl around the tips and disturb the flow — the board feels "looser." The end people are effectively doing less useful work. **In a finite wing, the "tip" regions produce less lift, and the energy lost to swirling tip vortices is the induced drag.**

**Swept Wing Analogy — The "Skating Forward" :**
Picture a hockey player skating forward with their stick held straight ahead versus angled at 45° to their direction of motion. The angled stick meets the air (or any fluid) more "sideways" — the **component of flow perpendicular to the leading edge** is what the wing "feels." By sweeping back, the wing effectively sees a slower freestream component normal to its leading edge, delaying transonic effects.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Parameters (Always bolded in answers):**
> - **Aspect Ratio (AR)** = b²/S — dimensionless
> - **Oswald Efficiency Factor (e)** ≈ 0.8 – 0.95 for clean wings
> - **Induced Drag Coefficient (C<sub>D,i</sub>)** ∝ 1/(π·e·AR)
> - **Sweep Angle (Λ)** measured in **degrees**, critical for high-subsonic/transonic aircraft
> - **Critical Mach Number (M<sub>cr</sub>)** for swept wing ≈ M<sub>∞</sub>·cos(Λ) (component-normal approximation)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Finite Wings Behave Differently from Airfoils

In 2-D (infinite) wing theory, we assume:
- No flow variation in the spanwise direction
- No tip effects
- Lift is purely a function of α and airfoil shape

**The "Why" — Three-Dimensionality at the Tip:**
A real wing has a finite span. The pressure difference between the lower (high pressure) and upper (low pressure) surfaces cannot be sustained at the tip. Air spills from below to above, rolling up into two **counter-rotating trailing vortices** (port-side vortex rotates clockwise, starboard vortex rotates counter-clockwise when viewed from behind the aircraft).

**The "How" — Downwash Mechanism:**
The trailing vortices induce a downward velocity component (downwash, **w**) on the wing itself. This downwash tilts the local freestream vector backward by an angle **ε (epsilon)** — the **induced angle of attack**. The actual angle of attack the airfoil "sees" becomes:

$$\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \alpha - \epsilon$$

The lift vector, originally perpendicular to the freestream, now tilts backward by ε, producing a horizontal component — **Induced Drag (D<sub>i</sub>)**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Finite-Wing Aerodynamics

1. **Step 1:** Aircraft moves at velocity V<sub>∞</sub>; wing produces lift L.
2. **Step 2:** Pressure difference Δp between lower and upper surfaces tries to equalize at the tips.
3. **Step 3:** Air flows from below to above at the tips, creating trailing vortices of strength Γ.
4. **Step 4:** Biot-Savart law: these vortices induce a downward velocity **w** at the wing's location.
5. **Step 5:** Downwash tilts the local flow by angle ε = arctan(w/V<sub>∞</sub>) ≈ w/V<sub>∞</sub>.
6. **Step 6:** Effective angle of attack becomes α<sub>eff</sub> = α − ε.
7. **Step 7:** Lift reduces (since α<sub>eff</sub> < α) → new lift curve slope: a = a₀/(1 + a₀/(π·e·AR)).
8. **Step 8:** Tilted lift vector has a horizontal component = Induced Drag D<sub>i</sub>.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $AR = b^2/S$ | Aspect ratio; high AR ⇒ low induced drag (gliders use AR > 30) |
| 2 | $a = a_0 / (1 + a_0/(\pi e AR))$ | 3-D lift curve slope; used in stability & control derivations |
| 3 | $C_{L} = a (\alpha - \alpha_{0})$ | 3-D lift coefficient; cornerstone of all flight performance |
| 4 | $C_{D,i} = C_{L}^{2}/(\pi e AR)$ | Induced drag coefficient (Lifting-Line Theory) |
| 5 | $C_{D} = C_{D,0} + C_{L}^{2}/(\pi e AR)$ | **Total drag polar** — most-tested formula in KTU |
| 6 | $L/D_{\max} = 0.5\sqrt{\pi e AR / C_{D,0}}$ | Maximum aerodynamic efficiency; used in range/endurance |
| 7 | $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = M_{cr,\text{unswept}} / \cos \Lambda$ | Effective critical Mach on swept wing (cosine rule) |
| 8 | $V_{n,\text{swept}} = V_{\infty} \cos \Lambda$ | Velocity component normal to leading edge |
| 9 | $\epsilon = 2 C_{L}/(\pi e AR)$ | Downwash angle in radians |
| 10 | $b_{\text{local}} = b \cdot \eta$ where $\eta = 2y/b$ | Non-dimensional spanwise coordinate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The total drag polar $C_D = C_{D,0} + C_L^2/(\pi e AR)$ is the **most frequently asked** formula in Module 1 questions. Memorize it verbatim with the constant π·e·AR in the denominator.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Transports (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Use **swept wings** with AR ≈ 9–11 and Λ ≈ 25°–35° to cruise efficiently at M ≈ 0.85 while delaying drag-divergence Mach number.
- **Sailplanes (Gliders):** Use **high-AR unswept wings** (AR > 30) to minimize induced drag and maximize L/D for soaring.
- **Fighter Aircraft (F-22, Su-57):** Use **moderate sweep (Λ ≈ 45°–50°)** plus **variable-sweep** to balance transonic maneuverability with supersonic dash capability.
- **UAVs/Micro-Air-Vehicles:** Low-AR wings produce large induced drag but enable tight turns and hover.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Induced Drag from Downwash (Lifting-Line Concept)

### 3.1.1 Start with Lift and Drag Components on a Wing Section

Consider an elemental wing section at spanwise station **y** with local lift **L′** (lift per unit span) and local induced drag **dD<sub>i</sub>**.

The total lift vector is perpendicular to the local flow (which is tilted by ε due to downwash). Decompose into vertical (useful lift) and horizontal (induced drag) components:

$$L'_{\text{useful}} = L' \cos \epsilon \approx L' \quad (\text{for small } \epsilon)$$

$$dD_i = L' \sin \epsilon \approx L' \epsilon$$

### 3.1.2 Express Lift per Unit Span in Terms of Local Circulation

From thin-airfoil theory, lift per unit span relates to circulation Γ(y) by the **Kutta-Joukowski theorem**:

$$L'(y) = \rho_\infty V_\infty \Gamma(y)$$

The downwash induced at a spanwise station **y** by the entire trailing-vortex sheet is obtained from the **Biot-Savart law** applied to the lifting line. For a general lift distribution, Prandtl's lifting-line theory gives:

$$\epsilon(y) = \frac{1}{4\pi V_\infty} \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \frac{(d\Gamma/dy_0)}{(y - y_0)} \, dy_0$$

### 3.1.3 Assume Elliptic Lift Distribution (Optimal Case)

Assume the **elliptic lift distribution** (which Prandtl proved minimizes induced drag):

$$\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0 \sqrt{1 - (2y/b)^2} = \Gamma_0 \sqrt{1 - \eta^2}$$

where $\eta = 2y/b$ is the non-dimensional span coordinate. The total lift becomes:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \Gamma(y) \, dy \cdot 2 = \rho V_\infty \int_0^{b/2} \Gamma_0 \sqrt{1 - \eta^2} \, dy$$

### 3.1.4 Solve the Lifting-Line Equation for Elliptic Distribution

The Prandtl lifting-line equation is:

$$a_0 \left[\alpha(y) - \alpha_{0,L=0}(y)\right] = \frac{2\Gamma(y)}{V_\infty c(y)} + \frac{1}{V_\infty} \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \frac{d\Gamma/dy_0}{y - y_0} dy_0$$

For the elliptic distribution, the integral evaluates to a **constant downwash** across the span:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Gamma_0}{2 b V_\infty} \cdot 2 \pi \cdot \text{(geometric factor)} = \frac{2 C_L}{\pi e AR}$$

### 3.1.5 Compute Induced Drag Coefficient

The total induced drag is:

$$D_i = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} L'(y) \, \epsilon(y) \, dy = \rho_\infty V_\infty \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \Gamma(y) \epsilon(y) \, dy$$

Substituting elliptic Γ and constant ε, performing the integration, and non-dimensionalizing with dynamic pressure q<sub>∞</sub>S gives:

$$C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR}$$

This is the **fundamental induced-drag result** of Prandtl's lifting-line theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 1/AR dependence is why gliders use very long, slender wings — doubling the aspect ratio halves the induced drag for the same lift.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Sweep on Critical Mach Number

### 3.2.1 Physical Setup

At transonic speeds, local Mach number over the wing approaches unity, causing shock formation and drag divergence. Sweep effectively reduces the velocity "felt" perpendicular to the leading edge.

### 3.2.2 Velocity Decomposition

Let V<sub>∞</sub> be the freestream velocity and Λ the leading-edge sweep. The component of freestream velocity **normal to the leading edge** is:

$$V_n = V_\infty \cos \Lambda$$

The component **along the leading edge** is:

$$V_t = V_\infty \sin \Lambda$$

Only the normal component contributes to the pressure distribution (by linearized supersonic/subsonic potential-flow theory). Therefore, the effective Mach number for the wing section is:

$$M_{n,\text{eff}} = M_\infty \cos \Lambda$$

### 3.2.3 Critical Mach Number Relationship

The critical Mach number of the **unswept** wing is M<sub>cr,0</sub>. For the **swept** wing, the critical condition becomes:

$$M_{cr,\text{swept}} \cos \Lambda = M_{cr,0}$$

Solving for the swept-wing critical Mach number:

$$M_{cr,\text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr,0}}{\cos \Lambda}$$

For Λ = 30°, cos(30°) = 0.866, so critical Mach increases by ≈15% — a major benefit for transonic transport aircraft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Critical Result:** $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = M_{cr,0}/\cos \Lambda$ — sweep delays drag divergence. This formula appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 sweep question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Induced Drag Variation with Sweep

| Sweep Angle Λ | Aspect Ratio Reduction Factor | Effective AR | % Increase in C<sub>D,i</sub> (constant C<sub>L</sub>) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0° | 1.000 | AR | 0% |
| 15° | 0.983 | 0.983·AR | +1.7% |
| 30° | 0.935 | 0.935·AR | +7.0% |
| 45° | 0.854 | 0.854·AR | +17.1% |
| 60° | 0.750 | 0.750·AR | +33.3% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-Intuitive Trap:** Although sweep delays compressibility drag, it *increases* induced drag because sweep effectively reduces the **normal** velocity, which is equivalent to a reduction in effective aspect ratio.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Aspect Ratio and Induced Drag. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

**Aspect Ratio (AR):** It is defined as the ratio of the square of the wing span to the wing planform area, expressed as:

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S}$$

A high aspect ratio wing (e.g., glider, AR > 30) produces low induced drag and high aerodynamic efficiency.

**Induced Drag (D<sub>i</sub>):** It is the drag produced as a byproduct of lift generation, caused by the trailing vortices shed at the wing tips. These vortices induce a downwash which tilts the lift vector aft, producing a horizontal drag component. Its coefficient is:

$$C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \, e \, AR}$$

Induced drag decreases with increasing AR and decreases with increasing speed (since C<sub>L</sub> is lower at high speed).

---

### Question 2: What is leading-edge sweep and why is it used in transonic aircraft? [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

**Leading-Edge Sweep (Λ):** The angle between the wing's leading edge and a line perpendicular to the aircraft's longitudinal axis.

**Why it is used:** At transonic cruise Mach numbers (M ≈ 0.8–0.9), local airflow over the wing accelerates to M = 1, causing shock formation and drag divergence. By sweeping the wing back by angle Λ, the velocity component normal to the leading edge reduces to V<sub>∞</sub>·cos(Λ). The effective Mach number becomes M<sub>∞</sub>·cos(Λ), which delays the onset of compressibility effects and allows higher cruise speeds.

$$M_{cr,\text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr,\text{unswept}}}{\cos \Lambda}$$

For Λ = 30°, this raises the critical Mach number by ≈15%.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**(a)** Derive the induced drag coefficient $C_{D,i} = C_L^2/(\pi e AR)$ for a finite wing with elliptic lift distribution. State clearly the assumptions used. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For a transport aircraft, b = 35 m, S = 110 m², e = 0.85, C<sub>D,0</sub> = 0.022, ρ = 0.549 kg/m³ (at 8 km), W = 5×10⁵ N. Compute: (i) Aspect Ratio, (ii) C<sub>L</sub> at cruise V = 230 m/s, (iii) Total drag coefficient, (iv) Induced drag in Newtons, (v) Maximum L/D ratio. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow outside the boundary layer.
2. Attached flow; small angle of attack and small downwash.
3. Elliptic spanwise lift distribution (L(y) ∝ √(1 − (2y/b)²)).
4. Constant downwash ε along the span (a key consequence of the elliptic distribution).
5. Lifting-line replaces the wing; trailing-vortex sheet is planar at the wing.

**Derivation Outline:**

Starting from the Prandtl lifting-line equation (a boundary-value integral equation in Γ(y)):

$$a_0 \left[\alpha - \alpha_{0}(y)\right] = \frac{2 \Gamma(y)}{V_\infty c(y)} + \frac{1}{V_\infty}\int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \frac{d\Gamma/dy_0}{y - y_0}\, dy_0$$

**Step 1:** Assume $\Gamma(y) = \Gamma_0 \sqrt{1 - (2y/b)^2}$.

**Step 2:** The Hilbert-type integral evaluates to a constant equal to $-2\Gamma_0/b$ (can be shown via residue theorem or known integral identity).

**Step 3:** The equation becomes decoupled (constant downwash):

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Gamma_0}{2b V_\infty} \cdot 2\pi = \frac{2 C_L}{\pi e AR}$$

**Step 4:** Induced drag is the rearward tilt of lift:

$$D_i = L \tan \epsilon \approx L \epsilon = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 S C_L \cdot \frac{2 C_L}{\pi e AR} = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 S \cdot \frac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR}$$

**Step 5:** Divide by dynamic pressure q<sub>∞</sub>S to obtain the non-dimensional coefficient:

$$\boxed{C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR}}$$

> **Examiner's Note (1 mark):** Explicitly state the assumption of elliptic lift distribution, which gives the **minimum** induced drag (and e = 1).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** b = 35 m, S = 110 m², e = 0.85, C<sub>D,0</sub> = 0.022, ρ = 0.549 kg/m³, W = 5×10⁵ N, V = 230 m/s.

**(i) Aspect Ratio:**

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S} = \frac{35^2}{110} = \frac{1225}{110} = 11.136$$

**(ii) Lift Coefficient at Cruise:**

$$C_L = \frac{W}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{0.5 \times 0.549 \times 230^2 \times 110}$$

Calculate denominator:

$$0.5 \times 0.549 \times 52900 \times 110 = 0.5 \times 0.549 \times 5,819,000$$

$$= 0.5 \times 3,194,631 = 1,597,315.5 \, \text{N}$$

So:

$$C_L = \frac{500{,}000}{1{,}597{,}315.5} = 0.3130$$

**(iii) Total Drag Coefficient:**

$$C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR} = \frac{(0.3130)^2}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 11.136} = \frac{0.09797}{29.72} = 0.003297$$

$$C_D = C_{D,0} + C_{D,i} = 0.022 + 0.003297 = 0.0253$$

**(iv) Induced Drag in Newtons:**

$$D_i = q_\infty S \, C_{D,i} = 1{,}597{,}315.5 \times 0.003297 = 5266.6 \, \text{N} \approx 5.27 \, \text{kN}$$

**(v) Maximum L/D Ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = 0.5 \sqrt{\frac{\pi e AR}{C_{D,0}}} = 0.5 \sqrt{\frac{\pi \times 0.85 \times 11.136}{0.022}}$$

$$= 0.5 \sqrt{\frac{29.72}{0.022}} = 0.5 \sqrt{1350.9} = 0.5 \times 36.76 = 18.38$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---|:---|
| AR | 11.14 |
| C<sub>L</sub> | 0.313 |
| C<sub>D</sub> | 0.0253 |
| D<sub>i</sub> | ≈ 5.27 kN |
| (L/D)<sub>max</sub> | 18.38 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **downwash and induced drag** in a finite wing. With neat sketches, show how the trailing vortices are formed and how they tilt the lift vector. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A wing has b = 12 m, S = 18 m², e = 0.9, and C<sub>D,0</sub> = 0.018. The aircraft flies at sea level (ρ = 1.225 kg/m³) at V = 80 m/s with weight W = 25,000 N. Calculate: (i) AR, (ii) C<sub>L</sub>, (iii) C<sub>D,i</sub> and C<sub>D</sub>, (iv) Induced drag, (v) (L/D) at this flight condition. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Explanation (5 marks) + Sketch Description (2 marks):**

A finite wing has two surfaces — lower (high pressure) and upper (low pressure). Near the wing tips, air from the high-pressure side flows around the tip to the low-pressure side, forming **trailing vortices** that are **counter-rotating** (port-side clockwise, starboard-side counter-clockwise when viewed from behind).

These vortices, by the **Biot-Savart law**, induce a **downwash velocity w** at the location of the wing. The downwash tilts the local freestream vector downward by an angle ε = arctan(w/V<sub>∞</sub>).

The wing now "sees" an effective angle of attack:

$$\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \alpha - \epsilon$$

The lift vector, which is perpendicular to the local flow, also tilts backward by ε. Decomposing this tilted lift into vertical (useful) and horizontal (drag) components:

$$L_{\text{useful}} = L \cos \epsilon \approx L$$

$$D_i = L \sin \epsilon \approx L \epsilon$$

Since ε ∝ C<sub>L</sub>, we have D<sub>i</sub> ∝ C<sub>L</sub>² — the induced drag grows quadratically with lift, dominating at low speeds and high angles of attack (e.g., takeoff, landing, approach).

**Sketch description (text version):** Draw a plan view of a finite wing showing the two trailing vortices spiraling behind each tip. Draw a side view showing the freestream V<sub>∞</sub>, the tilted local flow at angle ε, and the resulting lift L perpendicular to the tilted flow, decomposed into vertical (L) and horizontal (D<sub>i</sub>).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** b = 12 m, S = 18 m², e = 0.9, C<sub>D,0</sub> = 0.018, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, V = 80 m/s, W = 25,000 N.

**(i) Aspect Ratio:**

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S} = \frac{144}{18} = 8.0$$

**(ii) Lift Coefficient:**

Dynamic pressure: $q_\infty = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 80^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6400 = 3920 \, \text{N/m}^2$

$$C_L = \frac{W}{q_\infty S} = \frac{25{,}000}{3920 \times 18} = \frac{25{,}000}{70{,}560} = 0.3543$$

**(iii) Induced and Total Drag Coefficient:**

$$C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR} = \frac{(0.3543)^2}{\pi \times 0.9 \times 8.0} = \frac{0.1255}{22.619} = 0.005549$$

$$C_D = C_{D,0} + C_{D,i} = 0.018 + 0.005549 = 0.0235$$

**(iv) Induced Drag:**

$$D_i = q_\infty S \, C_{D,i} = 3920 \times 18 \times 0.005549 = 70{,}560 \times 0.005549 = 391.5 \, \text{N}$$

**(v) L/D at this condition:**

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{0.3543}{0.0235} = 15.07$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---|:---|
| AR | 8.0 |
| C<sub>L</sub> | 0.354 |
| C<sub>D</sub> | 0.0235 |
| D<sub>i</sub> | 391.5 N |
| L/D | 15.07 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Before Exam!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant π·e·AR:** Students often write $C_{D,i} = C_L^2/AR$ — this loses 2 marks. The correct form is $C_{D,i} = C_L^2/(\pi e AR)$.
> 2. **Using wrong AR formula:** Some write AR = S/b². Correct: $AR = b^2/S$.
> 3. **Confusing induced and parasite drag dominance:** At LOW speed (takeoff, approach), induced drag dominates. At HIGH speed (cruise), parasite drag dominates. State this explicitly in design questions.
> 4. **Sweep direction sign error:** Sweep is measured from the lateral axis; Λ > 0° = swept back (most common). Do NOT confuse with leading-edge angle from longitudinal axis.
> 5. **Forgetting units in downwash angle:** ε is in **radians** when used in the drag formula $D_i = L \tan \epsilon$. Convert degrees→radians if needed.
> 6. **Ignoring e (Oswald factor) in drag polar:** Always include e; for "clean" elliptic-like wings, e ≈ 0.9–0.95; for rectangular wings, e ≈ 0.7–0.85.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aspect Ratio (AR) = b²/S** is the single most important finite-wing geometric parameter. Higher AR = lower induced drag.
- **Induced drag arises from trailing vortices** shed at the wing tips due to pressure equalization between upper and lower surfaces.
- **Downwash (w)** is the downward induced velocity at the wing; it tilts the local flow by ε = 2C<sub>L</sub>/(πeAR).
- **3-D lift curve slope:** $a = a_0 / [1 + a_0/(\pi e AR)]$ — always **less** than 2-D slope a₀.
- **Total drag polar (most important formula):** $C_D = C_{D,0} + C_L^2/(\pi e AR)$ — parabolic in C<sub>L</sub>.
- **Maximum L/D:** $(L/D)_{\max} = 0.5\sqrt{\pi e AR / C_{D,0}}$ — occurs at $C_L = \sqrt{\pi e AR \cdot C_{D,0}}$.
- **Elliptic lift distribution** gives minimum induced drag (e = 1).
- **Sweep angle Λ** is measured from the **lateral axis** (perpendicular to fuselage), not from longitudinal axis.
- **Sweep delays drag divergence:** $M_{cr,\text{swept}} = M_{cr,0}/\cos\Lambda$ — sweep buys transonic performance.
- **Sweep penalizes induced drag:** Effective AR drops by factor $\cos\Lambda$ in some linearized analyses.
- **Induced drag ∝ 1/V²**, parasite drag ∝ V² — they are equal at $(L/D)_{\max}$ speed.
- **Practical values:** Transport AR ≈ 8–12, Λ ≈ 25°–35°; Fighter AR ≈ 3–5, Λ ≈ 40°–60°; Glider AR > 30, Λ ≈ 0°.
- **Biot-Savart's law** for trailing vortices is the theoretical foundation of Prandtl's lifting-line theory.
- **Always state assumptions** (inviscid, incompressible, attached flow, elliptic distribution) in derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flaps and Control Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flaps and Control Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of aerospace engineering, **Control Surfaces** are hinged or movable aerodynamic devices attached to the trailing edges, leading edges, or body of an aircraft wing, empennage (tail), or fuselage. They are deflected by the pilot (or an autopilot / flight control system) to alter the airflow around the aircraft, thereby generating aerodynamic forces and moments that maneuver the vehicle in pitch, roll, or yaw.

**Flaps** are a specific class of *secondary* control surfaces located on the trailing edge of the wing. Unlike primary control surfaces (which deflect symmetrically to change lift distribution), flaps are deployed in unison on the left and right wings. Their primary function is to **increase the camber and/or the effective chord length of the wing**, thereby dramatically increasing both the lift coefficient ($C_L$) and the drag coefficient ($C_D$) at low speeds — typically during takeoff, initial climb, approach, and landing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between *primary* control surfaces (aileron, elevator, rudder) and *secondary* control surfaces (flaps, slats, spoilers, speedbrakes). The instrumentation focus (this being PEAET863 — Aerospace Instrumentation) demands understanding of how the *position* of these surfaces is *measured* and *actuated*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bird Wing & Hand" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a flat piece of cardboard and moving it slowly through the air. Notice how little "pull" (lift) it generates. Now, bend the trailing edge of the cardboard downward — instantly, the cardboard wants to be pulled upward by a much stronger force, but it also feels a stronger backward push (drag).

**What just happened?**
- The bent trailing edge increased the **curvature (camber)** of the surface.
- The air flowing over the curved path has to travel faster (and at lower pressure), generating more lift.
- The deflected surface also presents a more blunt profile to the airstream, increasing drag.

This is exactly what an aircraft **flap** does. The aircraft is essentially "bending its wing's hand" to grab more air at low speeds so it can land safely without requiring an impossibly long runway. The **ailerons, elevator, and rudder** work on a different principle — they are the *steering wheel* of the aircraft, deflecting in opposite directions on the two wings (ailerons) or up/down (elevator) or left/right (rudder) to *turn and tilt* the aircraft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:**
> A typical commercial transport aircraft (e.g., Boeing 737, Airbus A320) uses flap deflection to nearly **double its maximum lift coefficient** at landing, reducing required approach speed by approximately 25–30%.

### 1.3 Categories of Control Surfaces — A Taxonomy

| Category | Surface | Location | Primary Effect | Symmetric / Differential |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (3-axis control)** | Aileron | Outboard trailing edge of wing | Roll about longitudinal axis | Differential (left vs. right) |
| | Elevator | Trailing edge of horizontal stabilizer | Pitch about lateral axis | Symmetric (both move together) |
| | Rudder | Trailing edge of vertical stabilizer | Yaw about vertical axis | Symmetric |
| **Secondary (High-lift / Drag devices)** | Plain, Split, Slotted, Fowler Flap | Wing trailing edge | Increases $C_{L_{max}}$ and $C_D$ | Symmetric (both wings) |
| | Leading-Edge Slat / Krueger | Wing leading edge | Delays stall, increases $C_{L_{max}}$ | Symmetric |
| | Spoiler / Speedbrake | Upper wing surface | Destroys lift, increases drag | Differential for roll assist |
| | Trim Tabs | Trailing edge of control surfaces | Relieves pilot control force | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerodynamic Force and Moment Convention:**
> Aircraft motions are described about three body axes:
> - **Roll** ($\phi$) — rotation about the longitudinal (nose-to-tail) $x$-axis.
> - **Pitch** ($\theta$) — rotation about the lateral (wingtip-to-wingtip) $y$-axis.
> - **Yaw** ($\psi$) — rotation about the vertical (top-to-bottom) $z$-axis.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Lift Generation and Flap Action

The fundamental lift equation governs all fixed-wing aircraft:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$$

where:
- $L$ = total lift force (N)
- $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³)
- $V$ = true airspeed (m/s)
- $S$ = wing planform area (m²)
- $C_L$ = lift coefficient (dimensionless)

When an aircraft is in the *high-lift configuration* (flaps extended), the **same equation** still applies — but the wing now operates at a much higher $C_L$ at the same angle of attack. This is because the flap modifies the wing's geometry in three concurrent ways:

**Step 1 — Increase of Effective Camber**
The trailing-edge flap pushes the mean camber line of the airfoil rearward and downward. The Kutta–Joukowski theorem tells us that lift is proportional to the circulation $\Gamma$ around the airfoil. A more cambered airfoil produces stronger circulation and therefore a higher $C_L$ for any given angle of attack $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Increase of Effective Chord**
Some flap types (Fowler, double-slotted) physically translate rearward and downward as they deflect. This *increases* the wing's chord length $c$. Because $C_L$ for a flat plate scales approximately as $c$ in 2-D potential flow, more chord = more lift.

**Step 3 — Delay of Trailing-Edge Flow Separation**
A plain flap deflected downward creates a sharp corner at the wing–flap junction, causing **early flow separation** and a *loss* of effectiveness. *Slotted* flaps solve this by opening a gap between the wing and the flap. High-energy air from below the wing is ducted through the slot and re-energizes the slow boundary layer on the flap's upper surface, postponing separation to a much higher angle of attack.

**Step 4 — Penalty: Increased Drag**
Flap deflection does not come for free. The flap is essentially a cambered plate at a high angle of attack relative to the local flow. It generates:
- **Induced drag** proportional to $\Delta C_L^2$ (drag-due-to-lift grows quadratically with the lift increment).
- **Parasitic / form drag** due to the abrupt geometric discontinuity.
- **Wave drag** at transonic Mach numbers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Flap deployment is one of the few maneuvers where an aircraft is *deliberately* made less efficient (higher $C_D$) to achieve safety margins at low speed. A pilot will never use flaps in cruise flight.

### 2.2 The Three Primary Control Surfaces — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Ailerons (Roll Control)
- **Location:** Outboard trailing edge of each wing.
- **Operation:** *Differentially* deflected — the left aileron moves up while the right aileron moves down, and vice versa.
- **Aerodynamic Effect:** The wing with the *down-deflected* aileron generates *more* lift and *more* induced drag; the wing with the *up-deflected* aileron generates *less* lift and *less* drag.
- **Resultant Aircraft Motion:** The aircraft *rolls* toward the wing with less lift. The asymmetry in drag also causes an adverse yaw, which is typically counteracted by the rudder (coordinated turn) or by *aileron–rudder interconnect* systems.

#### 2.2.2 Elevator (Pitch Control)
- **Location:** Trailing edge of the horizontal stabilizer.
- **Operation:** Both halves move *symmetrically* — trailing edge up (nose down) or trailing edge down (nose up).
- **Aerodynamic Effect:** A down-deflected elevator produces a downward force on the tail, which (by Newton's third law) pitches the nose up.
- **Hinge Moment Compensation:** Trim tabs and servo tabs are used to neutralize the aerodynamic hinge moment that opposes the pilot's input force.

#### 2.2.3 Rudder (Yaw Control)
- **Location:** Trailing edge of the vertical stabilizer.
- **Operation:** Symmetrically deflected left or right.
- **Aerodynamic Effect:** A left-deflected rudder produces a side force to the left on the tail, yawing the nose to the *left*.
- **Functions Beyond Turn Coordination:** Used to counter asymmetric thrust (engine-out scenarios), to counteract sideslip during crosswind landings, and to spin recovery.

### 2.3 Classification of Trailing-Edge Flaps

The KTU syllabus emphasizes flap evolution. Below is a logical progression:

| Flap Type | Year of Introduction | Geometry | Slots | $C_{L_{max}}$ Improvement | Typical Aircraft |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plain Flap** | Pre-WWII | Hinged, deflects down only | 0 | Modest (~1.4×) | Early trainers, light aircraft |
| **Split Flap** | 1930s | Hinged, deflects underside only | 0 | Slightly better | DC-3, historical |
| **Slotted Flap** | 1930s | Hinged, with gap (slot) to wing | 1 | ~1.7× | Many WWII fighters |
| **Fowler Flap** | 1930s (Patent) | Translates rearward + deflects | 0–1 | ~2.0× | Modern airliners, jets |
| **Double-Slotted Flap** | 1940s | Two elements, two slots | 2 | ~2.2× | Boeing 727, 737 Classic |
| **Triple-Slotted Flap** | 1950s | Three elements, three slots | 3 | ~2.5× | Boeing 747 Classic, 707 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The progression from Plain → Slotted → Fowler → Multi-slotted represents a *direct* response to the requirement for ever-higher $C_{L_{max}}$ at ever-lower approach speeds for heavier aircraft on fixed runway lengths.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every equation a KTU student must memorize for flap and control surface problems. **Boundary conditions, units, and standard notations are non-negotiable for board exam marks.**

| # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S\,C_L$ | Fundamental lift equation | N |
| 2 | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S\,C_D$ | Fundamental drag equation | N |
| 3 | $C_{L_{max}} \approx C_{L_{clean}} + \Delta C_{L_{flap}}$ | Flap lift increment model | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_D = C_{D_0} + \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi\,e\,AR}$ | Total drag = parasitic + induced | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 W}{\rho S\,C_{L_{max}}}}$ | Stall speed (1g, level flight) | m/s |
| 6 | $V_{approach} \approx 1.3\,V_{stall}$ | Standard approach speed factor | m/s |
| 7 | $\alpha_{stall} \approx \alpha_{0} + k\,\delta_{flap}$ | Flap deflection delays stall angle | degrees / radians |
| 8 | $\tau = I\,\ddot{\theta}$ | Rotational actuator torque (control surface) | N·m |
| 9 | $H = C_{h_\delta}\,\delta + C_{h_q}\,\dfrac{q\,c}{2V}$ | Hinge moment on control surface | N·m |
| 10 | $\delta_{cmd} = K_p\,e + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d\,\dot{e}$ | PID control law (actuator servo) | radians |
| 11 | $T_{induction} = C_\tau\,q\,c^3$ | Induced rolling moment coefficient scaling | N·m |
| 12 | $a = \dfrac{\partial C_L}{\partial \alpha}$ | Lift-curve slope (per radian) | 1/rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical substitution boundary:** Equation (5) shows that *doubling* $C_{L_{max}}$ via flaps reduces stall speed by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Hence approach speed drops by ~30%, and landing distance (which scales as $V^2$) is reduced by *half*.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern commercial aviation, flap systems are at the heart of the **Takeoff And Landing (TOLA) performance envelope**. The Airbus A320, for example, has a *Full Flaps* setting of $35°$ (Configuration Full), giving $C_{L_{max}} \approx 2.5$. In contrast, the clean wing (no flaps) typically has $C_{L_{max}} \approx 1.4$. The instrumentation question — central to PEAET863 — is: **how do we know the flap is actually at the commanded angle?**

This is the bridge to the *instrumentation* part of your syllabus:
- **RVDT / LVDT** (Rotary/Linear Variable Differential Transformer) mounted at the flap track.
- **Synchro / Resolver** units for older aircraft.
- **Hall-effect encoders** in modern fly-by-wire systems.
- **Closed-loop servo actuators** with built-in position feedback.

These sensors send position signals to the **Flight Control Unit (FCU) / Flight Management System (FMS)** and to the cockpit *flap position indicator* gauge, ensuring that what the pilot commands is what the surface actually does.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How Flap Deflection Reduces Stall Speed

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** that examiners love to set. We will derive the relationship between flap-induced $C_{L_{max}}$ increase and the corresponding reduction in stall speed.

**Starting Premise (Given):**
An aircraft of weight $W$ in steady, level, 1g flight must satisfy the lift-equals-weight condition:

$$L = W$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Lift Equation at Stall Speed**
At the instant of stall, the wing is producing its maximum possible lift coefficient. We substitute the lift equation into the equilibrium condition:

$$\frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{stall}^2\,S\,C_{L_{max}} = W$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Stall Speed Algebraically**
Multiply both sides by 2:

$$\rho\,V_{stall}^2\,S\,C_{L_{max}} = 2W$$

Divide by $\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max}}$:

$$V_{stall}^2 = \frac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max}}}$$

Take the square root:

$$V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max}}}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare Clean Configuration vs. Flapped Configuration**
Let us denote the *clean* (flap-up) configuration with subscript "0" and the *flapped* configuration with subscript "f". The aircraft weight and wing area are unchanged; only $C_{L_{max}}$ changes.

$$V_{stall,\,0} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max},0}}}$$

$$V_{stall,\,f} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max},f}}}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio (a Dimensionless Diagnostic)**
Divide the second equation by the first:

$$\frac{V_{stall,\,f}}{V_{stall,\,0}} = \frac{\sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max},f}}}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max},0}}}}$$

The constants $\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S}$ cancel cleanly:

$$\frac{V_{stall,\,f}}{V_{stall,\,0}} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{L_{max},0}}{C_{L_{max},f}}}$$

**Step 5 — Invert for the Reduction in Stall Speed**
The percentage reduction in stall speed due to flap deployment is therefore:

$$\Delta V_{stall}\,\% = \left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{C_{L_{max},0}}{C_{L_{max},f}}}\right) \times 100\,\%$$

This is the **Killer Closed-Form** for this topic.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)**
Suppose an aircraft has $C_{L_{max},0} = 1.4$ in the clean configuration, and a *Fowler flap* deploys to give $C_{L_{max},f} = 2.5$. Find the percentage reduction in stall speed.

**Conversion logic (textual row by row):**

$$\Delta V_{stall}\,\% = \left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{1.4}{2.5}}\right) \times 100\,\%$$

Compute the ratio inside the square root:

$$\frac{1.4}{2.5} = 0.56$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{0.56} \approx 0.7483$$

Subtract from 1:

$$1 - 0.7483 = 0.2517$$

Convert to percentage:

$$\Delta V_{stall}\,\% \approx 25.17\,\%$$

**Result:** The flap deployment reduces stall speed by approximately **25.2%**, which corresponds to a *landing distance reduction of nearly 44%* (since runway distance scales with $V^2$).

### 3.2 Derivation: Flap-Induced Drag Penalty

The lift-induced drag of a wing in the trailing-vortex model is:

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi\,e\,AR}$$

where $e$ is the Oswald efficiency factor and $AR$ is the wing aspect ratio.

When the flap is deployed, $C_L$ at a given angle of attack increases by $\Delta C_L$. The new induced drag is:

$$C_{D_i,\,f} = \frac{(C_L + \Delta C_L)^2}{\pi\,e\,AR}$$

Expand the square:

$$C_{D_i,\,f} = \frac{C_L^2 + 2\,C_L\,\Delta C_L + (\Delta C_L)^2}{\pi\,e\,AR}$$

The **incremental drag** due to flap is:

$$\Delta C_{D_i} = \frac{2\,C_L\,\Delta C_L + (\Delta C_L)^2}{\pi\,e\,AR}$$

**Interpretation:** The flap drag has a *linear* term (in $C_L$) and a *quadratic* term (in $\Delta C_L$). The quadratic term is unavoidable — it exists even when the wing is producing *zero* lift. This is why "dirty" (flap-deployed) aircraft have a higher zero-lift drag than clean aircraft, requiring more thrust just to maintain straight-and-level flight.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hinge Moment and Actuator Sizing

A control surface in flight experiences an *aerodynamic hinge moment* $H$ about its hinge line. For an instrumentation student, the key insight is that this moment must be overcome by the *actuator* (servo motor, hydraulic jack, or electric actuator).

The hinge moment is commonly linearized as:

$$H = q\,S_{cs}\,c_{cs}\,C_{h_\delta}\,\delta + q\,S_{cs}\,c_{cs}\,C_{h_q}\,\frac{\dot{\delta}\,c_{cs}}{2V}$$

In equilibrium, the actuator torque $\tau$ must equal $H$:

$$\tau = H$$

For an electric motor with torque constant $K_t$ and current $i$:

$$\tau = K_t\,i$$

Therefore, the *required* motor current to hold a given deflection is:

$$i = \frac{H}{K_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation Bridge:** This is exactly why **position feedback sensors** (RVDTs, LVDTs, encoders) are mounted at the control surface. Without them, the actuator would *not know* what $\delta$ to command, and the closed-loop PID controller (Equation 10 in the formula sheet) would have no error signal $e$ to act on. *This is the heart of PEAET863 — Aerospace Instrumentation.*

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Flap vs. Slat vs. Spoiler

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important comparative table** for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions on this topic. Memorize the trend, not just the words.

| Parameter | Plain Flap | Slotted Flap | Fowler Flap | Leading-Edge Slat | Spoiler |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Location | Trailing edge | Trailing edge | Trailing edge | Leading edge | Upper wing |
| Increases $C_{L_{max}}$? | Yes (small) | Yes (medium) | Yes (large) | Yes (medium) | No (decreases) |
| Increases Chord? | No | No | Yes | Slightly | No |
| Effect on $C_{D_0}$ | Moderate increase | Moderate increase | Large increase | Small increase | Very large increase |
| Effect on Approach Speed | Reduces | Reduces more | Reduces most | Reduces | Increases |
| Effect on Stall Angle | Decreases | Slightly decreases | Slightly decreases | Increases (delays stall) | N/A |
| Effect on Pitching Moment | Nose-up | Nose-up | Strong nose-up | Slight nose-down | Nose-down |
| Typical Setting Angle | 0–40° | 0–40° | 0–35° | 0–25° | 0–60° |
| Pilot Use | T/O, Ldg | T/O, Ldg | T/O, Ldg | T/O, Ldg | Roll, descent braking |

### 3.5 Quantitative Worked Example (Full KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A Boeing-class transport aircraft has $W = 250{,}000$ N, $S = 124$ m², $C_{L_{max,\,clean}} = 1.4$. With *full flap* deployed, $C_{L_{max,\,flap}} = 2.5$. Sea-level density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate:
> (a) Clean-configuration stall speed.
> (b) Flapped-configuration stall speed.
> (c) Percentage reduction in stall speed.
> (d) New approach speed assuming $V_{app} = 1.3\,V_{stall}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Clean stall speed:**

$$V_{stall,\,0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 250{,}000}{1.225 \times 124 \times 1.4}}$$

Compute denominator step by step: $1.225 \times 124 = 151.9$, then $151.9 \times 1.4 = 212.66$. Compute numerator: $2 \times 250{,}000 = 500{,}000$.

$$V_{stall,\,0} = \sqrt{\frac{500{,}000}{212.66}} = \sqrt{2351.2} \approx 48.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Flapped stall speed:**

$$V_{stall,\,f} = \sqrt{\frac{500{,}000}{1.225 \times 124 \times 2.5}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{500{,}000}{379.75}} = \sqrt{1316.6} \approx 36.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**(c) Percentage reduction:**

$$\Delta V_{stall}\,\% = \frac{48.5 - 36.3}{48.5} \times 100\,\% = \frac{12.2}{48.5} \times 100\,\% \approx 25.2\,\%$$

**(d) New approach speed:**

$$V_{app} = 1.3 \times 36.3 \approx 47.2 \text{ m/s} \quad (\approx 170 \text{ km/h})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final sanity check:** A 25% reduction in stall speed matches our Section 3.1 derivation. The numerical answer is internally consistent.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Flap Actuation & Instrumentation Loop

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete control surface instrumentation feedback loop**, which is the *instrumentation-side* view of flaps (central to PEAET863).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[FLIGHT_DECK_PANEL<br>FLAP_LEVER] --> nodeB[FLIGHT_CONTROL_UNIT<br>FCU_OR_FMS]
    nodeB --> nodeC[ACTUATOR_DRIVER<br>MOTOR_OR_HYDRAULIC_VALVE]
    nodeC --> nodeD[FLAP_TRACK_AND_CARRIAGE<br>MECHANICAL_LINKAGE]
    nodeD --> nodeE[FLAP_SURFACE<br>AERODYNAMIC_DEFLECTION]
    nodeE --> nodeF[FLIGHT_ENVELOPE_RESPONSE<br>LIFT_AND_DRAG_CHANGE]
    nodeF --> nodeG[AIRCRAFT_RESPONSE<br>PITCH_AND_SPEED]
    nodeE --> nodeH[POSITION_SENSOR<br>RVDT_LVDT_ENCODER]
    nodeH --> nodeI[FEEDBACK_SIGNAL_PROCESSOR<br>ADC_AND_FILTER]
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeJ[COCKPIT_INDICATOR<br>FLAP_POSITION_GAUGE]
    nodeF --> nodeK[AIR_DATA_SENSOR<br>ANGLE_OF_ATTACK_PITOT]
    nodeK --> nodeB
```

**Interpretation of nodes (label-only reading):**
- `nodeA` — Pilot's flap lever is the human-machine input.
- `nodeB` — FCU issues the *command* signal based on lever position, air data, and feedback.
- `nodeC` — Actuator driver converts command to mechanical power.
- `nodeD` — Mechanical carriage physically translates/rotates the flap.
- `nodeE` — Flap surface in the airflow, generating aerodynamic effect.
- `nodeF` — Aerodynamic result (lift, drag, pitching moment).
- `nodeG` — Aircraft responds with pitch attitude and airspeed change.
- `nodeH` — Position sensor (RVDT / LVDT / encoder) reads actual flap angle.
- `nodeI` — Signal conditioning (amplification, ADC conversion, filtering).
- `nodeJ` — Cockpit display shows the *measured* flap position.
- `nodeK` — Air data sensors (AoA, dynamic pressure) feed back to close the outer loop.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Control Surface Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SURFACE_FAMILY[CONTROL_SURFACE_FAMILY]
        subgraph PRIMARY_AXES[PRIMARY_3AXIS_SURFACES]
            nodeA1[AILERON<br>ROLL_CONTROL]
            nodeA2[ELEVATOR<br>PITCH_CONTROL]
            nodeA3[RUDDER<br>YAW_CONTROL]
        end
        subgraph SECONDARY_DEVICES[SECONDARY_SURFACES]
            nodeB1[TRAILING_EDGE_FLAPS<br>PLAIN_SLIT_SLOTTED_FOWLER]
            nodeB2[LEADING_EDGE_SLATS<br>AND_KRUEGER]
            nodeB3[SPOILERS_AND_SPEEDBRAKES]
            nodeB4[TRIM_TABS_AND_SERVO_TABS]
        end
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeB3
    nodeA1 -. differential deflection .-> nodeB3
    nodeB1 --> nodeA2
    nodeB1 -. pitch trim change .-> nodeA2
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Flap Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[NEED_HIGHER_CLMAX] --> nodeQ1[STALL_SPEED_TOO_HIGH_FOR_LANDING]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeY1[YES_SIMPLE_TRAINER]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeN1[NO_MEDIUM_PERFORMANCE]
    nodeY1 --> nodeR1[USE_PLAIN_OR_SPLIT_FLAP]
    nodeN1 --> nodeQ2[NEED_LARGE_CLMAX_INCREMENT]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeY2[YES_AIRLINER]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeN2[NO_FIGHTER]
    nodeY2 --> nodeR2[USE_FOWLER_OR_MULTI_SLOTTED_FLAP]
    nodeN2 --> nodeR3[USE_SLOTTED_FLAP_PAIRED_WITH_LEADING_EDGE_SLAT]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Cross-Section of a Fowler Flap

> [!NOTE]
> Physical shape drawings of airfoils are difficult in Mermaid. The following is a *functional geometry diagram* showing the **key regions** of a Fowler flap system in cross-section. Substitute a hand-drawn diagram in your exam copy for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[UPPER_SURFACE_REGION] --> nodeP2[SLOT_GAP_REGION]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[FLAP_UPPER_SURFACE]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[FLAP_LOWER_SURFACE]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[RETRACTED_BAY_REGION]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[LOWER_SURFACE_REGION]
    nodeP2 -. air ducted from below .-> nodeP3
    nodeP3 -. prevents flow separation .-> nodeP4
```

**Key concept (visible in functional blocks):**
- The slot gap allows **high-energy air** from the *lower surface* to bleed through to the *upper surface* of the flap.
- This re-energizes the boundary layer, delaying separation and allowing the flap to operate at high deflection angles without stalling.
- The retracted bay region shows where the flap stows during cruise flight to minimize parasitic drag.
