## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. Differentiate between primary and secondary control surfaces. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
- **Primary control surfaces** are the hinged aerodynamic devices used by the pilot to maneuver the aircraft about its three body axes (roll, pitch, yaw). They are *asymmetrically* or *symmetrically* deflected to produce *control moments*. Examples: **aileron** (roll), **elevator** (pitch), **rudder** (yaw).
- **Secondary control surfaces** are used not to maneuver the aircraft about its axes, but to *augment lift* or *increase drag* for specific flight phases (takeoff, landing, descent). They are usually *symmetrically* deflected on both wings. Examples: **flaps** (trailing edge), **slats** (leading edge), **spoilers**.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. Why are slotted flaps preferred over plain flaps for high-performance aircraft?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
A plain flap, when deflected, creates a sharp corner at the wing–flap junction, which causes **early flow separation** on the upper surface. This limits the maximum usable flap deflection angle and hence the achievable $C_{L_{max}}$. A **slotted flap** incorporates a *gap* (slot) between the wing and the flap, allowing high-energy air from the lower surface to bleed through and re-energize the boundary layer on the flap's upper surface. This delays flow separation to a much higher angle of attack, allowing greater deflection angles and a higher $C_{L_{max}}$ (~1.7× clean vs. ~1.4× for plain flap).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1, Q2 — Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, classify the different types of trailing-edge flaps used in subsonic aircraft. Compare their $C_{L_{max}}$ improvement and explain why multi-slotted flaps give the highest lift augmentation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aircraft weighing $W = 60{,}000$ N has a wing area $S = 20$ m². The maximum lift coefficient in the clean configuration is $C_{L_{max}} = 1.2$ and with full flap deployment it is $C_{L_{max}} = 2.0$. If the aircraft is operating at sea level ($\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³), determine:
1. The clean-configuration stall speed.
2. The flapped-configuration stall speed.
3. The percentage reduction in stall speed.
4. The approach speed in the flapped configuration, assuming $V_{app} = 1.3\,V_{stall}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the function of **ailerons**, **elevators**, and **rudder** as the three primary control surfaces. For each, state the axis of rotation it controls, the direction of deflection, and the resulting aircraft motion.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A transport aircraft is in straight-and-level flight at $V = 80$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $S = 100$ m², and is in the clean configuration with $C_L = 0.4$. Calculate the lift force being generated. If the pilot deploys flaps, the new $C_L$ becomes $1.2$ at the same speed, determine the new lift force and the lift increment. Comment on the resulting effect on induced drag using the relation $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / (\pi\,e\,AR)$ with $e = 0.85$ and $AR = 8$.

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Flap Classification and Lift Augmentation (7 Marks)

**Flap Types (in order of increasing $C_{L_{max}}$):**

**1. Plain Flap [1 mark]**
A simple hinged trailing-edge device. Deflects downward, increasing camber. Gives $\Delta C_{L_{max}} \approx 0.4$. Found on light trainers.

**2. Split Flap [1 mark]**
A variant where only the lower surface of the trailing edge deflects, while the upper surface remains fixed. Higher drag than a plain flap for the same lift increment. Found on DC-3-era aircraft.

**3. Slotted Flap [1 mark]**
A flap with a slot (gap) between the wing and the flap, allowing high-energy air to bleed through and re-energize the upper-surface boundary layer. Achieves $C_{L_{max}} \approx 1.7$–$1.9$ times the clean value.

**4. Fowler Flap [1 mark]**
Translates rearward (increasing chord) *and* deflects. Chord increase is a major contributor to $C_{L_{max}}$ improvement. Found on most modern airliners.

**5. Double / Triple-Slotted Flap [1.5 marks]**
Two or three flap elements, two or three slots. Maximum $C_{L_{max}}$ achievable in subsonic flight. Used on large transport aircraft (Boeing 747, Airbus A340).

**Why multi-slotted flaps give the highest $C_{L_{max}}$ [1.5 marks]:**
Multiple slots progressively re-energize the boundary layer across the entire chord. The flow remains attached even at very high deflection angles (30°–40°). Additionally, the multi-element system creates a series of *cascading airfoils* — each one accelerating the flow over the next, effectively multiplying circulation. The penalty is mechanical complexity, weight, and significantly higher parasitic drag.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $W = 60{,}000$ N, $S = 20$ m², $C_{L_{max},0} = 1.2$, $C_{L_{max},f} = 2.0$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**(1) Clean stall speed [1.5 marks]:**

$$V_{stall,\,0} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L_{max},0}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 60{,}000}{1.225 \times 20 \times 1.2}}$$

Compute denominator: $1.225 \times 20 = 24.5$; $24.5 \times 1.2 = 29.4$. Numerator: $2 \times 60{,}000 = 120{,}000$.

$$V_{stall,\,0} = \sqrt{\frac{120{,}000}{29.4}} = \sqrt{4081.6} \approx 63.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**(2) Flapped stall speed [1.5 marks]:**

$$V_{stall,\,f} = \sqrt{\frac{120{,}000}{1.225 \times 20 \times 2.0}} = \sqrt{\frac{120{,}000}{49.0}} = \sqrt{2448.98} \approx 49.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**(3) Percentage reduction [2 marks]:**

$$\Delta V_{stall}\,\% = \frac{63.9 - 49.5}{63.9} \times 100\,\% = \frac{14.4}{63.9} \times 100\,\% \approx 22.5\,\%$$

Cross-check using closed form: $\sqrt{1.2/2.0} = \sqrt{0.6} = 0.7746$, so reduction $= 1 - 0.7746 = 0.2254 = 22.54\%$. ✓

**(4) Approach speed [2 marks]:**

$$V_{app} = 1.3 \times 49.5 = 64.4 \text{ m/s} \quad (\approx 232 \text{ km/h})$$

### 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Primary Control Surfaces (7 Marks)

**Aileron [2.5 marks]:** Located on the *outboard* trailing edge of each wing. Operated *differentially*: the left aileron moves up while the right moves down, and vice versa. The down-deflected aileron creates *more* lift on its wing; the up-deflected aileron creates *less* lift on the other wing. The asymmetry in lift causes the aircraft to **roll** about its longitudinal ($x$) axis. The drag asymmetry also produces a *parasitic yaw* that is corrected with the rudder.

**Elevator [2.5 marks]:** Located on the trailing edge of the *horizontal stabilizer*. Operated *symmetrically*: both halves move together. A trailing-edge-down deflection produces a downward aerodynamic force on the tail, which (by Newton's third law) pitches the **nose up** about the lateral ($y$) axis. A trailing-edge-up deflection produces nose-down pitch.

**Rudder [2 marks]:** Located on the trailing edge of the *vertical stabilizer (fin)*. Operated *symmetrically*. A left-deflected rudder produces a side force to the left on the tail, yawing the **nose left** about the vertical ($z$) axis. Used in coordinated turns, crosswind landings, and engine-out scenarios.

#### Part (b) — Lift and Induced Drag Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V = 80$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $S = 100$ m².

**Clean configuration lift [2 marks]:**

$$L_0 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 80^2 \times 100 \times 0.4$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 6400 \times 100 \times 0.4$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6400 \times 40$$

$$= 156{,}800 \text{ N}$$

**Flapped configuration lift [2 marks]:**

$$L_f = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 80^2 \times 100 \times 1.2$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6400 \times 100 \times 1.2$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6400 \times 120$$

$$= 470{,}400 \text{ N}$$

**Lift increment [1 mark]:**

$$\Delta L = 470{,}400 - 156{,}800 = 313{,}600 \text{ N}$$

**Induced drag analysis [2 marks]:**

Clean: $C_{D_i,\,0} = \dfrac{0.4^2}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 8} = \dfrac{0.16}{21.36} \approx 0.00749$

Flapped: $C_{D_i,\,f} = \dfrac{1.2^2}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 8} = \dfrac{1.44}{21.36} \approx 0.0674$

**Comment:** Induced drag increases by a factor of $9$ (since drag scales as $C_L^2$, and $1.2/0.4 = 3$, so $3^2 = 9$). This is the unavoidable **lift-induced drag penalty** of flap deployment.

---

### 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (and where KTU examiners dock marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to square-root the ratio in $V_{stall}$:** $V_{stall} \propto 1/\sqrt{C_{L_{max}}}$, not $1/C_{L_{max}}$. Students who write $V_{stall} \propto 1/C_{L_{max}}$ lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_{L_{max}}$ with $C_L$:** The maximum lift coefficient is a *property of the airfoil* at a given configuration, not the operating lift coefficient. Do not confuse them in numerical problems.
> 3. **Confusing aileron deflection with flap deflection:** Ailerons are *differential* (left vs. right opposite); flaps are *symmetric* (both wings same). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Always quote $V_{stall}$ in m/s (or convert to km/h or knots and state both).
> 5. **No sketch in 7-mark descriptive questions:** A 7-mark question without a labeled diagram is penalized up to 2 marks. Always include a *neat* labeled sketch.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of instrumentation:** This is PEAET863 (Aerospace Instrumentation). Whenever discussing flap / control surface operation, mention the **position sensor (RVDT/LVDT)** and the **closed-loop actuator** for full marks.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Box Before the Exam):**

- ☐ **Control surfaces** are hinged aerodynamic devices that deflect to produce forces and moments for aircraft control.
- ☐ **Primary (3-axis) surfaces:** Aileron (roll), Elevator (pitch), Rudder (yaw).
- ☐ **Secondary surfaces:** Flaps, slats, spoilers, trim tabs.
- ☐ **Flap function:** Increase $C_{L_{max}}$ and $C_D$ at low speed; symmetric deflection on both wings.
- ☐ **Slats** are on the *leading* edge; **flaps** are on the *trailing* edge.
- ☐ **Flap types in increasing $C_{L_{max}}$ order:** Plain → Split → Slotted → Fowler → Double-Slotted → Triple-Slotted.
- ☐ **Fowler flap** is special because it *translates rearward*, increasing the wing chord.
- ☐ **Slot** re-energizes the boundary layer, delaying stall.
- ☐ **Fundamental lift equation:** $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ — must be memorized.
- ☐ **Stall speed formula:** $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_{L_{max}})}$ — must be memorized.
- ☐ **Doubling $C_{L_{max}}$ reduces $V_{stall}$ by factor $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (~30%).**
- ☐ **Approach speed** is typically $V_{app} = 1.3\,V_{stall}$ — must be memorized.
- ☐ **Induced drag** scales as $C_L^2$ — flap deployment greatly *increases* induced drag.
- ☐ **Ailerons** are *differentially* deflected; flaps are *symmetrically* deflected.
- ☐ **Adverse yaw** is the unwanted yawing moment produced by differential aileron drag, corrected by the rudder.
- ☐ **Trim tabs** reduce pilot control forces; **servo tabs** assist actuator movement.
- ☐ **Instrumentation focus (PEAET863):** RVDT / LVDT / encoder → signal conditioner → FCU → actuator.
- ☐ **Closed-loop control law** for actuator servo: $\delta_{cmd} = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ (PID).
- ☐ **Hinge moment** must be overcome by actuator torque; sizing the actuator requires $H = q S_{cs} c_{cs} C_h$.
- ☐ **Lower bound**: $C_{L_{max}}$ of a clean subsonic wing is typically $1.2$–$1.5$; with Fowler flaps, $2.0$–$2.8$.
- ☐ **Units always:** Lift in N, weight in N, speed in m/s, density in kg/m³, area in m².
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Aircraft Performance Parameters - Thrust and Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Aircraft Performance Parameters - Thrust and Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Aircraft Performance Parameters

Aircraft performance is fundamentally governed by the **interaction between thrust (or power) produced by the propulsion system and the aerodynamic forces (lift, drag) acting on the airframe**. The two cornerstone parameters in propulsion-driven performance analysis are **Thrust ($T$)** and **Power ($P$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Thrust* is the forward-acting propulsive force generated by the engine-propeller system, measured in **Newtons (N)**. *Power* is the rate at which the engine does useful work to overcome drag and accelerate the aircraft, measured in **Watts (W)** or **Horsepower (HP)**, where **1 HP ≈ 745.7 W**.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Thrust

By the **principle of momentum conservation** applied to a control volume around the engine, thrust is mathematically expressed as:

$$
T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a)A_e
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}$ = **mass flow rate** of air (kg/s)
- $V_e$ = **exit velocity** of the propulsive jet (m/s)
- $V_0$ = **free-stream / flight velocity** (m/s)
- $P_e$ = **exit static pressure** (N/m²)
- $P_a$ = **ambient static pressure** (N/m²)
- $A_e$ = **exit area** of the nozzle (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $\dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$ is called the **momentum thrust**, while $(P_e - P_a)A_e$ is the **pressure thrust**. Together they constitute the **gross thrust** of the engine.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Power

The **useful propulsive power** delivered to the aircraft (also called **Thrust Power**, $P_T$) is the product of thrust and forward velocity:

$$
P_T = T \cdot V_0
$$

For propeller-driven aircraft, we distinguish between:
- **Shaft Power (SHP):** Power delivered by the engine to the propeller shaft.
- **Thrust Power (THP):** Useful power converted into forward motion.
- **Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_p$):** Ratio of thrust power to shaft power.

$$
\eta_p = \frac{P_T}{\text{SHP}} = \frac{T \cdot V_0}{\text{SHP}}
$$

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Rowing Boat & The Engine":**
> Imagine you are rowing a boat. Your **arms provide thrust** by pushing water backward (Newton's 3rd Law). The **rate at which you do useful work** — pulling the oar through the water to move the boat forward — is your **power**.
> 
> - If you push the water slowly but with a huge force, you generate high thrust at low speed (a *bulldozer* of the sky — cargo aircraft at takeoff).
> - If you push a small amount of water very fast, you get high speed but lower thrust (a *racing boat* — fighter jet at supersonic cruise).
> 
> The **engine is your arms**, the **propulsive jet is the displaced water**, and the **aircraft is the boat**. Just as you cannot have both *huge force AND huge speed* in rowing, an engine cannot simultaneously deliver *high thrust at high velocity* — this trade-off is captured by **propulsive efficiency**.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Performance Indices

| Index | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value (Civil) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thrust-to-Weight Ratio | $T/W$ | $T/W = T/(mg)$ | Jet: 0.3–0.4, Fighter: >1.0 |
| Power-to-Weight Ratio | $P/W$ | $P/W = P/(mg)$ | Piston: 0.05–0.1, Turbofan: 0.3–0.5 |
| Specific Fuel Consumption (TSFC) | $c_T$ | $c_T = \dot{m}_f / T$ | Turbofan: ~15–25 mg/(N·s) |
| Specific Fuel Consumption (PSFC) | $c_P$ | $c_P = \dot{m}_f / P$ | Piston: ~0.4 lb/(hp·hr) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio ($T/W$)** dictates *acceleration and climb capability*, while the **Power-to-Weight Ratio ($P/W$)** dictates *climb rate and ceiling performance*. Both must be analyzed together for a holistic performance picture — a key KTU examination point.

---

## 1.6 Why These Parameters Matter in Aerospace Instrumentation

The **Aerospace Instrumentation** course (PEAET863) treats thrust and power as **measured quantities** that must be acquired via:
- **Thrust stands** (static ground testing)
- **In-flight thrust estimation** using $T = D$ in unaccelerated level flight.
- **Engine monitoring sensors** (shaft torquemeters, RPM, EGT, fuel flow meters).
- **Power computation** from $P = 2\pi N \tau$ where $N$ is rotational speed and $\tau$ is torque.

> [!NOTE]
> Instrumentation bridges the gap between *theoretical performance equations* and *real-world flyable aircraft*. Every parameter we derive must ultimately be **measurable** in the cockpit or test cell.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Thrust Generation

Thrust generation is rooted in the **control-volume application of Newton's 2nd & 3rd Laws** to the engine. The engine acts as a device that:

1. **Accelerates a mass of air** ($\dot{m}$) from freestream velocity $V_0$ to exit velocity $V_e$.
2. **Increases the pressure** of the exhaust gas (in jet engines) above ambient.
3. **Expels this high-energy fluid rearward**, producing an equal-and-opposite forward reaction force on the aircraft.

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Air Intake:** The engine ingests air at freestream conditions ($V_0, P_a, T_a$).
- **Step 2 — Energy Addition:** Fuel is burned, raising the total energy (thermal + kinetic) of the working fluid.
- **Step 3 — Exhaust Acceleration:** The hot, high-pressure gas is expanded through a nozzle, accelerating to $V_e > V_0$.
- **Step 4 — Momentum Exchange:** By Newton's 3rd Law, the rearward momentum of the exhaust produces a **forward thrust** on the airframe.

> [!TIP]
> **Why $V_e > V_0$ is essential:** If $V_e = V_0$, there is no velocity change, hence no momentum change, hence **no thrust**. Thrust is fundamentally a *velocity-difference* phenomenon.

---

## 2.2 Thrust in Different Engine Classes

The formula for thrust **varies in form** depending on the engine type, but the underlying principle is conserved:

### (a) Turbojet / Turbofan Engine
$$
T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a)A_e
$$

### (b) Propeller-Driven Engine (using Propeller Disk Theory)
$$
T = \dot{m}_p (V_2 - V_1)
$$

Where $V_1$ = velocity far upstream of disk, $V_2$ = velocity far downstream, and by actuator-disk theory:
$$
V_2 - V_1 = 2(V_1 - V_i) \quad \text{and} \quad V_1 + V_2 = 2 V_d
$$

Here, $V_i$ is the **induced velocity** at the disk and $V_d$ is the **disk velocity**.

### (c) Rocket Engine
$$
T = \dot{m} V_e + (P_e - P_a)A_e
$$

(For rockets, freestream velocity is negligible, so $V_0 \approx 0$.)

---

## 2.3 The Thrust–Power Relationship

The **propulsive power** delivered to the aircraft is:
$$
P_T = T \cdot V_0
$$

For a jet engine, the **rate of change of kinetic energy** of the propulsive jet is the **rate of kinetic energy addition**:
$$
P_{KE} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e^2 - V_0^2)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propulsive Efficiency** captures the fraction of jet kinetic energy that is converted into useful aircraft motion:
> $$
> \eta_p = \frac{P_T}{P_{KE}} = \frac{2}{1 + (V_e/V_0)}
> $$
> 
> **Maximum propulsive efficiency** occurs when $V_e = V_0$, giving $\eta_p = 1$. In practice, this is impossible (it would require infinite fuel), so real turbofans operate with $V_e \approx V_0$ to maximize efficiency (the principle behind the high-bypass turbofan).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a)A_e$ | General thrust equation (jet) | N |
| 2 | $P_T = T \cdot V_0$ | Thrust power (useful propulsive power) | W |
| 3 | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1 + (V_e/V_0)}$ | Propulsive efficiency (jet) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2(V/V_i)}{1 + (V/V_i)^2}$ | Propulsive efficiency (propeller) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{\text{shaft}} = 2\pi N \tau$ | Shaft power from torque & RPM | W |
| 6 | $\text{SHP} = \dfrac{P_T}{\eta_p}$ | Shaft horsepower (propeller) | W or HP |
| 7 | $\text{ESHP} = \text{SHP} \cdot \eta_p$ | Equivalent shaft horsepower | W or HP |
| 8 | $T/W = T/(mg)$ | Thrust-to-weight ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P/W = P/(mg)$ | Power-to-weight ratio | W/N |
| 10 | $c_T = \dot{m}_f / T$ | Thrust-specific fuel consumption | kg/(N·s) or mg/(N·s) |
| 11 | $\text{R/C} = (T - D)V/W$ | Rate of climb | m/s |
| 12 | $P_s = (T - D)V/W$ | Specific excess power | m/s |
| 13 | $V_{\max} : T = D$ | Maximum speed condition | — |
| 14 | $V_{\text{ROC,max}} : T - D = (P_a - P_r)/V$ | Max rate of climb (jet) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $V_e = V_0 \implies \eta_p = 1$ (theoretical maximum, impractical).
> - $T = D \implies V = V_{\max}$ (level unaccelerated flight).
> - $T - D = 0$ at $V_{\max}$: **no excess thrust, no climb capability.**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern aircraft certification and instrumentation, these parameters are used to:

1. **Define Engine Certification Standards:** FAR Part 33 / EASA CS-E require demonstration of rated thrust, TSFC, and engine life.
2. **Generate Cockpit Display Logic:** $T/W$ and $P/W$ are not displayed directly, but their effects (climb rate, acceleration) are indicated via air data computers.
3. **Compute Specific Range:** $V/C_T$ (nautical miles per kg of fuel) — used in flight-planning FMS.
4. **Enable In-Flight Performance Modeling:** Real-time estimation of $T$ from $D$ (drag) using aerodynamic databases.
5. **Support Engine Health Monitoring (EHM):** Sensors measure $N$ (RPM), $\tau$ (torque), $T_{t4}$ (turbine inlet temp), $P_{t7}$ (exhaust pressure) to compute derived $T$ and $P$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** In exams, always state the **units** explicitly. A 14-mark question on thrust-power relationships typically expects students to derive $\eta_p$ and apply it to a numerical problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Thrust Equation

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume
Consider a control volume enclosing the engine from the freestream inlet to the exhaust exit plane. Apply the **steady-flow momentum equation** (Reynolds Transport Theorem):

$$
\sum F_x = \dot{m}_e V_e - \dot{m}_0 V_0
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Forces
The net force in the $x$-direction on the control volume contents is:
- Pressure force at exit: $+P_e A_e$ (pushing forward on the gas).
- Pressure force at inlet: $-P_0 A_0$ (pushing backward on the gas).
- Body forces (neglect gravity for short engine).

If the inlet plane is at ambient pressure ($P_0 = P_a$) and the inlet area is small, this term reduces to:
$$
\sum F_x = P_e A_e - P_a A_0 \approx P_e A_e - P_a A_e
$$

Wait — to maintain consistency in sign convention for thrust produced *by* the engine *on* the aircraft, the **reaction force on the engine** is:
$$
T = (P_e - P_a)A_e + \dot{m}(V_e - V_0)
$$

### Step 3 — State the Final Result
$$
\boxed{\,T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) + (P_e - P_a)A_e\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact** thrust equation. For a **perfectly expanded nozzle** ($P_e = P_a$), the pressure term vanishes and $T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Propulsive Efficiency (Jet Engine)

### Step 1 — Define Thrust Power
The useful propulsive power imparted to the aircraft equals thrust times flight velocity:
$$
P_T = T \cdot V_0
$$

Substituting the thrust expression (assuming perfectly expanded nozzle, $P_e = P_a$):
$$
P_T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) \cdot V_0
$$

### Step 2 — Define Jet Kinetic Power
The rate of kinetic energy carried away by the jet (relative to the aircraft) is:
$$
P_{KE} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e^2 - V_0^2)
$$

Using the algebraic identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$:
$$
P_{KE} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e - V_0)(V_e + V_0)
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Efficiency Ratio
$$
\eta_p = \frac{P_T}{P_{KE}} = \frac{\dot{m}(V_e - V_0)V_0}{\frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e - V_0)(V_e + V_0)}
$$

Cancel common terms $\dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$:
$$
\eta_p = \frac{2V_0}{V_e + V_0}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Dimensionless Form
Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_0$:
$$
\boxed{\,\eta_p = \frac{2}{1 + (V_e/V_0)}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Analyze the Limiting Cases
- If $V_e = V_0$ (ideal expansion matched to flight speed): $\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1+1} = 1$ (100% — the theoretical upper limit).
- If $V_e \gg V_0$ (rocket at low speed, $V_0 \to 0$): $\eta_p \to 0$ (most energy wasted as jet KE).
- If $V_e \to 0$ (no exhaust velocity): $\eta_p = 2$ — but this is **physically impossible** as it would imply negative energy flux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optimum $V_e$ for any given flight speed is **just slightly greater than $V_0$** — this is the design philosophy behind **high-bypass turbofans** and **variable-geometry exhaust nozzles**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Specific Excess Power and Rate of Climb

### Step 1 — Force Balance in Climb
For an aircraft climbing at steady speed and constant mass:
$$
T - D - W\sin\gamma = 0
$$

where $\gamma$ is the climb angle. For small climb angles ($\sin\gamma \approx \gamma \approx \text{R/C}/V$):
$$
T - D = W \sin\gamma \approx W \cdot \frac{\text{R/C}}{V}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Rate of Climb
Multiplying both sides by $V/W$:
$$
\frac{(T - D)V}{W} = \text{R/C}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify Specific Excess Power
$$
\boxed{\,P_s = \frac{(T - D)V}{W} = \text{R/C}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Specific Excess Power ($P_s$) has units of **m/s** — it represents how many meters per second the aircraft can climb per unit of available excess power. It is a fundamental **energy maneuverability** metric for fighter aircraft.

### Step 4 — Power Form of Rate of Climb
Since $T \cdot V = P_a$ (power available) and $D \cdot V = P_r$ (power required):
$$
\text{R/C} = \frac{P_a - P_r}{W}
$$

This is the **power form** of the rate-of-climb equation, and is the form most commonly tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of TSFC and Fuel-Flow Relation

### Step 1 — Definition
Thrust-Specific Fuel Consumption is defined as fuel flow rate per unit thrust:
$$
c_T = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{T} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{N·s}}\right]
$$

### Step 2 — Total Fuel Consumption for a Mission Segment
For a flight of duration $\Delta t$ at constant thrust $T$:
$$
W_f = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta t = c_T \cdot T \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 3 — Endurance for a Jet
For a jet, endurance at constant altitude and speed (using Breguet-like reasoning):
$$
E = \frac{1}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L/D}{g} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compute endurance or range, students often confuse the jet form with the propeller form. The propeller form uses $\eta_p$ and $c_P$ instead of $c_T$ — be sure to identify the engine type first.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Propulsive Efficiency of a Turbofan

**Problem:** A turbofan engine produces thrust at $V_0 = 250$ m/s flight speed, with an effective exhaust velocity $V_e = 280$ m/s. The mass flow rate is 500 kg/s. Compute:
(i) Thrust, (ii) Thrust power, (iii) Jet kinetic power, (iv) Propulsive efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(i) Thrust** (assuming perfectly expanded nozzle, $P_e = P_a$):
$$
T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0) = 500 \times (280 - 250)
$$
$$
T = 500 \times 30 = 15{,}000 \;\text{N} = 15 \;\text{kN}
$$

**(ii) Thrust Power:**
$$
P_T = T \cdot V_0 = 15{,}000 \times 250 = 3{,}750{,}000 \;\text{W} = 3.75 \;\text{MW}
$$

**(iii) Jet Kinetic Power:**
$$
P_{KE} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_e^2 - V_0^2) = \frac{1}{2}(500)(280^2 - 250^2)
$$
$$
P_{KE} = 250 \times (78{,}400 - 62{,}500) = 250 \times 15{,}900 = 3{,}975{,}000 \;\text{W} = 3.975 \;\text{MW}
$$

**(iv) Propulsive Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_p = \frac{P_T}{P_{KE}} = \frac{3.75}{3.975} = 0.9434 = 94.34\%
$$

**Alternative direct formula check:**
$$
\eta_p = \frac{2}{1 + (V_e/V_0)} = \frac{2}{1 + (280/250)} = \frac{2}{1 + 1.12} = \frac{2}{2.12} = 0.9434 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that $V_e/V_0 = 1.12$ — close to 1 — gives a *high* propulsive efficiency (~94%). This validates the design of high-bypass turbofans used on modern airliners.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: At-A-Glance Comparison

| Parameter | Jet Engine | Propeller Engine | Rocket |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thrust equation | $\dot{m}(V_e-V_0)$ | $\dot{m}_p(V_2-V_1)$ | $\dot{m}V_e$ |
| Power type | Thrust power $TV_0$ | Shaft power $2\pi N\tau$ | Jet KE power |
| Efficiency | $\eta_p = 2/(1+V_e/V_0)$ | $\eta_p = 2V_i V_0 / (V_0^2+V_i^2)$ | $2V_0^2/V_e^2$ (low!) |
| TSFC typical | 15–25 mg/(N·s) | 0.4–0.6 lb/(hp·hr) PSFC | 10–35 mg/(N·s) |
| Best for | High subsonic cruise | Low-speed / STOL | Space / supersonic |
| $T/W$ typical | 0.3–1.0+ | 0.2–0.4 | 0.5–5.0 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thrust & Power Measurement System

This diagram maps the **instrumentation chain** that acquires, processes, and displays thrust and power data — the central theme of PEAET863 Aerospace Instrumentation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Eng1: Engine Core]:::engine
    B[S1: Fuel Flow Sensor]:::sensor

    classDef engine fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Sensors (S1–S5)** acquire raw engine variables (fuel flow, RPM, torque, pressures, temperatures).
> - **Computers (G, H, I)** apply the formulas from Section 3 (e.g., $P = 2\pi N\tau$ to compute shaft power; $T = D$ at constant altitude/speed to infer thrust).
> - **Displays (D1–D3)** present the information to the pilot/crew.
> - **Storage (L, M)** logs data for post-flight analysis — essential for **Engine Health Monitoring (EHM)**.

## 4.2 Schematic: Thrust-Power Relationship in Level Flight

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
