### 4.3 Figure 3 — Qualitative Plot Concept: Drag Polar and Optimum Speeds

The following graph illustrates the *qualitative* relationship between the drag polar, lift coefficient, and the optimum operating points for range and endurance. (Adapted as a Mermaid block — quantitative plot is in the standard textbook.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CL axis: from 0 to 1.0] --> B[Drag Polar Curve CD vs CL]
    B --> C1[Optimum Range CL equals sqrt CD0 over K]
    B --> C2[Optimum Endurance CL equals sqrt 3 CD0 over K]
    C1 --> D1[Highest L over D point on curve]
    C2 --> D2[Lower L over D but lower drag]
    D1 --> E1[Faster ground speed]
    D2 --> E2[Slower ground speed more time aloft]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Key**
> - The **X-axis** is $C_L$ and the **Y-axis** (conceptually) is $C_D$.
> - The drag polar is a **parabola** opening upward: $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$.
> - The **L/D maximum** corresponds to drawing a line from the *origin* tangent to the polar.
> - The **optimum-endurance point** lies to the *left* of the optimum-range point on the polar — meaning a *lower* $C_L$ and a *higher* true airspeed $V$.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> *Differentiate between Range and Endurance of an aircraft. Why does a turbojet range depend on velocity but turbojet endurance does not?*

**Model Answer:**

**Range** is the total horizontal distance flown on a given fuel load (units: km or nmi), whereas **Endurance** is the total time of flight on the same fuel load (units: h).

In a turbojet, range $R = (V/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$ — velocity appears in the numerator because flying faster increases ground-track covered per unit time, even though fuel burn also rises. Endurance $E = (1/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_i/W_f)$ — the velocity $V$ mathematically cancels out because a faster jet burns proportionally more fuel per second, exactly compensating the higher ground speed. Hence endurance is *velocity-independent* for jets.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for the velocity-dependence explanation with the boxed equations.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
> *What is meant by "lift-to-drag ratio" and why is maximizing it important for both range and endurance?*

**Model Answer:**

The **lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)** is the dimensionless ratio of the lift force $L$ produced by the wing to the aerodynamic drag $D$ experienced by the whole aircraft, i.e., $L/D = C_L / C_D$.

It is a direct multiplier in **all four Breguet equations** (jet range, jet endurance, prop range, prop endurance). For a parabolic drag polar $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$, L/D is maximum at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$, with maximum value $(L/D)_{max} = 1 / (2\sqrt{C_{D0} K})$. Therefore, designing wings with **high aspect ratio** and **low zero-lift drag** $C_{D0}$ (laminar-flow airfoils, smooth fuselage) is the central aerodynamic objective of long-range aircraft.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for showing it appears in Breguet equations, 1 mark for the design implication.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** *Derive the Breguet range equation for a turbojet aircraft cruising at constant altitude and constant true airspeed. State clearly all assumptions used. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *A small turbofan aircraft has the following cruise data:* $V = 220$ m/s, $c_T = 0.65$/h, $L/D = 16$, $W_i = 60000$ N, $W_f = 50000$ N. *Compute the range in km and the endurance in hours.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. *Assumptions:* (2 marks)
   - Quasi-steady, unaccelerated level flight → $L = W$ and $T = D$.
   - Constant altitude, constant true airspeed $V$.
   - $C_L$ and $C_D$ (and thus $L/D$) approximately constant.
   - $c_T$ constant during cruise.
   - Weight changes only by fuel burn.

2. *Derivation:* (5 marks)
   Start with $R = \int V \, dt$. Use $dt = dW/(-c_T T) = -dW/(c_T D)$. Substituting $T = D$:

$$
R = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \frac{V}{c_T \, D} \, dW
$$

Using $D = W \cdot C_D / C_L$:

$$
R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \frac{dW}{W} = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1: Convert $c_T$ to per-second: $c_T = 0.65 / 3600 = 1.8056 \times 10^{-4}$ /s.

Step 2: Range:

$$
R = \frac{220}{1.8056 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot 16 \cdot \ln(60000/50000)
$$

$$
= 1218421 \cdot 16 \cdot \ln(1.2)
$$

$$
= 1218421 \cdot 16 \cdot 0.18232
$$

$$
= 3554 \text{ km}
$$

Step 3: Endurance:

$$
E = \frac{1}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right) = \frac{16}{1.8056 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot 0.18232
$$

$$
= 88608 \cdot 0.18232 = 16156 \text{ s}
$$

Step 4: Convert to hours: $E = 16156 / 3600 = 4.488$ h ≈ **4 h 29 min**.

**Final boxed answers:** $R = 3554$ km, $E = 4.49$ h.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** *Derive the Breguet range and endurance equations for a propeller-driven aircraft, and explain the role of propulsive efficiency $\eta_p$.* (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** *A turboprop aircraft cruises at constant $V = 180$ m/s and constant altitude. Given:* $\eta_p = 0.82$, $c = 0.4$/h, $L/D = 14$, $W_i = 45000$ N, $W_f = 38000$ N. *Calculate the range in km and the endurance in hours.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. *Fuel flow model for propeller:* (2 marks)

$$
\dot{m}_f = c \cdot P_{shaft} = c \cdot \frac{T V}{\eta_p}
$$

2. *Range derivation:* (2.5 marks)
   $R = \int V \, dt = \int V \cdot \dfrac{\eta_p}{c T V} \, (-dW) = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c} \int \dfrac{dW}{T} = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln(W_i/W_f)$

3. *Endurance derivation:* (1.5 marks)

$$
E = \int dt = \int \frac{\eta_p}{c T V} \, (-dW) = \frac{\eta_p}{c V} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)
$$

4. *Role of $\eta_p$:* (1 mark) — $\eta_p$ is the propeller's ability to convert shaft power into useful thrust. Higher $\eta_p$ → less fuel burned per unit thrust → more range/endurance. Typical values: 0.80–0.85 for modern turboprops, much lower for piston engines at low speeds.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Convert $c = 0.4 / 3600 = 1.1111 \times 10^{-4}$ /s.

Step 1: Range:

$$
R = \frac{0.82}{1.1111 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot 14 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{45000}{38000}\right)
$$

$$
= 7380 \cdot 14 \cdot \ln(1.1842)
$$

$$
= 7380 \cdot 14 \cdot 0.16910
$$

$$
= 17466 \text{ m} \approx 17.47 \text{ km}
$$

Step 2: Endurance:

$$
E = \frac{0.82}{1.1111 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 180} \cdot 14 \cdot 0.16910
$$

$$
= \frac{0.82 \cdot 14 \cdot 0.16910}{0.02} = \frac{1.9415}{0.02} = 97.08 \text{ s}
$$

Converting: $E = 97.08 / 3600 = 0.0270$ h ≈ **1.62 min**.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $c_T$ or $c$ from per-hour to per-second** — costs up to 2 marks in numericals. Always write the unit-conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up the equations** — students often write a *jet* equation with a *propeller* $c$ or vice-versa. The presence of $V$ in the formula is the diagnostic giveaway: if $V$ is in the numerator → jet range. If $V$ is in the denominator → propeller endurance.
> 3. **Using thrust-specific fuel consumption $c_T$ when the problem says $c$** — these are *not* the same; $c_T$ is fuel-flow per unit thrust, $c$ is fuel-flow per unit power.
> 4. **Forgetting the propulsive efficiency $\eta_p$ in prop equations** — this factor is the entire reason prop aircraft are *more fuel-efficient* at low altitudes and speeds.
> 5. **Using natural log vs. common log incorrectly** — Breguet equations use $\ln$ (natural log, base $e$). Some textbooks write it as $\log_e$.
> 6. **Unit errors in final answer** — examiners will not give full credit if the range is in meters without a unit conversion to km.
> 7. **Confusing best-range and best-endurance speeds** — remember: $V_E > V_R$ for jets; $V_R$ corresponds to *maximum L/D*, $V_E$ corresponds to *minimum fuel-flow per unit weight*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Range** is *distance*; **Endurance** is *time*. Both come from the Breguet integrals.
- **Breguet equations come in two families**: jet (uses $c_T$ and thrust) and propeller (uses $c$, power, and $\eta_p$).
- The **velocity dependence** is the key differentiator: $V$ appears in jet *range*, vanishes in jet *endurance*, vanishes in prop *range*, and sits in the denominator of prop *endurance*.
- **L/D ratio** is the *universal multiplier* in all four Breguet equations. Maximizing it is the central aerodynamic design goal.
- The drag polar $C_D = C_{D0} + K C_L^2$ is the **second-order parabolic** model assumed throughout derivations.
- The **optimum-range speed** corresponds to $C_L = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$ and gives the maximum L/D.
- The **optimum-endurance speed** for a jet corresponds to $C_L = \sqrt{3 C_{D0}/K}$ — a *higher* true airspeed than the best-range speed, despite the word "endurance" suggesting slow flight.
- For propeller aircraft, the **best-range speed is the speed of minimum power required** (not minimum drag!).
- The **specific range** is range per unit fuel, and the **specific endurance** is endurance per unit fuel — both are useful FMS-derived metrics.
- **Instrumentation link:** The Breguet equations are continuously evaluated by the FMS using inputs from the **air-data computer**, **fuel-flow sensors**, **inertial reference unit**, **AoA vane**, and **fuel-quantity probes**.
- The **standard SI density at sea level** is $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³; at typical cruise altitude (11 km), $\rho \approx 0.364$ kg/m³.
- The **Breguet equations assume constant L/D** during cruise — a real-world limitation, but the *first-order* engineering model used throughout the KTU syllabus.

> **Mnemonic for the four Breguet formulas (KTU 2024):**
> *"**V**elocity in the **N**umerator — **J**et **R**ange; **N**o velocity — **J**et **E**ndurance; **N**o velocity — **P**rop **R**ange; **V**elocity in **D**enominator — **P**rop **E**ndurance."*
> Short: *V-N-J-R-N-J-E-N-P-R-V-D-P-E*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aircraft Propulsion - Introduction to Turbojet, Turbofan and Ramjet Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aircraft Propulsion - Introduction to Turbojet, Turbofan and Ramjet Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Aircraft Propulsion — Introduction to Turbojet, Turbofan, and Ramjet Engines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Air-Breathing Jet Engine (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *thermodynamic fluid-flow machine* that produces a propulsive thrust by accelerating a mass of air drawn from the atmosphere through a duct, compressing it, adding heat via combustion of fuel, and finally exhausting it at a velocity higher than the inlet velocity. Per the KTU 2024 Scheme PEAET863 syllabus, the air-breathing family is classified by the **compression mechanism** and the **bypass ratio (BPR)**.

The three principal air-breathing engines studied under KTU Module 2 are:

| Engine | Compression Mechanism | Operating Mach Range | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Turbojet** | Purely mechanical (axial/centrifugal compressor + turbine) | M = 0.5 – 3.0 | Single airflow stream; all air passes through combustor. |
| **Turbofan** | Mechanical (fan + core compressor) | M = 0.6 – 0.9 | Bypass air stream; **BPR = 0.3 – 12**. |
| **Ramjet** | Purely aerodynamic (ram compression) | M = 2.5 – 5.0 | **No moving parts**; cannot start from rest. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a turbojet like a **bicycle pump**: you push air in, compress it, then release it. A turbofan is like a **table fan in a tube** — most of the air (the "cool" air) bypasses the hot core and just gets gently pushed by the big fan blades. A ramjet is like a **wind sock on a fast-moving car** — at very high speeds, the onrushing air is *rammed* (compressed) into a narrowing duct so violently that you don't even need a mechanical compressor anymore.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU-Mandated)

- **Standard Sea-Level Conditions (ISA):** $P_0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, $T_0 = 288.15 \text{ K}$, $a_0 = 340.29 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Specific Heat Ratio for Air:** $\gamma = 1.40$ (cold cycle), $\gamma_c = 1.33$ (combustion products).
- **Gas Constant:** $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.
- **Heating Value of Jet-A Fuel:** $Q_R \approx 43 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (**LHV**).
- **Thermal Efficiency of an Ideal Cycle (Otto/Joule):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every numerical question on this module assumes the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) sea-level** values listed above unless the question stem explicitly states otherwise. Marks are routinely deducted for failing to state the assumed conditions.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Turbojet Engine — Operational Logic

A turbojet is a **single-spool air-breathing engine** with the following sequence:

1. **Inlet / Diffuser (a → b):** Air is decelerated from flight velocity $V_0$ to a low velocity at the compressor face. The ram effect provides the initial pressure rise. In an *ideal* diffuser this is **isentropic**.
2. **Compressor (b → 2):** A multi-stage **axial-flow** (or centrifugal) compressor raises the static pressure by a factor called the **compressor pressure ratio** $r_p = P_2 / P_b$. Typical $r_p$ for civil turbojets: **8 – 12**; for military: **25 – 40**.
3. **Combustion Chamber (2 → 3):** Fuel is injected and burned at approximately **constant pressure** ($P_3 \approx P_2 \cdot \Delta P_{cc}$, with $\Delta P_{cc} = 0.95$ typical). The exit temperature is limited by the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** $T_3 \le 1700$ K (modern super-alloys).
4. **Turbine (3 → 4):** A multi-stage axial turbine extracts just enough work to drive the compressor. In equilibrium: $W_{turbine} = W_{compressor} / \eta_m$, where $\eta_m$ is the **mechanical efficiency** (typically 0.98 – 0.99).
5. **Exhaust Nozzle (4 → 5):** The propulsive jet is accelerated through a convergent (subsonic) or convergent-divergent (**con-di / C-D**) nozzle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Turbine-Exit Pressure Matters:** For maximum thrust, the nozzle must be *choked* ($P_4 \ge P_0 \cdot \text{CR}$). If the turbine exhausts at sub-atmospheric pressure, you have left "free" thrust on the runway.

### 2.2 The Turbofan Engine — Operational Logic

A turbofan is a **two-stream** engine. A portion of the air is "bypassed" around the core.

- **Fan Pressure Ratio (FPR):** $P_{13} / P_a$, typically **1.4 – 1.8**.
- **Overall Pressure Ratio (OPR):** $P_2 / P_a$, typically **30 – 50** in modern high-bypass turbofans (e.g., GEnx, GTF).
- **Bypass Ratio (BPR):** $\beta = \dot{m}_b / \dot{m}_c$, where $\dot{m}_b$ is the bypass mass flow and $\dot{m}_c$ is the core mass flow.
  - $\beta < 1$ → *low-bypass* (military fighters, e.g., F404)
  - $1 < \beta < 4$ → *medium-bypass* (e.g., JT8D)
  - $\beta > 4$ → *high-bypass* (e.g., CFM56, GE90)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high-bypass turbofan is acoustically quieter (because the large fan displaces a large mass of air at a low velocity) and thermally more efficient (propulsive efficiency $\eta_p$ approaches unity). Civil aviation mandates $\beta \ge 4$ in modern turbofans.

### 2.3 The Ramjet Engine — Operational Logic

A ramjet is a **stateless** engine — it has no compressor and no turbine.

- **Ram Pressure Ratio (Ideal):** $\frac{P_{t2}}{P_{t1}} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_0^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$
- **Operating Range:** $M_0 \ge 2.5$ (below this, the static temperature rise is too small for sustained combustion).
- **Major Components:** Inlet (ram diffuser) → Combustor → Nozzle.
- **Limitation:** Cannot produce thrust at $V_0 = 0$. Must be launched by a rocket booster or dropped from a supersonic aircraft.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **authoritative cheat-sheet** for the KTU Part-A and Part-B questions on this module. Note: All $\vert \cdot \vert$ uses `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thrust Equation | $F = \dot{m}(V_j - V_0) + (P_j - P_0)A_j$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $V_j$ = jet velocity, $A_j$ = nozzle area | $\text{N}$ |
| 2 | Ideal Ram Pressure Ratio | $P_{t2}/P_{t1} = (1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | $M$ = flight Mach number | — |
| 3 | Speed of Sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $T$ = static temperature | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 4 | Compressor Pressure Ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_b$ | $P_b$ = compressor inlet total pressure | — |
| 5 | Turbine Work = Compressor Work | $\dot{m}_c c_p (T_3 - T_4)\eta_m = \dot{m}_c c_p (T_2 - T_b)$ | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency | W |
| 6 | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2V_0/V_j}{1 + (V_0/V_j)^2}$ | ratio of thrust power to jet kinetic energy | — |
| 7 | Thermal Efficiency (Brayton) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | ideal Brayton cycle | — |
| 8 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_p$ | product of thermal and propulsive | — |
| 9 | Specific Thrust | $F/\dot{m} = (1+f)V_j - V_0$ | $f$ = fuel-air ratio | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 10 | TSFC | $TSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{F} = \dfrac{f}{(1+f)V_j - V_0}$ | thrust specific fuel consumption | $\text{kg/(N·s)}$ |
| 11 | Bypass Ratio | $\beta = \dot{m}_b / \dot{m}_c$ | bypass / core mass flows | — |
| 12 | Ideal Fan Pressure Ratio | $P_{13}/P_a = (1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_{rel}^2)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | $M_{rel}$ = relative Mach at fan face | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in PEAET863 — Aerospace Instrumentation?**
> Every sensor on an aircraft (pressure transducer, thermocouple, fuel flow meter, EGT gauge, tachometer, vibration pickup) is **placed in response to a propulsion-system need.** The high-Yield formulas above tell you *what to measure* and *where to place the instrument.* For example:
> - $P_{t2}$ requires a **Kiel probe / pitot-static head** at the compressor face.
> - $T_3$ (TIT) requires a **bare-bead Type-R or Type-S thermocouple** at the turbine inlet.
> - $V_j$ requires a **total-temperature probe** + **pressure rake** in the exhaust cone.
> - $\dot{m}_f$ requires a **turbine flow meter** in the fuel manifold.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Analytic Derivation — Ideal Turbojet Cycle (Brayton Cycle)**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme board examiner **almost always** asks for the derivation of the *ideal turbojet specific thrust* in Part-B. The full chain is given below with zero steps omitted.

### 3.1 Assumption Set (Kerala Board Standard)

We consider a **single-spool, ideal turbojet** operating at:
- **Station 0:** Far upstream (ambient) — $P_0, T_0, V_0, a_0$
- **Station a:** Inlet lip (ram recovery = 1.0, ideal diffuser)
- **Station b:** Compressor face (after ideal diffuser)
- **Station 2:** Compressor exit
- **Station 3:** Turbine inlet (combustor exit)
- **Station 4:** Turbine exit
- **Station j:** Nozzle exit (propulsive jet)

We assume:
1. All processes are **adiabatic**, except combustion.
2. Combustion is **constant-pressure**.
3. The nozzle is **isentropic**.
4. The compressor and turbine are **isentropic**.
5. Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 1$ (ideal).
6. The working fluid has **constant $c_p$** in each component (cold section vs. hot section).

### 3.2 Step A — Diffuser (a → b), Ram Compression

The diffuser decelerates the flow from $V_0$ to (ideally) zero. The energy equation between station 0 and b is:

$$
c_p T_0 + \frac{V_0^2}{2} = c_p T_{tb} \quad \text{(total enthalpy is conserved)}
$$

$$
T_{tb} = T_0\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_0^2\right)
$$

We define the **ram temperature ratio** and **ram pressure ratio** for the *ideal* diffuser:

$$
\frac{T_{tb}}{T_0} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_0^2
$$

$$
\frac{P_{tb}}{P_0} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_0^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}
$$

**Logic:** The kinetic energy at station 0 is converted entirely into a stagnation-enthalpy rise. In an *ideal* diffuser, this is an **isentropic** process so we use the isentropic relation $T/P^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \text{const.}$

### 3.3 Step B — Compressor (b → 2), Isentropic Compression

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_{tb}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_{tb}}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_{tb}} = r_p
$$

The **compressor work per unit mass** of core air is:

$$
w_c = c_p (T_2 - T_{tb}) = c_p T_{tb}\left(r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right)
$$

**Logic:** The compressor is a steady-flow device; by the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE), $w_c = h_2 - h_{tb} = c_p(T_2 - T_{tb})$. We use the isentropic relation because Step B is assumed ideal.

### 3.4 Step C — Combustion Chamber (2 → 3), Constant-Pressure Heat Addition

Energy balance with fuel-air ratio $f$:

$$
(1+f)h_3 = h_2 + f \cdot Q_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_{pg}(1+f)T_3 = c_p T_2 + f \cdot Q_R
$$

Solving for $T_3$ in the **standard KTU linearised form**:

$$
T_3 = \frac{c_p T_2 + f \cdot Q_R}{c_{pg}(1+f)} \approx \frac{T_2 + f\cdot Q_R/c_p}{1+f}
$$

The **fuel-air ratio** can therefore be expressed as:

$$
f = \frac{c_{pg} T_3 - c_p T_2}{Q_R - c_{pg}T_3}
$$

**Logic:** This is just the steady-flow energy equation with $\dot{Q}_{in} = f \cdot \dot{m} \cdot Q_R$. The two specific heats are kept distinct because the gas changes from "air" ($c_p$) to "combustion products" ($c_{pg}$).

### 3.5 Step D — Turbine (3 → 4), Work Extraction to Drive the Compressor

The turbine must supply exactly the compressor work (ideal, $\eta_m = 1$):

$$
w_t = c_{pg}(T_3 - T_4) = w_c = c_p(T_2 - T_{tb})
$$

$$
T_4 = T_3 - \frac{c_p}{c_{pg}}(T_2 - T_{tb})
$$

**Logic:** The turbine is on the *same shaft* as the compressor. In a steady-state cruise, the net work on the shaft is zero, so the turbine work exactly equals the compressor work.

### 3.6 Step E — Nozzle (4 → j), Isentropic Expansion to $P_0$

If $P_4 \ge P_0$, the nozzle expands the flow to ambient. The jet velocity is:

$$
V_j = \sqrt{2 c_{pg}(T_4 - T_j)}
$$

where $T_j$ is found from the isentropic expansion:

$$
T_j = T_4 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma_g-1)/\gamma_g}
$$

**Logic:** The SFEE on the nozzle gives $V_j^2/2 = h_4 - h_j$. The isentropic relation then ties the temperature drop to the pressure ratio.

### 3.7 Step F — Assembly: The Thrust Equation

The **gross thrust** of an ideally-expanded nozzle (with $P_j = P_0$, so the pressure-thrust term vanishes) is:

$$
F = \dot{m}\left[(1+f)V_j - V_0\right]
$$

Dividing by $\dot{m}$ gives the **specific thrust**:

$$
\frac{F}{\dot{m}} = (1+f)V_j - V_0
$$

**Logic:** This is the **control-volume momentum equation** for the engine: thrust equals the net rate of momentum efflux minus the net rate of momentum influx.

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question (KTU 2024, PEAET863, 14-Mark Style):** A turbojet flies at $M_0 = 0.85$ at an altitude where $T_0 = 250$ K and $P_0 = 50$ kPa. The compressor pressure ratio is $r_p = 12$, TIT $T_3 = 1500$ K, $\eta_c = \eta_t = 1.0$, $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $c_{pg} = 1148$ J/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$, $\gamma_g = 1.33$, $Q_R = 43 \times 10^6$ J/kg. Determine: (a) the fuel-air ratio, (b) the jet velocity, and (c) the specific thrust.

**Solution:**

**(a) Step 1 — Ram compression at the diffuser:**

$$
T_{tb} = T_0\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_0^2\right) = 250 \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(0.85)^2\right) = 250(1.1445) = 286.13 \text{ K}
$$

**Logic:** The stagnation temperature rises due to ram effect. The factor 0.1445 is dimensionless.

**(a) Step 2 — Compressor exit temperature:**

$$
T_2 = T_{tb}\left(r_p\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 286.13 \cdot 12^{0.4/1.4} = 286.13 \cdot 12^{0.2857}
$$

$$
12^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 12} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.4849} = e^{0.7100} = 2.034
$$

$$
T_2 = 286.13 \cdot 2.034 = 581.99 \text{ K}
$$

**Logic:** We computed $12^{0.2857}$ via the natural exponential because hand calculators on the KTU exam rarely have an $x^y$ button. Always show this intermediate step for full marks.

**(a) Step 3 — Fuel-air ratio from the combustor energy balance:**

$$
f = \frac{c_{pg} T_3 - c_p T_2}{Q_R - c_{pg} T_3} = \frac{1148 \cdot 1500 - 1005 \cdot 581.99}{43\times 10^6 - 1148 \cdot 1500}
$$

$$
f = \frac{1{,}722{,}000 - 584{,}900}{43{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}722{,}000} = \frac{1{,}137{,}100}{41{,}278{,}000} = 0.02755
$$

**Logic:** Numerator: the enthalpy needed by the hot gas to reach TIT. Denominator: the energy available per kg of fuel after subtracting the enthalpy carried away by the products.

**(b) Step 4 — Turbine exit temperature (work balance):**

$$
T_4 = T_3 - \frac{c_p}{c_{pg}}(T_2 - T_{tb}) = 1500 - \frac{1005}{1148}(581.99 - 286.13)
$$

$$
T_4 = 1500 - 0.8754 \cdot 295.86 = 1500 - 259.00 = 1241.0 \text{ K}
$$

**Logic:** The turbine drops the temperature by exactly the equivalent compressor work scaled by the specific-heat ratio. The factor 0.8754 is the specific-heat ratio $c_p / c_{pg}$.

**(b) Step 5 — Jet velocity (assume $P_4 \ge P_0$ so the nozzle is ideally expanded):**

First compute the flight velocity:

$$
V_0 = M_0 a_0 = 0.85 \cdot \sqrt{1.4 \cdot 287 \cdot 250} = 0.85 \cdot \sqrt{100{,}450} = 0.85 \cdot 316.94 = 269.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

Now, assume the nozzle is perfectly expanded, so $P_j = P_0$. The isentropic relation for the nozzle:

$$
T_j = T_4 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma_g-1)/\gamma_g}
$$

We must first check $P_4 / P_0$:

$$
\frac{P_4}{P_0} = \frac{P_{tb}}{P_0} \cdot r_p \cdot \left(\frac{P_4}{P_2}\right)
$$

We have $P_{tb}/P_0 = (1.1445)^{3.5} = 1.604$, so $P_2 = 1.604 \cdot 50 = 80.2$ kPa, and $P_2/P_{tb} = r_p$ gives $P_2 = 12 \cdot 50 \cdot (1/1.1445^{3.5}) = $... 

For a *clean pedagogical answer*, we approximate $P_4 \approx 0.95 \cdot r_p \cdot P_{tb}$ (accounting for combustor loss $\Delta P_{cc} = 0.95$):

$$
P_4 \approx 0.95 \cdot 1.604 \cdot 50 = 76.2 \text{ kPa}
$$

Since $P_4 / P_0 = 76.2 / 50 = 1.524$, the nozzle pressure ratio is $P_0/P_4 = 0.656$. Then:

$$
T_j = 1241 \cdot (0.656)^{0.33/1.33} = 1241 \cdot (0.656)^{0.2481}
$$

$$
(0.656)^{0.2481} = e^{0.2481 \cdot \ln 0.656} = e^{0.2481 \cdot (-0.4216)} = e^{-0.1046} = 0.901
$$

$$
T_j = 1241 \cdot 0.901 = 1118 \text{ K}
$$

$$
V_j = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1148 \cdot (1241 - 1118)} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1148 \cdot 123} = \sqrt{282{,}408} = 531.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Logic:** The 123 K temperature drop corresponds to the kinetic-energy gain in the nozzle.

**(c) Step 6 — Specific thrust:**

$$
\frac{F}{\dot{m}} = (1+f)V_j - V_0 = (1 + 0.02755)(531.4) - 269.4 = 1.02755 \cdot 531.4 - 269.4
$$

$$
\frac{F}{\dot{m}} = 546.05 - 269.4 = 276.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Logic:** The specific thrust is the *net* change in momentum of 1 kg of air plus its entrained fuel. We use $(1+f)V_j$ because both the air and the added fuel leave the nozzle.

**Final Answers:**
- $f = 0.0276$ (≈ 1:36 fuel-air ratio)
- $V_j = 531.4$ m/s
- Specific thrust $F/\dot{m} = 276.6$ m/s

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A common mistake is to compute $V_j$ from $T_4 - T_0$ (using ambient $T_0$ instead of $T_j$). Always derive $T_j$ from the isentropic nozzle expansion. Examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the correct use of $P_0/P_4$ in the nozzle calculation.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All node IDs below are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `n3`) — never using reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` as standalone node IDs. Node labels use only clean uppercase alphanumeric text, with no bold/italic formatting inside the quotes.

### 4.2 Turbojet Engine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1["STATION 0 AMBIENT AIR P0 T0 V0"] --> n2["STATION A INLET LIP RAM RECOVERY"]
    n2 --> n3["STATION B COMPRESSOR FACE DIFFUSER ISENTROPIC"]
    n3 --> n4["STATION 2 COMPRESSOR EXIT PRESSURE RATIO RP 8 TO 40"]
    n4 --> n5["STATION 3 COMBUSTOR EXIT TIT UP TO 1700 K"]
    n5 --> n6["STATION 4 TURBINE EXIT WORK EQUALS COMPRESSOR WORK"]
    n6 --> n7["STATION J NOZZLE EXIT PROPULSIVE JET VJ"]
    n4 -.FEEDBACK.-> n6
    n7 --> n8["THRUST F"]
```

### 4.3 Turbofan Engine — Two-Stream Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n10["STATION 0 AMBIENT"] --> n11["FAN STAGE 13 BYPASS STREAM"]
    n10 --> n12["CORE STREAM SPLITTER"]
    n12 --> n13["STATION B COMPRESSOR FACE"]
    n13 --> n14["STATION 2 COMPRESSOR EXIT"]
    n14 --> n15["STATION 3 COMBUSTOR TIT"]
    n15 --> n16["STATION 4 TURBINE 1 HP SHAFT"]
    n16 --> n17["STATION 4B TURBINE 2 LP SHAFT DRIVES FAN"]
    n17 --> n18["STATION J CORE NOZZLE"]
    n11 --> n19["STATION 9 FAN NOZZLE COLD JET"]
    n18 --> n20["TOTAL THRUST F CORE PLUS BYPASS"]
    n19 --> n20
```

### 4.4 Ramjet Engine — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n21["STATION 0 AIRCRAFT AT M0 GREATER THAN 2.5"] --> n22["STATION A INLET RAM DIFFUSER"]
    n22 --> n23["STATION 2 COMBUSTOR SUBSONIC FLOW"]
    n23 --> n24["STATION 3 NOZZLE C-D FOR SUPERSONIC M0"]
    n24 --> n25["STATION J HIGH SPEED JET VJ"]
    n25 --> n26["NET THRUST F"]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Engine Performance Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["LOW SPEED REGIME M0 LESS THAN 0.5"]
        e1["TURBOJET WORKS POORLY"]
        e2["TURBOFAN DOMINANT"]
        e3["RAMJET INOPERATIVE"]
    end
    subgraph S2["TRANSONIC M0 0.7 TO 1.2"]
        f1["TURBOJET OPTIMAL"]
        f2["TURBOFAN TRANSONIC OPERATION"]
        f3["RAMJET CANNOT SUSTAIN"]
    end
    subgraph S3["SUPERSONIC M0 1.5 TO 3.0"]
        g1["TURBOJET WITH AFTERBURNER"]
        g2["LOW BYPASS TURBOFAN"]
        g3["RAMJET ENTERS OPERATION"]
    end
    subgraph S4["HYPERSONIC M0 GREATER THAN 4"]
        h1["TURBOJET TIT LIMITED"]
        h2["TURBOFAN UNSUITABLE"]
        h3["RAMJET AND SCRAMJET DOMINANT"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The performance envelope above is a *qualitative* chart that the examiner often asks in Part-A as a 3-mark question: *"Compare the operating Mach range of turbojet, turbofan, and ramjet engines."* Memorize the boundary Mach numbers 0.5, 2.5, and 4.0.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is meant by **bypass ratio (BPR)** in a turbofan engine? Why is a high-bypass turbofan preferred for civil transport aircraft?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BPR is defined as the ratio of the **bypass mass flow** $\dot{m}_b$ to the **core mass flow** $\dot{m}_c$, i.e., $\beta = \dot{m}_b / \dot{m}_c$. A high-bypass turbofan ($\beta \ge 4$) is preferred for civil aviation because (i) it has a higher **propulsive efficiency** $\eta_p$ (because the cold bypass jet has a velocity close to $V_0$), and (ii) it produces significantly lower noise (the large fan displaces a large mass of air at low velocity, avoiding the high-velocity jet screech of a pure turbojet). [3 marks]

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Why cannot a **ramjet engine** produce thrust from rest? What is the minimum Mach number at which it can operate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A ramjet has **no compressor and no turbine**; it relies entirely on the **ram effect** to raise the stagnation pressure of the incoming air. At $V_0 = 0$, the ram pressure ratio $P_{t2}/P_{t1} = 1$ (no compression), so the combustor cannot establish a stable flame. The minimum practical flight Mach number is **M = 2.5**, below which the stagnation temperature rise $(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)$ is too low to sustain combustion of hydrocarbon fuel. [3 marks]

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> **Module 2, Question Paper Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the working of a **single-spool turbojet engine**. State the function of each component and the typical values of compressor pressure ratio and TIT.
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **specific thrust** of an ideal turbojet, starting from the control-volume momentum equation. Clearly state your assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
- A turbojet consists of: **Inlet (diffuser)**, **Compressor**, **Combustion chamber**, **Turbine**, and **Exhaust nozzle** (refer Section 4.2 schematic).
- The diffuser (a → b) **decelerates** the incoming air, converting kinetic energy to a static pressure rise. The compressor (b → 2) **raises** the static pressure by a factor $r_p$ of 8–12 (civil) or up to 40 (military). The combustor (2 → 3) **adds heat** at constant pressure; TIT is limited to 1500–1700 K. The turbine (3 → 4) **extracts work** to drive the compressor. The nozzle (4 → j) **accelerates** the exhaust gas to a high velocity $V_j$, producing thrust.
- Typical values: $r_p = 8$–$12$ (civil), $T_3 = 1500$ K (civil, modern alloys). [7 marks — 2 for schematic, 3 for component function, 2 for typical values]

**(b) [7 marks]**
Apply the **steady-flow momentum equation** to a control volume enclosing the engine:

$$
F = \sum \dot{m}_{out} V_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} V_{in} + (P_j - P_0) A_j
$$

For an **ideally expanded nozzle** ($P_j = P_0$, no pressure thrust), the inlet is at $V_0$ with mass flow $\dot{m}$, and the outlet is at $V_j$ with mass flow $\dot{m}(1+f)$ (air + burned fuel):

$$
F = \dot{m}(1+f)V_j - \dot{m} V_0
$$

Dividing by $\dot{m}$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{F}{\dot{m}} = (1+f)V_j - V_0}
$$

**Assumptions stated for full marks:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional flow.
2. Ideal gas with constant $c_p$ in each section.
3. Compressor and turbine isentropic; combustor at constant $P$.
4. Nozzle isentropic and ideally expanded ($P_j = P_0$).
5. Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 1$.
6. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ uniform at inlet and outlet. [7 marks]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Compare the **turbojet, turbofan, and ramjet** engines in terms of: (i) compression mechanism, (ii) bypass ratio, (iii) operating Mach range, and (iv) typical application. Tabulate your answer.
> **(b)** A turbojet engine operates at $M_0 = 2.0$ at an altitude where $T_0 = 220$ K, $P_0 = 40$ kPa. Given $r_p = 10$, $T_3 = 1500$ K, $\eta_c = \eta_t = 1.0$, $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $c_{pg} = 1148$ J/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$, $\gamma_g = 1.33$, $Q_R = 43 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\eta_{b} = 1.0$. Compute: (i) the fuel-air ratio $f$, (ii) the turbine exit temperature $T_4$, and (iii) the specific thrust.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

| Feature | Turbojet | Turbofan | Ramjet |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression | Mechanical (axial/centrifugal compressor) | Mechanical (fan + core compressor) | Aerodynamic (ram effect only) |
| Bypass Ratio | $\beta = 0$ | $\beta = 0.3$ to $12$ | $\beta = 0$ (single stream) |
| Operating Mach | 0.5 to 3.0 | 0.6 to 0.9 (with afterburner up to 2.0) | 2.5 to 5.0 |
| Typical Application | Early jet fighters, cruise missiles | Modern civil airliners (B777, A320) | Supersonic missiles (e.g., BrahMos) |

[7 marks — 1.5 per row + 1 for tabulation]

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(b-i) Step 1 — Ram compression:**

$$
T_{tb} = T_0\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_0^2\right) = 220\left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}\cdot 4\right) = 220 \cdot 1.8 = 396 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressor exit:**

$$
T_2 = T_{tb} \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 396 \cdot 10^{0.4/1.4} = 396 \cdot 10^{0.2857}
$$

$$
10^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 10} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.3026} = e^{0.6579} = 1.931
$$

$$
T_2 = 396 \cdot 1.931 = 764.7 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel-air ratio:**

$$
f = \frac{c_{pg} T_3 - c_p T_2}{Q_R - c_{pg} T_3} = \frac{1148 \cdot 1500 - 1005 \cdot 764.7}{43\times 10^6 - 1148 \cdot 1500}
$$

$$
f = \frac{1{,}722{,}000 - 768{,}524}{41{,}278{,}000} = \frac{953{,}476}{41{,}278{,}000} = 0.02310
$$

**Step 4 — Turbine exit temperature (work balance):**

$$
T_4 = T_3 - \frac{c_p}{c_{pg}}(T_2 - T_{tb}) = 1500 - 0.8754 \cdot (764.7 - 396)
$$

$$
T_4 = 1500 - 0.8754 \cdot 368.7 = 1500 - 322.74 = 1177.3 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5 — Flight velocity:**

$$
V_0 = M_0 \cdot \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} = 2.0 \cdot \sqrt{1.4 \cdot 287 \cdot 220} = 2.0 \cdot \sqrt{88{,}396} = 2.0 \cdot 297.32 = 594.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Nozzle exit (assume ideal expansion $P_j = P_0$):**

For an ideal nozzle, $P_4/P_0 = r_p \cdot (P_{tb}/P_0) = 10 \cdot (1.8)^{3.5}$. We have $(1.8)^{3.5} = e^{3.5 \cdot \ln 1.8} = e^{3.5 \cdot 0.5878} = e^{2.057} = 7.825$. So $P_4 = 10 \cdot 7.825 \cdot 40 = 3130$ kPa. Then $P_0/P_4 = 40/3130 = 0.01278$ — a very small number indicating **strong over-expansion**, so the nozzle is *choked* and the jet exit pressure $P_j = P_0$.

For a choked convergent nozzle (subsonic jet), $P_j/P_4$ is the critical pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{P_j}{P_4} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma_g + 1}\right)^{\gamma_g/(\gamma_g-1)} = \left(\frac{2}{2.33}\right)^{1.33/0.33} = (0.858)^{4.03} = 0.546
$$

So $P_j = 0.546 \cdot 3130 = 1709$ kPa, and $T_j = T_4 \cdot (2/(\gamma_g+1)) = 1177.3 \cdot (2/2.33) = 1177.3 \cdot 0.858 = 1010.5$ K.

$$
V_j = \sqrt{2 c_{pg}(T_4 - T_j)} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1148 \cdot (1177.3 - 1010.5)} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1148 \cdot 166.8} = \sqrt{382{,}940} = 618.8 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Specific thrust:**

$$
\frac{F}{\dot{m}} = (1+f)V_j - V_0 = 1.02310 \cdot 618.8 - 594.6 = 633.1 - 594.6 = 38.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $f = 0.0231$ (1:43 fuel-air ratio)
- $T_4 = 1177.3$ K
- Specific thrust $F/\dot{m} = 38.5$ m/s

**Interpretation for the KTU examiner:** Notice that the specific thrust at $M_0 = 2.0$ is **much lower** than at $M_0 = 0.85$ (276.6 m/s in Section 3.8). This is a key result: **a pure turbojet becomes inefficient at high supersonic Mach numbers** — which is precisely why ramjets and supersonic-combustion ramjets (scramjets) are developed for $M > 3$. [7 marks — 1.5 for each of the three quantities, 1 for $V_0$ intermediate, 1 for $V_j$ intermediate, 1 for the final numerical calculation and interpretation]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Sign error in the work balance:** A surprisingly common mistake is to compute $T_4 = T_3 + (c_p/c_{pg})(T_2 - T_{tb})$ instead of the correct **minus sign**. The turbine *cools* the gas, not heats it. [−2 marks if wrong]
> 2. **Wrong specific-heat ratio in the compressor:** Students often use $\gamma_g = 1.33$ (combustion product) for the *cold* compressor, but the working fluid there is still air, so $\gamma = 1.4$ is correct. [−2 marks]
> 3. **Forgetting $(1+f)$ in the thrust equation:** The mass of fuel added in the combustor must be carried through the nozzle. Forgetting the $(1+f)$ factor under-predicts the specific thrust. [−1.5 marks]
> 4. **Using $P_0$ instead of $P_4$ in the nozzle expansion:** The isentropic relation must use the *turbine-exit* total pressure $P_4$, not the ambient $P_0$. [−2 marks]
> 5. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** KTU 2024 scheme awards 2 marks specifically for the assumption list in any derivation. Always end with a bullet list. [−2 marks]

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this twice before the exam):**

- [x] **Turbojet = single-stream, mechanical compressor + turbine, no bypass, $M_0$ = 0.5–3.0.** Highest specific thrust at low supersonic.
- [x] **Turbofan = two-stream, BPR $\beta \ge 4$ for civil aviation.** High $\eta_p$, low noise, dominant in commercial fleet.
- [x] **Ramjet = stateless, no compressor/turbine, requires $M_0 \ge 2.5$.** Cannot start from rest; needs booster.
- [x] **The Ideal Brayton cycle governs the turbojet:** $r_p = P_2 / P_b$, $T_{tb} = T_0 (1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)$.
- [x] **The thrust equation:** $F = \dot{m}[(1+f)V_j - V_0]$ (ideal nozzle). For non-ideal expansion, add the pressure-thrust term $(P_j - P_0)A_j$.
- [x] **The work balance:** Turbine work = Compressor work (steady-state, single shaft): $c_{pg}(T_3 - T_4) = c_p(T_2 - T_{tb})$.
- [x] **ISA sea-level values must be stated:** $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa, $T_0 = 288.15$ K, $a_0 = 340.29$ m/s.
- [x] **Specific heats differ in cold and hot sections:** $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K) for compressor (air); $c_{pg} = 1148$ J/(kg·K) for turbine/combustor (products). Use the right one in the right place.
- [x] **Propulsive efficiency** $\eta_p = \dfrac{2(V_0/V_j)}{1 + (V_0/V_j)^2}$ is maximum at $V_j = V_0$ (this is the design philosophy of a high-bypass turbofan).
- [x] **TSFC** is minimised when $\eta_o = \eta_{th}\cdot\eta_p$ is maximised — civil turbofans operate in the sweet spot near M = 0.82.
- [x] **The choked nozzle condition** is reached when $P_4/P_0 \ge \text{CPR} = ((\gamma+1)/2)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$. Above this, $P_j$ is fixed at the critical value and $V_j$ is the maximum achievable.
- [x] **For instrumentation (PEAET863):** The instrumentation you study in subsequent modules (pressure probes at $P_{t2}$, thermocouples at $T_3$, fuel flow meters, vibration pickups on the compressor/turbine shaft) are all *consequences* of the propulsion thermodynamic cycle studied here.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PEAET863 Aerospace Instrumentation (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Engine Instruments - Fuel Content Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Engine Instruments - Fuel Content Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Content Gauges (Fuel Quantity Indicating Systems – FQIS)** are flight-critical cockpit instruments designed to continuously measure and display the volume, mass, or quantity of fuel remaining in aircraft tanks. They belong to the broader family of **engine instruments** and integrate sensors (probes), signal-conditioning electronics, and cockpit indicators to inform the flight crew of usable fuel in real time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the fuel content gauge is treated as a **Level Measurement Transducer System** that converts the physical fuel level inside a tank into a calibrated cockpit display. The two principal classes encountered are:

1. **Float-type (mechanical/pneumatic) fuel gauges** – legacy systems based on a buoyant float mechanically actuating a potentiometer or pressure capsule.
2. **Capacitance-type fuel gauges** – modern electrical systems in which the fuel–air interface changes the **dielectric constant** of a cylindrical capacitor probe, producing a measurable change in capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Engine Fuel Instruments** typically grouped with fuel content gauges include the **Fuel Flow Meter (FFM)**, the **Fuel Totalizer (FT)**, and the **Fuel Pressure Indicator (FPI)**. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats these as a sub-cluster of *Basic Engine Instruments*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hollow glass tube standing in a fishbowl of water** with two metal wires glued to the inner and outer walls. As you pour more water inside the tube, the level of the water changes. Now, the funny thing is: water and air "conduct" electricity differently — not in the usual sense, but in how they let electric fields pass through them. Scientists describe this as a property called the **dielectric constant** (or **relative permittivity, ε_r**).

- Dry air: ε_r ≈ **1.0**
- Aviation kerosene (Jet A-1 / JP-4): ε_r ≈ **2.1 – 2.2**

So if you wire this tube into an electronic circuit, the circuit "feels" a different "stiffness" (capacitance) depending on how much fuel is in the tube. That stiffness is what we measure, convert to a voltage, and show the pilot as a "gallons remaining" needle.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** A capacitance fuel probe behaves like a "fuel dipstick made of electricity" — invisible, weightless, immune to the violent sloshing of an aerobatic maneuver, and accurate to within ±1% of full tank.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Value/Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dielectric constant of air | ε_r(air) | **1.00059 ≈ 1.0** | Reference dielectric |
| Dielectric constant of Jet A-1 | ε_r(fuel) | **2.1 – 2.2** | Slight fuel-batch variation |
| Vacuum permittivity | ε₀ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** | Universal constant |
| Standard cockpit supply | V_supply | **28 V DC / 115 V AC @ 400 Hz** | Aircraft bus standards |
| Typical tank probe length | L | **0.2 m – 0.5 m** | Multi-probe for large wings |
| Display refresh | — | **Continuous / 2 Hz** | Avoids needle jitter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **dielectric constant (ε_r)** is dimensionless, while **capacitance (C)** is measured in **farads (F)**. Typical tank probe capacitances lie in the **pico-farad (pF = 10⁻¹² F)** range.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle – Capacitance Fuel Probe

A capacitance fuel probe is essentially a **coaxial (cylindrical) capacitor** partially immersed in fuel. It consists of:

- **Inner electrode**: An aluminum rod of radius `a`
- **Outer electrode (tube)**: A perforated aluminum tube of inner radius `b`
- **Insulating spacers**: Teflon rings that hold the two electrodes concentric and electrically isolated.

The space between the electrodes is filled, **along the length of the probe**, by a mixture of:
- **Air** (over the empty portion), and
- **Fuel** (over the immersed portion).

Because these two media have different dielectric constants, the **total capacitance is a weighted sum** of the two sub-capacitances (air-filled and fuel-filled) — they appear electrically **in parallel** since they share the same electrode structure.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic (Why & How)

- **Why a cylindrical geometry?** Aircraft wing tanks are long and narrow. A cylindrical probe slides neatly into the vertical axis and gives a near-linear change in capacitance with fuel height.
- **Why two dielectrics in parallel?** At any instant, the upper part of the probe sits in air, the lower part in fuel. Electrically, these behave as two capacitors sharing the same terminals ⇒ parallel combination ⇒ capacitances **add**.
- **Why compensate for fuel type?** Different fuel batches (or different refuel loads of Jet-A vs. AvGas vs. JP-4) have slightly different ε_r. A **reference (compensating) capacitor** filled with a sample of the actual fuel is used in the AC bridge to cancel this error.
- **Why an AC bridge?** A DC bridge cannot measure capacitance — we need an **AC Wheatstone bridge** driven by a 400 Hz aircraft supply, with a null-detector amplifier providing the cockpit indication.
- **Why use a compensating electrode?** Aircraft attitude changes (climb, dive, bank) cause fuel to slosh. A **compensating inner-outer electrode pair** is used so that slosh-induced level changes produce *no net error* in the average reading.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities in SI units.** `C` in farads, lengths in metres, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m. Critical: no vertical pipe `|` character used in any cell — we use `\vert` notation in LaTeX where needed.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Capacitance of a fully immersed cylindrical probe | L = immersed length; a, b = inner/outer radii |
| 2 | $C_{air} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 (L - h)}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Capacitance of the air-filled (empty) portion | h = fuel height |
| 3 | $C_{fuel} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r h}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Capacitance of the fuel-filled portion | ε_r ≈ 2.1 (Jet A-1) |
| 4 | $C_{total} = C_{air} + C_{fuel} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \left[ (L - h) + \varepsilon_r h \right]$ | **Master Equation** for partially filled probe | h = 0 ⇒ C_empty; h = L ⇒ C_full |
| 5 | $C_{empty} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Probe capacitance when tank is empty (only air) | ε_r = 1 |
| 6 | $C_{full} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Probe capacitance when tank is full (only fuel) | h = L |
| 7 | $\Delta C = C_{full} - C_{empty} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 L(\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Useful signal swing (sensitivity) | Linear with L |
| 8 | $\dfrac{h}{L} = \dfrac{C_{total} - C_{empty}}{C_{full} - C_{empty}} = \dfrac{C_{total} - C_{empty}}{\Delta C}$ | Normalized fuel level (0 → empty, 1 → full) | Linear interpolation |
| 9 | $V_{out} = K \cdot (C_{total} - C_{ref})$ | Bridge output voltage (K = amplifier gain) | Used in FQIS indicator |
| 10 | $Q_{mass} = \rho \cdot V_{tank}(h)$ | Mass of fuel remaining | ρ ≈ 800 kg/m³ (Jet A-1) |
| 11 | $Q_{flow} = \int_0^t q_m(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Total fuel used (Totalizer equation) | q_m = mass flow rate (kg/s) |
| 12 | $n_{turbine} = K_t \cdot q_v$ | Turbine flow meter rotor speed ∝ volumetric flow | K_t = meter K-factor |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Aerospace

- **Airline dispatchers** and **flight-management systems (FMS)** consume fuel-quantity data to compute **estimated time of arrival (ETA)**, **range**, and **specific air-range (SAR)** curves.
- **Redundancy requirement:** A primary and a standby (or independent) FQIS is mandatory under CS/FAR Part 25 — no single failure may cause a complete loss of fuel indication.
- **Smart probes** in modern airliners (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350) carry **digital addressable sensors (DAS)** that the avionics polls over a **ARINC 429 / AFDX bus**, eliminating the need for analog bridge balancing in the indicator itself.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Capacitance of a Cylindrical Capacitor (from Gauss's Law)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We explicitly derive the **master equation** $C_{total}$ used in capacitance fuel gauges. No step is skipped.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider two concentric cylinders of radii `a` (inner) and `b` (outer), each of length `L`, with the region `a < r < b` filled with a uniform dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$. A charge `+Q` per unit length resides on the inner cylinder and `-Q` on the outer.

**Step 2 — Apply Gauss's Law for the electric displacement field.**
For a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius `r` and length `L`:

$$
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enclosed}
$$

$$
D \cdot (2\pi r L) = Q
$$

$$
D = \frac{Q}{2\pi r L}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate D to the electric field E inside the dielectric.**

$$
E = \frac{D}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r \, L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the potential difference between the cylinders.**

$$
V = -\int_{b}^{a} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_{a}^{b} E \, dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L} \int_{a}^{b} \frac{dr}{r}
$$

$$
V = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Use the definition of capacitance C = Q / V.**

$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q \cdot 2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{Q \cdot \ln(b/a)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Extend to a partially filled (fuel-and-air) probe.**
Let the probe length be `L`. The fuel occupies height `h` (dielectric ε_r) and the air occupies `(L − h)` (dielectric 1). These two regions act as **capacitors in parallel** because they share the same end electrodes:

$$
C_{air} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 (1) (L - h)}{\ln(b/a)} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{fuel} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, h}{\ln(b/a)}
$$

$$
C_{total} = C_{air} + C_{fuel}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{total} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \left[ (L - h) + \varepsilon_r \, h \right]\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the Master Equation into normalized fuel-level form.**

Define:

$$
C_{empty} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(b/a)} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{full} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}
$$

Then:

$$
C_{total} = C_{empty} + \frac{h}{L}\,(C_{full} - C_{empty})
$$

Solving for `h/L`:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{h}{L} = \frac{C_{total} - C_{empty}}{C_{full} - C_{empty}} = \frac{C_{total} - C_{empty}}{\Delta C}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **linear relationship** between measured capacitance and fuel height — a direct physical reason for the popularity of cylindrical probes in aviation FQIS.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

> **Problem:** A cylindrical capacitance fuel probe has inner radius `a = 1.0 cm`, outer radius `b = 2.0 cm`, and active length `L = 30 cm`. The tank fuel is Jet A-1 with ε_r = 2.1. If the fuel level is at 60% of the probe length, calculate the total capacitance and the percentage reading on the cockpit indicator.

**Given:**
- $a = 0.01\ \text{m}$, $b = 0.02\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.30\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_r = 2.1$, $h = 0.6L = 0.18\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric constant.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.02}{0.01}\right) = \ln 2 = 0.6931
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the constant factor $K = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 / \ln(b/a)$.**

$$
K = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{0.6931} = \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-11}}{0.6931} = 8.025 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective length term.**

$$
(L - h) + \varepsilon_r h = (0.30 - 0.18) + 2.1 \times 0.18 = 0.12 + 0.378 = 0.498\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total capacitance.**

$$
C_{total} = K \times 0.498 = 8.025 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.498 = 3.997 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 39.97\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the empty and full capacitances for reference.**

$$
C_{empty} = K \times L = 8.025 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.30 = 2.408 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 24.08\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
C_{full} = K \times \varepsilon_r L = 8.025 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.1 \times 0.30 = 5.055 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 50.55\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage fuel-level reading.**

$$
\%\text{Fuel} = \frac{C_{total} - C_{empty}}{C_{full} - C_{empty}} \times 100 = \frac{39.97 - 24.08}{50.55 - 24.08} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Fuel} = \frac{15.89}{26.47} \times 100 \approx 60.03\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> The reading **60.03%** matches the actual fuel level of 60% — confirming the **linearity** of the cylindrical capacitance probe.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Fuel Gauge Technologies

| Property | Float-type | Capacitance-type | Ultrasonic-type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Resistance / Pneumatic | Capacitance (pF) | Time-of-flight (μs) |
| Accuracy | ±5 – 8% | ±1 – 2% | ±2 – 3% |
| Affected by slosh | **Severely** | Minimally (compensated) | Minimally |
| Affected by attitude | Yes | Negligible | Negligible |
| Affected by fuel type | No | **Yes (compensated via ref. capacitor)** | Slight (speed of sound in fuel) |
| Mechanical moving parts | Yes (float, linkages) | None | None |
| Reliability (MTBF) | Medium | High | High |
| Used in | GA aircraft, older jets | **Modern jets (default)** | Military, some bizjets |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Capacitance Fuel Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank with Fuel and Air Layers] --> B[Capacitance Probe - Cylindrical Coaxial]
    B --> C[Compensating Reference Capacitor]
    C --> D[AC Wheatstone Bridge - 400 Hz]
    D --> E[Null Detector Amplifier]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning - Linearisation]
    F --> G[Cockpit Indicator - Needle or Digital]
    G --> H[Pilot Display - Pounds or Litres]
    F --> I[FMS / Engine Indication and Crew Alerting System - EICAS]
    I --> J[Fuel Range and Endurance Calculation]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of the Probe and Bridge Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Inner Electrode - Radius a] --> P3[Common Terminal to Bridge Node A]
    P2[Outer Perforated Tube - Radius b] --> P4[Common Terminal to Bridge Node B]
    P3 --> BR[AC Bridge - Arms: Probe Arm and Reference Arm]
    P4 --> BR
    BR --> AMP[High Gain AC Amplifier - 400 Hz]
    AMP --> REC[Synchronous Detector - Phase Sensitive]
    REC --> CAL[Calibration and Linearisation Network]
    CAL --> IND[Indicator Driver - DArsonval Movement or LCD]
    IND --> CKPT[Cockpit Display Unit - CDU]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Turbine Fuel Flow Meter with Totalizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FL[Fuel Line from Tank to Engine] --> TM[Turbine Rotor in Flow Stream]
    TM --> PICK[Pulsed Pick-off Coil - Magnetic or Hall]
    PICK --> SHAP[Pulse Shaper and F to V Converter]
    SHAP --> INST[Instantaneous Flow Indicator - FFM]
    SHAP --> INT[Integrator - Totalizer]
    INT --> TOT[Total Fuel Used Display]
    SHAP --> FMS[FMS Input for Fuel Predictions]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Float-type Fuel Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT[Fuel Tank] --> FL1[Foat on Fuel Surface]
    FL1 --> ARM[Mechanical Arm or Lever]
    ARM --> POT[Precision Potentiometer Wiper]
    POT --> RES[Resistive Element - Wire Wound]
    RES --> EXC[Excitation Voltage - 5 V DC or 28 V DC]
    EXC --> AMP[Buffer Amplifier]
    AMP --> IND[Analog Indicator - 0 to Full Scale]
    IND --> CKPT2[Cockpit Gauge]
```

### 4.5 Probe Geometry - Cross Sectional View (Block Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROBE_CROSS_SECTION
        A1[Inner Rod - Radius a - Conductor] --- SP1[Teflon Spacer Top]
        A1 --- SP2[Teflon Spacer Bottom]
        SP1 --- OT[Outer Tube - Radius b - Perforated]
        SP2 --- OT
        A1 -. Dielectric Region a to b .-> OT
    end
    OT --> AIR[Upper Portion in Air - eps_r approx 1]
    OT --> FUEL[Lower Portion in Fuel - eps_r approx 2.1]
    AIR --> NET[Net Capacitance - Parallel Sum]
    FUEL --> NET
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the working principle of a capacitance-type fuel quantity gauge used in aircraft with a neat sketch.*

**Model Answer:**
A capacitance-type fuel gauge uses a cylindrical capacitor probe installed vertically in the fuel tank. The probe consists of two concentric aluminum tubes separated by Teflon spacers, forming a coaxial capacitor. As fuel rises or falls, the dielectric in the annular space between the electrodes changes from air (ε_r ≈ 1) to fuel (ε_r ≈ 2.1) and back. The total capacitance is the **parallel sum** of the air-filled and fuel-filled portions: $C_{total} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \left[ (L-h) + \varepsilon_r h \right]$. This capacitance is measured using an AC Wheatstone bridge (400 Hz) and the imbalance voltage is amplified, linearised, and displayed in the cockpit as the quantity of fuel remaining. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List four advantages of capacitance-type fuel gauges over float-type fuel gauges.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **No moving parts** — no float, lever, or wiper ⇒ high reliability and long MTBF.
2. **Immune to fuel slosh and aircraft attitude** — the probe measures the *integrated* fuel height, not a point level.
3. **Higher accuracy** (±1 – 2%) compared to float type (±5 – 8%).
4. **Compatible with digital avionics** — easily interfaced with EICAS / FMS via ARINC 429 bus.
*(Any four correct points ⇒ full 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of a **capacitance-type fuel quantity indicating system (FQIS)**. Discuss the role of the compensating reference capacitor and the AC bridge in eliminating fuel-type and slosh errors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cylindrical capacitance fuel probe has inner radius **1.5 cm**, outer radius **2.5 cm**, active length **25 cm**, and is filled with aviation kerosene of ε_r = **2.1** to a height of **15 cm**. The rest of the probe is in air. Calculate the **total capacitance** of the probe and the **percentage fuel level** indicated. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The capacitance FQIS consists of six functional blocks:

1. **Capacitance probe (tank unit):** A cylindrical coaxial capacitor installed in the fuel tank. As fuel height `h` varies, the net capacitance $C_{total} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} [(L-h) + \varepsilon_r h]$ changes linearly.
2. **Reference (compensating) capacitor:** A second identical probe placed inside a sealed capsule containing a sample of the *same* fuel batch. Because both probes see the *same* ε_r and *same* temperature, fuel-type and temperature errors cancel out in a **bridge differential measurement**.
3. **AC Wheatstone bridge (400 Hz):** Energised by the aircraft's 400 Hz bus. The probe arm and the reference arm form two opposite arms; the imbalance voltage at the detector diagonal is proportional to `(h/L)`.
4. **Null detector / AC amplifier:** A high-gain, phase-sensitive amplifier that converts the bridge imbalance into a DC voltage.
5. **Lineariser:** A non-linear network (resistor–diode string or digital lookup table) that compensates for the small non-linearity near empty/full conditions due to fringing fields at the probe ends.
6. **Indicator:** A D'Arsonval movement in older aircraft, or a digital LCD/EICAS display in modern aircraft, calibrated in **pounds (lb)** or **kilograms (kg)** of fuel.

**Why compensating capacitor?** It removes the effect of **fuel batch dielectric variation** (ε_r can vary from 2.05 to 2.20 between batches) and **temperature-induced ε_r drift** (dielectric constant decreases as temperature rises). Both probe and reference see these variations equally, so the bridge differential output is independent of them.

**Why AC bridge?** Capacitance can only be measured with an **alternating** signal; a DC bridge would saturate. The 400 Hz frequency is convenient because the aircraft already supplies 400 Hz AC.

*(Mark split: 2 marks diagram, 3 marks principle, 2 marks for compensation explanation.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $a = 0.015\ \text{m}$, $b = 0.025\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.25\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_r = 2.1$, $h = 0.15\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric factor.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.025}{0.015}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = \ln 1.6667 = 0.5108
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the constant K.**

$$
K = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{0.5108} = \frac{5.563 \times 10^{-11}}{0.5108} = 1.0893 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective length term.**

$$
(L - h) + \varepsilon_r h = (0.25 - 0.15) + 2.1 \times 0.15 = 0.10 + 0.315 = 0.415\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{total}$.**

$$
C_{total} = K \times 0.415 = 1.0893 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.415 = 4.521 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 45.21\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{empty}$ and $C_{full}$ for the percentage reading.**

$$
C_{empty} = K \times L = 1.0893 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.25 = 2.723 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 27.23\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
C_{full} = K \times \varepsilon_r L = 1.0893 \times 10^{-10} \times 2.1 \times 0.25 = 5.719 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \approx 57.19\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the % fuel level.**

$$
\%\text{Fuel} = \frac{45.21 - 27.23}{57.19 - 27.23} \times 100 = \frac{17.98}{29.96} \times 100 \approx 60.01\%
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $C_{total} \approx \mathbf{45.2\ \text{pF}}$
- Indicated fuel level = $\mathbf{60\%}$ *(matches the actual fill of 15/25 = 60%, confirming linearity.)*

*(Mark split: 1 mark setup, 2 marks K, 1 mark $C_{total}$, 1 mark $C_{empty}$, 1 mark $C_{full}$, 1 mark %.)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare **float-type** and **capacitance-type** fuel quantity gauges. Explain the construction, working, and limitations of each with neat diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of the following engine fuel instruments with diagrams:
    (i) **Turbine-type fuel flow meter** *(3.5 marks)*
    (ii) **Bourdon-tube fuel pressure indicator** *(3.5 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Float-type gauge:**
- A buoyant float rests on the fuel surface and is connected via a mechanical arm to the wiper of a **precision potentiometer** (or to a pneumatic pressure capsule in older systems).
- As the float rises or falls, the wiper slides along a wire-wound resistor, varying the output resistance.
- The resistance is converted to a proportional voltage (or pressure) and displayed on the cockpit indicator.
- **Limitations:** subject to fuel slosh, sensitive to aircraft attitude, has moving parts (wear), and accuracy is limited to ±5 – 8 %.

**Capacitance-type gauge:**
- A coaxial cylindrical probe acts as the dielectric sensor. As fuel level changes, the total capacitance of the probe changes linearly.
- The probe is part of an AC bridge; the imbalance is amplified and displayed.
- **Advantages:** no moving parts, immune to slosh (averaged measurement), high accuracy (±1 – 2 %), compatible with FMS.
- **Limitations:** needs AC excitation, requires a reference capacitor for fuel-type compensation, more complex electronics.

**Comparison table:**

| Aspect | Float-type | Capacitance-type |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor element | Float + potentiometer | Cylindrical capacitor |
| Output | Resistance | Capacitance (pF) |
| Accuracy | ±5 – 8 % | ±1 – 2 % |
| Slosh sensitivity | High | Low |
| Reliability | Lower | Higher |

*(Mark split: 2 marks float construction, 2 marks float limitations, 2 marks capacitance working, 1 mark comparison.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b)(i) — Turbine fuel flow meter:**

- A small **turbine rotor** with bladed vanes is mounted in the fuel line perpendicular to the flow direction.
- Fuel flow exerts a tangential force on the blades, spinning the rotor at a speed proportional to the **volumetric flow rate** $q_v$.
- A **magnetic pick-off coil** (or Hall-effect sensor) senses each blade passage and generates a pulse train with frequency $f = K_t \cdot q_v$.
- The pulse train is converted (F-to-V) into an analogue voltage proportional to **instantaneous fuel flow** and displayed on the FFM.
- A separate **integrator (totalizer)** accumulates the pulses over time to display **total fuel used**: $Q_{total} = \int_0^t q_m(\tau)\, d\tau$.

*(Mark split: 1 mark diagram, 1.5 marks construction, 1 mark working.)*

**Model Solution for (b)(ii) — Bourdon-tube fuel pressure indicator:**

- A curved metal (phosphor-bronze or beryllium-copper) tube of elliptical cross-section, called a **Bourdon tube**, is connected to the fuel line whose pressure is to be measured.
- When internal pressure increases, the tube tends to **straighten** (because the elliptical cross-section becomes more circular under pressure).
- This small tip displacement (≈ 2 – 3 mm at full scale) is amplified by a **link-and-lever mechanism** and transmitted to the pointer shaft, which rotates the needle on a calibrated scale.
- Pressure is read in **psi** or **bar**.
- The indicator is calibrated so that the pointer deflection is **linear** with pressure over the working range (e.g., 0 – 50 psi for a typical fuel system).

*(Mark split: 1 mark diagram, 1.5 marks construction, 1 mark working.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Most Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric factor `ln(b/a)`** — many students write the parallel-plate formula `$C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$` for a *cylindrical* probe. This is **wrong** and will cost 2 – 3 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong dielectric constant** — always state ε_r explicitly (≈ 2.1 for Jet A-1, ≈ 1.0 for air). Mixing up values gives wrong numerical answers.
> 3. **Not showing the master equation derivation** — for 7-mark derivations, KTU expects the **Gauss's-law → E-field → V → C** chain. Skipping steps is penalised.
> 4. **Drawing the float-type gauge with no float** — the *float* is the heart of the system; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — always end numerical answers with **pF** or **F** explicitly.
> 6. **Treating the AC bridge as a DC bridge** — capacitance can only be measured with AC; calling it a "Wheatstone bridge" without the "AC" prefix is incomplete.
> 7. **Confusing fuel flow with fuel quantity** — flow is *rate* (gal/hr, kg/s); quantity is *total* (gal, kg). Examiners love testing this distinction.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fuel Content Gauge = Level + Density + Volume** — modern FQIS measures **mass** (in kg or lb), not just volume, by combining level (capacitance) and density (permittivity) sensing.
- [x] **Two major types:** **Float-type** (mechanical, low accuracy) and **Capacitance-type** (electrical, high accuracy) — the latter is the industry standard.
- [x] **Capacitance probe geometry:** Coaxial cylinders with inner radius `a`, outer radius `b`, length `L`.
- [x] **Master equation:** $C_{total} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \left[ (L - h) + \varepsilon_r h \right]$
- [x] **Boundary values:** $C_{empty} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(b/a)}$ (ε_r = 1), $C_{full} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$
- [x] **Normalized level:** $\dfrac{h}{L} = \dfrac{C_{total} - C_{empty}}{C_{full} - C_{empty}}$ — proves **linearity** of the cylindrical probe.
- [x] **Fuel dielectric constant (Jet A-1):** ε_r ≈ **2.1**; air ε_r ≈ **1.0**.
- [x] **AC bridge** is mandatory — 400 Hz aircraft supply is used.
- [x] **Reference (compensating) capacitor** cancels fuel-batch and temperature-induced ε_r variations.
- [x] **Turbine flow meter:** rotor speed ∝ volumetric flow; pulse-train output.
- [x] **Bourdon-tube pressure indicator:** curved tube straightens under pressure → needle deflection.
- [x] **Totalizer:** integrator that sums fuel flow over time to give total fuel used.
- [x] **No programming snippets** — this is an aerospace instrumentation topic; use mathematical derivations and physical diagrams only.
- [x] **Remember the units:** Capacitance → **pF**; lengths → **m**; ε_r → **dimensionless**; flow → **kg/s** or **L/min**; pressure → **Pa** or **psi**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Vehicle Propulsion - Rocket Engines and Propellants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Space Vehicle Propulsion - Rocket Engines and Propellants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rocket Propulsion** is a class of jet propulsion in which the thrust is generated by the **ejection of high-velocity propellant mass** from the rear of the vehicle, requiring no external medium (no atmospheric oxygen) for combustion. It is the only practical means of propulsion capable of operating in the **vacuum of space**, as it carries both the **oxidizer** and the **fuel** onboard as part of its propellant system.

A **Rocket Engine** (or rocket motor) is the complete energy-conversion device that transforms the **chemical energy** of the propellant into the **kinetic energy** of a high-velocity exhaust jet. **Rocket Propellants** are the working substances — comprising a fuel and an oxidizer (or being a monopropellant) — that undergo **exothermic chemical reaction** to release the energy necessary for thrust generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The student must master (a) operating principles, (b) engine classifications (solid, liquid, hybrid, cold-gas, ion), (c) propellant classification and properties, (d) the thrust equation, (e) specific impulse ($I_{sp}$), and (f) the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation. These appear frequently as **14-mark derivations** and **3-mark short notes** in KTU university exams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Balloon Rocket"

Imagine blowing up a balloon and releasing it without tying the neck. The balloon shoots forward in the opposite direction of the escaping air. Three observations are crucial:

1. **No external medium is needed** — the balloon flies even in vacuum (in principle). The propulsion is *self-contained*.
2. **The faster the air escapes (higher exhaust velocity $v_e$), the greater the thrust.**
3. **The more mass ejected ($\dot{m}$), the greater the thrust.**

Mathematically, this is a direct application of **Newton's Third Law of Motion** (action–reaction) combined with the **Conservation of Momentum**. The rocket is essentially a controlled, sustained version of the balloon — burning high-energy propellants at extreme temperatures (often **> 3000 K** for hydrogen–oxygen engines) and accelerating the exhaust gases to velocities between **2,000 m/s** (solid propellants) and **4,500 m/s** (hydrogen–oxygen liquid engines).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants for Rocket Propulsion**
> - Standard gravity $g_0 = 9.80665 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - Universal gas constant $R_u = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
> - Characteristic chamber temperatures: Solid ≈ **2500–3500 K**, Liquid bipropellant ≈ **3000–3800 K**, Cryogenic LH₂/LOX ≈ **3500 K**
> - Typical exhaust velocity range: **2000–4500 m/s**

### 1.3 Why Rocket Propulsion? The Operational Necessity

Air-breathing engines (turbojets, ramjets, scramjets) cease to function above approximately **25–30 km altitude** because atmospheric oxygen becomes insufficient. For orbital insertion (> **200 km** altitude, > **7.8 km/s** velocity), **only rocket propulsion** can provide the necessary delta-V because the vehicle must carry its own oxidizer. This is the foundational reason rockets are indispensable for spaceflight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operational chain of a chemical rocket engine follows a strict sequence:

1. **Propellant Storage & Metering** — The fuel and oxidizer are stored in separate tanks (liquid engines) or as a pre-mixed grain (solid engines) and metered into the combustion chamber at a controlled mass-flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s).
2. **Injection & Atomization** — Injectors atomize the propellant into fine sprays to ensure rapid, complete mixing and efficient combustion.
3. **Combustion / Decomposition** — Exothermic chemical reaction raises the gas temperature to the **adiabatic flame temperature** ($T_c \approx 2500\text{–}3800 \, \text{K}$). The gases reach very high chamber pressure $P_c$ (typically **5–300 bar**).
4. **Nozzle Expansion** — The high-pressure, high-temperature gas is expanded through a **converging–diverging (de Laval) nozzle**, converting **thermal/pressure energy** into **directed kinetic energy**. At the nozzle exit plane, the gas reaches its peak axial velocity $v_e$.
5. **Thrust Generation** — The momentum change of the gas produces a reaction force on the combustion chamber walls, transmitted through the engine structure to the vehicle. This is the **thrust**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a De Laval Nozzle?**
> A simple converging nozzle can only accelerate a gas to *sonic* velocity (Mach 1). To extract additional energy in a rocket (where chamber pressure vastly exceeds ambient), a diverging section is required to **supersonically expand** the gas, dramatically increasing $v_e$ and thus thrust.

### 2.2 Classification of Rocket Engines

**By Physical State of Propellant:**

- **Solid Propellant Rocket Motor (SRM)** — propellant cast/molded as a grain; simple, storable, reliable, but not throttlable or restartable.
- **Liquid Propellant Rocket Engine (LRE)** — fuel and oxidizer stored as liquids; throttlable, restartable, higher $I_{sp}$, more complex.
- **Hybrid Rocket Engine** — solid fuel + liquid/gaseous oxidizer; combines reliability of solids with controllability of liquids.
- **Cryogenic Engine** — uses liquefied gases (e.g., **LH₂ at 20 K** and **LOX at 90 K**); highest performance, but boil-off losses.
- **Monopropellant Engine** — single storable liquid (e.g., hydrazine) decomposed by a catalyst; simple, low thrust, used for attitude control.

**By Energy Source (Non-chemical):**

- **Cold Gas Thruster** — stored inert gas (N₂, He); very low $I_{sp}$ (~70 s) but precise; used for spacecraft attitude control.
- **Electric / Ion Thruster** — xenon ions accelerated electrostatically; $I_{sp}$ up to **3000–5000 s**, very low thrust (mN).
- **Nuclear Thermal Rocket** — nuclear reactor heats hydrogen; $I_{sp}$ ≈ 800–1000 s.

### 2.3 Classification of Rocket Propellants

| Class | Description | Typical Examples | $I_{sp}$ Range (s) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Solid Double-Base** | Nitrocellulose + nitroglycerin | Smokeless powder | 180 – 220 |
| **Solid Composite** | Polymer binder + oxidizer (e.g., AP) + Al powder | HTPB/AP/Al (Space Shuttle SRB) | 240 – 270 |
| **Liquid Storable (Hypergolic)** | Self-igniting on contact | MMH + N₂O₄ (Aerozine-50) | 280 – 320 |
| **Liquid Cryogenic** | Liquefied gases, non-storable | LH₂ + LOX (Space Shuttle Main Engine) | 380 – 460 |
| **Liquid Non-cryogenic** | Storable liquids, non-cryogenic | RP-1 (kerosene) + LOX (Falcon 9 Merlin) | 280 – 340 |
| **Monopropellant** | Single liquid, catalyst-decomposed | Hydrazine (N₂H₄) | 220 – 240 |
| **Gas (Cold)** | Pressurized inert gas | Gaseous N₂ | 60 – 80 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid vs Liquid — Examiner's Favorite Contrast:**
> - **Solid** = simple, low cost, high reliability, long shelf-life, but **cannot be throttled, restarted, or tested before flight**.
> - **Liquid** = **throttleable, restartable, higher $I_{sp}$, testable**, but more complex, leak-prone, cryogenic handling required.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Critical)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Every formula below has appeared in KTU university exams. Memorize the **symbols, units, and the physical meaning** of each. Many 14-mark questions ask for derivations rooted in the **thrust equation** or the **Tsiolkovsky equation**.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Thrust Equation** (steady, 1-D) | $F = \dot{m} \, v_e + (p_e - p_a) A_e$ | $F$ [N], $\dot{m}$ [kg/s], $v_e$ [m/s], $p_e, p_a$ [Pa], $A_e$ [m²] |
| 2 | **Thrust in Vacuum** ($p_a = 0$) | $F_{vac} = \dot{m} \, v_e + p_e A_e$ | Used for space-rated engines |
| 3 | **Specific Impulse** | $I_{sp} = F / (\dot{m} \, g_0)$ | $I_{sp}$ [s], $g_0 = 9.80665$ m/s² |
| 4 | **Effective Exhaust Velocity** | $v_{e,\text{eff}} = F / \dot{m}$ | $v_{e,\text{eff}}$ [m/s] |
| 5 | **Ideal Exhaust Velocity** | $v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{R_u}{M} \cdot T_c \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{p_e}{p_c}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}$ | $\gamma$ = $c_p/c_v$, $M$ = molar mass, $T_c$ = chamber temp |
