The radius is $r_a = a(1 + e)$. - **Eccentricity ($e$):** A dimensionless number describing the shape of the orbit; $0 \le e < 1$ for ellipse, $e = 1$ for parabola, $e > 1$ for hyperbola. - **Semi-major axis ($a$):** Half the longest diameter of the elliptical orbit; determines the orbital energy and period. - **True anomaly ($\nu$):** The angle measured at the focus from perigee direction to the satellite's current position. - **Flight path angle ($\phi$):** The angle between the velocity vector and the local horizontal (perpendicular to radius vector). At perigee and apogee, $\phi = 0$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module 2 questions carry 14 marks. KTU uses **internal choice** — you must attempt EITHER Option A OR Option B in full. Each option has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks. ### **Part B - Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around Earth with perigee altitude $h_p = 500 \; \text{km}$ and apogee altitude $h_a = 1500 \; \text{km}$.** **(a) Determine the orbital period, semi-major axis, eccentricity, and semi-minor axis of the orbit. (7 Marks)** **(b) Compute the satellite's velocity at perigee, apogee, and at a true anomaly of $\nu = 90°$. (7 Marks)** **Given:** $R_\oplus = 6371 \; \text{km}$, $\mu_\oplus = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$. --- #### **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1:** Compute the perigee and apogee radii. $$r_p = R_\oplus + h_p = 6371 + 500 = 6871 \; \text{km} = 6.871 \times 10^6 \; \text{m}$$ $$r_a = R_\oplus + h_a = 6371 + 1500 = 7871 \; \text{km} = 7.871 \times 10^6 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the semi-major axis. $$a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2} = \frac{6871 + 7871}{2} = 7371 \; \text{km} = 7.371 \times 10^6 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the eccentricity. $$e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p} = \frac{7871 - 6871}{7871 + 6871} = \frac{1000}{14742} = 0.06784$$ **Step 4:** Compute the semi-minor axis. $$b = a \sqrt{1 - e^2} = 7371 \times \sqrt{1 - (0.06784)^2} = 7371 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.004602} = 7371 \times 0.99770 = 7354 \; \text{km}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the orbital period using Kepler's Third Law. $$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu_\oplus}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.371 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$ Compute $a^3$: $$a^3 = (7.371 \times 10^6)^3 = 7.371^3 \times 10^{18} = 400.36 \times 10^{18} = 4.0036 \times 10^{20} \; \text{m}^3$$ Compute $a^3 / \mu_\oplus$: $$\frac{a^3}{\mu_\oplus} = \frac{4.0036 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} = 1.0044 \times 10^{6} \; \text{s}^2$$ Take square root: $$\sqrt{1.0044 \times 10^6} = 1002.2 \; \text{s}$$ Multiply by $2\pi$: $$T = 2\pi \times 1002.2 = 6296.6 \; \text{s} \approx 104.94 \; \text{minutes}$$ **Results:** - $a = 7371 \; \text{km}$ - $e = 0.0678$ - $b = 7354 \; \text{km}$ - $T \approx 104.94 \; \text{min}$ **(7 marks awarded)** --- #### **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1:** Compute semi-latus rectum $p$. $$p = a(1 - e^2) = 7371 \times (1 - 0.004602) = 7371 \times 0.995398 = 7337.05 \; \text{km}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the specific angular momentum $h$. $$h = \sqrt{\mu_\oplus \cdot p} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.337 \times 10^6}$$ $$h = \sqrt{2.9244 \times 10^{21}} = 5.408 \times 10^{10} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$ **Step 3:** Compute velocity at perigee ($\nu = 0°$). At perigee, velocity is purely transverse, so $v_p = h / r_p$. $$v_p = \frac{h}{r_p} = \frac{5.408 \times 10^{10}}{6.871 \times 10^6} = 7870.6 \; \text{m/s} \approx 7.871 \; \text{km/s}$$ **Step 4:** Compute velocity at apogee ($\nu = 180°$). At apogee, $v_a = h / r_a$. $$v_a = \frac{h}{r_a} = \frac{5.408 \times 10^{10}}{7.871 \times 10^6} = 6871.5 \; \text{m/s} \approx 6.872 \; \text{km/s}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the radius at $\nu = 90°$. $$r_{90} = \frac{p}{1 + e \cos 90°} = \frac{p}{1 + 0} = p = 7337.05 \; \text{km}$$ **Step 6:** Use the vis-viva equation at $\nu = 90°$. $$v_{90} = \sqrt{\mu_\oplus \left( \frac{2}{r_{90}} - \frac{1}{a} \right)}$$ $$\frac{2}{r_{90}} = \frac{2}{7.337 \times 10^6} = 2.726 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{a} = \frac{1}{7.371 \times 10^6} = 1.357 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{2}{r_{90}} - \frac{1}{a} = (2.726 - 1.357) \times 10^{-7} = 1.369 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^{-1}$$ $$v_{90} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.369 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{5.457 \times 10^{7}} = 7387 \; \text{m/s} \approx 7.387 \; \text{km/s}$$ **Results:** - $v_p \approx 7.871 \; \text{km/s}$ - $v_a \approx 6.872 \; \text{km/s}$ - $v_{90°} \approx 7.387 \; \text{km/s}$ **(7 marks awarded)** --- ### **Part B - Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **(a) Starting from Newton's law of gravitation, derive the conic orbit equation and show that the orbit is an ellipse for bound orbits. State the conditions for circular, elliptical, parabolic, and hyperbolic trajectories. (7 Marks)** **(b) A geostationary satellite has an orbital period equal to the Earth's sidereal rotation period ($T = 86164.09 \; \text{s}$). Determine the radius of its circular orbit, its orbital speed, and its altitude above the Earth's surface. Comment on the significance of this orbit for communication satellites. (7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1:** Newton's law of gravitation: $F = \dfrac{\mu m}{r^2}$ acting radially inward. **Step 2:** In polar coordinates, the radial equation of motion is: $$\ddot{r} - r\dot{\nu}^2 = -\frac{\mu}{r^2}$$ **Step 3:** Conservation of angular momentum: $h = r^2 \dot{\nu}$ is constant. **Step 4:** Substitute $u = 1/r$ and use the chain rule with $\nu$ as independent variable. After substitution and simplification (as derived in Section 3.1): $$\frac{d^2 u}{d\nu^2} + u = \frac{\mu}{h^2}$$ **Step 5:** Solve the linear ODE with general solution $u(\nu) = A \cos(\nu - \nu_0) + \mu/h^2$. Setting $\nu_0 = 0$: $$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{\mu}{h^2}(1 + e \cos \nu) \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{h^2/\mu}{1 + e \cos \nu} = \frac{p}{1 + e \cos \nu}$$ **Step 6:** This is the conic orbit equation (Kepler's First Law). The shape depends on eccentricity $e$: | Eccentricity | Orbit Type | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | $e = 0$ | Circle | Constant radius; closed periodic | | $0 < e < 1$ | Ellipse | Bound closed orbit (satellites, planets) | | $e = 1$ | Parabola | Marginal escape trajectory ($v = v_{esc}$ exactly) | | $e > 1$ | Hyperbola | Unbound escape trajectory with excess speed | For bound orbits (spacecraft around a planet), $0 \le e < 1$ gives ellipses/circles. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks awarded)** --- #### **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1:** Apply Kepler's Third Law to a circular orbit ($T^2 = 4\pi^2 a^3 / \mu_\oplus$). Here $a = r$ for circular orbit. $$r^3 = \frac{\mu_\oplus T^2}{4\pi^2}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $T = 86164.09 \; \text{s}$ and $\mu_\oplus = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$. $$r^3 = \frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (86164.09)^2}{4\pi^2}$$ Compute $T^2$: $$T^2 = (86164.09)^2 = 7.4246 \times 10^{9} \; \text{s}^2$$ Compute $4\pi^2$: $$4\pi^2 = 39.478$$ Compute numerator: $$\mu_\oplus T^2 = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.4246 \times 10^{9} = 2.9594 \times 10^{24} \; \text{m}^5/\text{s}^2$$ Compute $r^3$: $$r^3 = \frac{2.9594 \times 10^{24}}{39.478} = 7.4962 \times 10^{22} \; \text{m}^3$$ Take cube root: $$r = (7.4962 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \; \text{m} = 42164 \; \text{km}$$ **Step 3:** Compute altitude above Earth's surface. $$h = r - R_\oplus = 42164 - 6371 = 35793 \; \text{km}$$ **Step 4:** Compute orbital speed (circular velocity at this radius). $$v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_\oplus}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{4.2164 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{9.454 \times 10^6} = 3075 \; \text{m/s} \approx 3.075 \; \text{km/s}$$ **Step 5:** Significance for communications satellites. - The orbital period equals Earth's sidereal rotation period (23 h 56 min 4 s), so the satellite appears **stationary** to a ground observer. - Three such satellites, spaced 120° apart in longitude, can provide **global coverage** (excluding polar regions). - The orbit is in the **equatorial plane** ($i = 0°$) for true geostationary behavior; inclined geosynchronous orbits show daily North-South oscillations. - Used by INSAT, GSAT, Intelsat, Inmarsat, and many other national and international communication networks. **(7 marks awarded)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing km and m mid-calculation is the #1 mistake. Always convert to a single system (preferably SI) at the start. Losing 1 mark. > 2. **Altitude vs. Radius Confusion:** Altitude $h$ is measured **above Earth's surface**, while radius $r$ is measured **from Earth's center**. KTU exam questions may use either — read carefully! Losing 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting $\nu_0 = 0$ Convention:** If the orbit equation is $r = p / (1 + e \cos(\nu - \nu_0))$, and you assume $\nu_0 = 0$ without checking whether periapsis is at $\nu = 0$, you will get the wrong $r$ for a given $\nu$. Losing 2 marks. > 4. **Wrong Sign of $e \cos \nu$ Term:** The conic equation is sometimes written $r = p/(1 - e \cos \nu)$ depending on the chosen convention. Pick one and stick with it. Losing 1 mark. > 5. **Confusing Mean Motion $n$ with True Anomaly Rate $\dot{\nu}$:** $n$ is the **average** angular velocity ($\sqrt{\mu/a^3}$), but instantaneous $\dot{\nu}$ varies along the orbit (highest at perigee, lowest at apogee, by Kepler's Second Law). Losing 1 mark. > 6. **Not Stating Assumptions:** Always explicitly state "assuming a two-body problem, neglecting perturbations (drag, $J_2$, third-body)" — this is what examiners expect for KTU-level solutions. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!** - ✅ **Kepler I** → Conic equation: $r = p / (1 + e \cos \nu)$. Ellipse if $0 \le e < 1$. Central body at one focus. - ✅ **Kepler II** → Areal velocity $dA/dt = h/2$ = constant. This is conservation of angular momentum in disguise. - ✅ **Kepler III** → $T^2 = 4\pi^2 a^3 / \mu$. Valid for any elliptical orbit; works for circular orbits too ($a = r$). - ✅ **Vis-Viva** → $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$. Use this for any speed-vs-position calculation. Most common KTU formula. - ✅ **Specific Energy** → $\mathcal{E} = -\mu/(2a)$. Bound orbit iff $\mathcal{E} < 0$. - ✅ **Perigee/Apogee** → $r_p = a(1 - e)$, $r_a = a(1 + e)$. - ✅ **Semi-latus Rectum** → $p = a(1 - e^2) = h^2/\mu$. - ✅ **Kepler's Equation** → $M = E - e \sin E$. Time-to-angle conversion. Numerical solution needed for any non-trivial orbit. - ✅ **Flight Path Angle** → $\tan \phi = (e \sin \nu)/(1 + e \cos \nu)$. Zero at perigee/apogee; maximum in between. - ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $\mu_\oplus = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$, $R_\oplus = 6371 \; \text{km}$. - ✅ **Geostationary orbit** is at $r = 42164 \; \text{km}$, $h = 35786 \; \text{km}$, $T = 86164 \; \text{s}$. - ✅ **Low Earth Orbit** is roughly $160$–$2000 \; \text{km}$ altitude, with $T \approx 88$–$127 \; \text{min}$. - ✅ Always state **assumptions** (two-body, no perturbations, point masses). - ✅ For numerical answers, **show all substitution steps**; partial credit is awarded for correct setup. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Space Vehicle Trajectories: Kepler's Laws**

topics.title: Basics of Orbital Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Orbital Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Orbital Mechanics** (also called *Astromynamics* or *Celestial Mechanics* in its broader astronomical sense) is the branch of aerospace engineering and applied physics that applies the principles of **Newtonian mechanics** and **Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation** to predict and describe the motion of spacecraft, satellites, rockets, and celestial bodies under the influence of gravitational forces.

In the context of **Aerospace Instrumentation (PEAET863, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, orbital mechanics forms the *theoretical backbone* against which on-board sensors (star trackers, sun sensors, gyroscopes, GPS receivers) generate attitude and position data. The course treats the **two-body problem** as the canonical model, where the motion of a spacecraft relative to a central attracting body (typically Earth) is governed entirely by the inverse-square gravitational field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module introduces orbital mechanics strictly from a *classical (Newtonian)* perspective. Relativistic corrections, three-body effects (e.g., Lagrange points), and atmospheric drag are treated as perturbations and not core syllabus material at this stage.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine you are standing on top of a tall mountain and you throw a cricket ball horizontally.

- **Throw it slowly** → it curves down and hits the ground a few hundred meters away.
- **Throw it faster** → it travels farther before landing.
- **Throw it *really* fast (about 7.9 km/s near Earth's surface)** → as the ball falls toward the curved Earth, the Earth's surface "curves away" beneath it at the same rate. The ball never hits the ground! It keeps falling *around* the Earth forever.

**That is exactly what an orbit is:** a state of *continuous free-fall* in which a body's tangential (forward) velocity is precisely balanced by the rate at which gravity pulls it inward relative to the curvature of the central body. The spacecraft is *always falling*; it simply keeps missing the Earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Misconception:** Astronauts in the International Space Station (ISS) are **NOT weightless** because they are "beyond gravity." Gravity at ISS altitude (~400 km) is still about **89%** of its surface value. They are weightless because they are in *continuous free-fall*, together with their spacecraft.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants
The following table lists the standard gravitational constants used throughout KTU board examinations. **Memorize these values** — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

| Symbol | Quantity | Numerical Value | Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:----------------|:-----|
| **G** | Universal Gravitational Constant | **6.674 × 10⁻¹¹** | N·m²·kg⁻² |
| **M_E** | Mass of Earth | **5.972 × 10²⁴** | kg |
| **μ_E** | Earth's Gravitational Parameter (G·M_E) | **3.986 × 10¹⁴** | m³·s⁻² |
| **R_E** | Mean Equatorial Radius of Earth | **6.371 × 10⁶** | m |
| **g₀** | Standard Surface Gravity | **9.80665** | m·s⁻² |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Shortcut:** Instead of computing G·M_E every time, KTU problems **always** give you μ directly, or expect you to use **μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²** for Earth-centered orbits. For lunar or solar problems, μ_moon and μ_sun are provided in the question.

### 1.4 Scope of the Two-Body Problem
The two-body problem restricts the analysis to **two point masses** interacting only via mutual gravitational attraction, with no other forces acting. Under this idealization:

- The total mechanical energy is conserved.
- The total angular momentum is conserved.
- The total linear momentum is conserved.
- The orbit lies in a **fixed inertial plane** (no out-of-plane motion).

These four conservation laws are the cornerstones upon which all subsequent derivations are built.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
Two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ separated by a center-to-center distance $r$ attract each other with a force directed along the line joining their centers:

$$F_{grav} = G \cdot \frac{m_1 \cdot m_2}{r^2}$$

The corresponding gravitational potential energy (defined as zero at infinity) is:

$$U_{grav} = -\frac{G \cdot m_1 \cdot m_2}{r}$$

The **negative sign** is crucial: it means the system is *bound*. To send a spacecraft to infinity requires adding positive energy to overcome this well.

### 2.2 Kepler's Three Laws of Planetary Motion
Derived empirically by **Johannes Kepler (1609–1619)** and later justified mathematically by Newton, these three laws fully describe closed-orbit motion under the inverse-square gravitational field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** A question asking *"State Kepler's laws and explain their physical significance"* appears almost every semester, typically as a 7-mark sub-part.

**Law 1 – The Law of Ellipses:**
> *Every planet (or spacecraft) moves in an elliptical orbit with the central attracting body located at one of the two foci.*

**Law 2 – The Law of Equal Areas:**
> *The line joining the central body to the orbiting body (the radius vector) sweeps out equal areas in equal times.*

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m} = \text{constant}$$

where $L$ is the orbital angular momentum and $m$ is the mass of the orbiting body.

**Physical significance:** The orbiting body moves **fastest at perigee (closest approach)** and **slowest at apogee (farthest point)**. This is why the visible motion of the Moon across the sky is non-uniform.

**Law 3 – The Law of Harmonies:**
> *The square of the orbital period of a body is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit.*

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} \cdot a^3$$

where $T$ is the orbital period, $a$ is the semi-major axis, and $\mu$ is the gravitational parameter of the central body.

### 2.3 The Six Classical Orbital Elements
A complete specification of an orbit's *size*, *shape*, *orientation*, and *current position* requires exactly six independent parameters, called the **Keplerian Orbital Elements**.

| # | Element | Symbol | Specifies | Typical Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:------:|:----------|:-------------|
| 1 | Semi-major axis | $a$ | Size of the orbit | km or m |
| 2 | Eccentricity | $e$ | Shape (0 = circle, <1 = ellipse) | dimensionless |
| 3 | Inclination | $i$ | Tilt of orbital plane w.r.t. reference (e.g., equator) | degrees (0° to 180°) |
| 4 | Right Ascension of Ascending Node | $\Omega$ (RAAN) | Where the orbit crosses equator going north | degrees (0° to 360°) |
| 5 | Argument of Perigee | $\omega$ | Where perigee lies within the orbital plane | degrees (0° to 360°) |
| 6 | True Anomaly | $\nu$ (or $f$) | Current position of the satellite in its orbit | degrees (0° to 360°) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why six?** A rigid body in 3D space has 6 degrees of freedom (3 for translation, 3 for rotation). The orbit is a one-dimensional curve embedded in 3D space, so it needs exactly 6 numbers to fully describe it.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains **every formula you must memorize** to solve the full range of KTU board problems in this module.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Use Case |
|:-:|:-------------|:-----------------------|:---------|
| 1 | Newton's Gravitation | $F = G m_1 m_2 / r^2$ | Force between two masses |
| 2 | Gravitational PE | $U = -G m_1 m_2 / r$ | Bound system energy |
| 3 | Circular Orbital Velocity | $v_c = \sqrt{\mu / r}$ | Speed in a circular orbit |
| 4 | Escape Velocity | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2\mu / r}$ | Speed to reach infinity |
| 5 | Vis-Viva Equation | $v^2 = \mu \left( \frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a} \right)$ | Speed at any point in orbit |
| 6 | Orbital Period (Kepler III) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{a^3 / \mu}$ | Time for one revolution |
| 7 | Specific Orbital Energy | $\varepsilon = -\mu / (2a)$ | Total energy per unit mass |
| 8 | Specific Angular Momentum | $h = \sqrt{\mu a (1-e^2)}$ | Angular momentum per unit mass |
| 9 | Periapsis Radius | $r_p = a(1-e)$ | Closest approach to focus |
| 10 | Apoapsis Radius | $r_a = a(1+e)$ | Farthest distance from focus |
| 11 | Semi-latus Rectum | $p = a(1-e^2) = h^2/\mu$ | Conic-section shape parameter |
| 12 | Flight Path Angle | $\tan\phi = \frac{e \sin\nu}{1 + e \cos\nu}$ | Angle between velocity and local horizontal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction:** Notice how absolute values like $\vert v \vert$ have been written using `\vert` in the LaTeX source to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your answer scripts, **never use the raw `|` character inside table cells** — it will be interpreted as a column separator and your table will collapse.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
Orbital mechanics underpins:

- **Mission planning** for satellites (LEO, MEO, GEO constellations such as GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, NavIC).
- **Launch vehicle trajectory design** — staging, parking orbits, and trans-lunar injection.
- **Rendezvous and docking** (e.g., SpaceX Crew Dragon approaching the ISS).
- **Interplanetary transfer** (Hohmann transfers, gravity assists like Voyager's "Grand Tour").
- **Space-debris tracking and collision avoidance** (Conjunction Analysis).

In aerospace instrumentation, orbital elements are continuously estimated on-board using **GPS receivers** and **ground-tracked radar**, then compared with the commanded state vector to fire **thrusters** for station-keeping.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **complete mathematical derivations** of the most important KTU board formulas. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly so that you can reproduce them in the examination hall for full marks.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Circular Orbital Velocity ($v_c$)

**Physical Setup:** Consider a satellite of mass $m$ in a *circular* orbit of radius $r$ around a central body of mass $M$. The only force acting on the satellite is gravitational attraction, which must exactly supply the centripetal force required to keep the satellite on its circular path.

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law for Circular Motion:**
For uniform circular motion, the net inward force equals mass × centripetal acceleration:

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a_c = m \cdot \frac{v^2}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Net Force:**
In the two-body problem with no drag or thrust, the only force is gravity. Using the symbol $\mu = G M$ for brevity:

$$F_{grav} = G \cdot \frac{M \cdot m}{r^2} = \frac{\mu \cdot m}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Cancel:**
Newton's law states that $F_{net} = F_{grav}$ (gravity *is* the net force). Setting them equal:

$$\frac{\mu \cdot m}{r^2} = m \cdot \frac{v^2}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the Common Factors:**
The satellite mass $m$ appears on both sides and cancels. Multiply both sides by $r$:

$$\frac{\mu}{r} = v^2$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Verification at Earth's Surface:**
Substituting $\mu_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² and $r = R_E = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m:

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{6.371 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{6.257 \times 10^7} \approx 7{,}910 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.91 \text{ km/s}$$

This is the famous **first cosmic velocity** (7.9 km/s).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Escape Velocity ($v_{esc}$)

**Physical Setup:** A projectile launched radially outward from a distance $r$ from a central body will *just* escape the gravitational field (i.e., reach $r \to \infty$ with zero residual speed) if it has a specific minimum launch speed.

**Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Energy:**
Total mechanical energy at launch (position $r$, speed $v_{esc}$) must equal total energy at infinity (potential = 0, kinetic = 0):

$$KE_{launch} + PE_{launch} = KE_{\infty} + PE_{\infty}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Expressions:**

$$\frac{1}{2} m v_{esc}^2 + \left( -\frac{\mu m}{r} \right) = \frac{1}{2} m (0)^2 + 0$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the Mass $m$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} v_{esc}^2 - \frac{\mu}{r} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_{esc}$:**

$$v_{esc}^2 = \frac{2\mu}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,v_{esc} = \sqrt{\frac{2\mu}{r}}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Important Ratio:**
Compare escape and circular velocities at the same radius:

$$\frac{v_{esc}}{v_c} = \frac{\sqrt{2\mu / r}}{\sqrt{\mu / r}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means **escape velocity is always $\sqrt{2}$ times the circular orbital velocity** at the same radius. At Earth's surface, $v_{esc} \approx 11.19$ km/s. No orbit can exist above this speed without thrust.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Vis-Viva Equation

The Vis-Viva (Latin for *"living force"*) equation gives the orbital speed at *any* point in an elliptical (or general conic) orbit in terms of the current radius $r$ and the semi-major axis $a$.

**Step 1 — Total Specific Energy at Any Point:**
The total specific mechanical energy (energy per unit mass) is the sum of kinetic and gravitational potential:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Total Specific Energy at Periapsis:**
At periapsis ($r = r_p = a(1-e)$), the velocity is purely tangential and given by $h / r_p$ (from angular momentum conservation):

$$v_p^2 = \frac{h^2}{r_p^2} = \frac{\mu a (1-e^2)}{a^2 (1-e)^2} = \frac{\mu (1+e)}{a(1-e)}$$

Therefore the specific energy at periapsis is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{v_p^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r_p} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Numerically:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} \left[ \frac{1+e}{2} - 1 \right] = \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} \cdot \frac{e-1}{2} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Astonishing result:** The total specific orbital energy depends *only* on the semi-major axis $a$, **not** on the eccentricity $e$ or the current position! This is the **fundamental energy equation of the two-body problem**.

**Step 4 — Equate Energies:**
Since energy is conserved throughout the orbit, the expression at any point must equal the expression at periapsis:

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v^2$:**

$$\frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{2a} = \mu \left( \frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{2a} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,v^2 = \mu \left( \frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a} \right)\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The Vis-Viva equation can be *re-derived in 10 seconds* in the exam by writing the energy conservation equation $\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$ and rearranging. **Memorize this intermediate form** — it earns full marks even if you forget the final rearrangement.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Kepler's Third Law ($T^2 \propto a^3$)

**Step 1 — Geometry of a Circle:**
For a circular orbit, the circumference is $2\pi r$ and the speed is constant. The period is therefore:

$$T = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \frac{2\pi r}{v_c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Circular Orbital Velocity:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi r}{\sqrt{\mu / r}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}$$

**Step 3 — Square Both Sides:**

$$\boxed{\,T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} \cdot a^3\,}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to Elliptical Orbits:**
For a general elliptical orbit, $r$ in the derivation is replaced by the semi-major axis $a$, and the result is identical in form. This is the mathematical statement of Kepler's Third Law.

**Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check for Earth:**
For a satellite in low Earth orbit with $a = 7.0 \times 10^6$ m (about 630 km altitude):

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.0 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} \approx 5{,}828 \text{ s} \approx 97.1 \text{ min}$$

This matches the well-known ~90-minute orbital period of the ISS. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Speed at Key Orbital Points

| Orbit Type | Periapsis Speed | Apoapsis Speed | Geometric Relation |
|:-----------|:----------------|:---------------|:-------------------|
| Circular ($e=0$) | $v_p = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ | $v_a = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ | $r_p = r_a = a$ |
| Elliptical ($0<e<1$) | $v_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}}$ | $v_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}}$ | $v_p \cdot r_p = v_a \cdot r_a = h$ |
| Parabolic ($e=1$) | $v_p = \sqrt{2\mu/r_p}$ | $v_a \to 0$ at $\infty$ | Just escapes |
| Hyperbolic ($e>1$) | $v_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}}$ | $v_a > 0$ at $\infty$ | Unbound, $a<0$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Keplerian Orbital Elements — Geometric Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical relationships** between the six classical orbital elements, the physical quantities they define, and the conservation laws they imply. This is the *block-level functional architecture* approach, which avoids the limitations of trying to render a true 3D conic in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Keplerian Orbit Specification] --> B[Size and Shape Block]
    A --> C[Orientation Block]
    A --> D[Position Block]

    B --> B1[Semi-Major Axis a]
    B --> B2[Eccentricity e]
    B1 --> F1[Energy = -mu over 2a]
    B1 --> F2[Period T from Kepler III]
    B2 --> F3[Periapsis r_p = a times 1 minus e]
    B2 --> F4[Apoapsis r_a = a times 1 plus e]

    C --> C1[Inclination i]
    C --> C2[RAAN Omega]
    C --> C3[Argument of Perigee omega]
    C1 --> G1[Defines orbital plane tilt]
    C2 --> G2[Defines ascending node longitude]
    C3 --> G3[Defines perigee within plane]

    D --> D1[True Anomaly nu]
    D1 --> H1[Current position of spacecraft]

    F1 --> L1[Conservation of Energy]
    F2 --> L1
    F2 --> L2[Conservation of Angular Momentum]
    F3 --> L2
    F4 --> L2
    G1 --> L3[Conservation of Linear Momentum in 3D]
    G2 --> L3
    G3 --> L3
```

### 4.2 Orbital Speed Variation Block Diagram (Vis-Viva Mapping)

This diagram shows how the **Vis-Viva speed** is logically determined at any point in the orbit by combining the radial distance $r$ with the semi-major axis $a$ through the gravitational parameter $\mu$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Current Radius r] --> C[Vis-Viva Calculator]
    B[Input: Semi-Major Axis a] --> C
    P[Input: Gravitational Parameter mu] --> C
    C --> D[Compute v squared = mu times 2 over r minus 1 over a]
    D --> E{Compare v with v_esc}
    E -->|v less than v_esc| F[Bound Elliptical Orbit]
    E -->|v equals v_esc| G[Parabolic Escape Trajectory]
    E -->|v greater than v_esc| H[Hyperbolic Unbound Trajectory]
    F --> I[Period T = 2 pi sqrt a cubed over mu]
    F --> J[Specific Energy epsilon = minus mu over 2a]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of the Two-Body Problem Solution

This block diagram outlines the **logical hierarchy** of how Newton's gravitation law leads, layer by layer, to the complete orbital description.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Newton Law of Gravitation F = G M m over r squared] --> L1
    L1[Two Body Assumption] --> L2[Conservation of Energy]
    L1 --> L3[Conservation of Angular Momentum]
    L1 --> L4[Conservation of Linear Momentum]
    L2 --> M1[Vis Viva Equation v squared = mu 2 over r minus 1 over a]
    L3 --> M2[Conic Section Equation r = p over 1 plus e cos nu]
    L3 --> M3[Kepler Second Law dA over dt = h over 2]
    L4 --> M4[Orbit lies in fixed inertial plane]
    M1 --> N1[Complete Orbital State Vector]
    M2 --> N1
    M3 --> N1
    M4 --> N1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *logical* concept, not a physical subsystem. The arrows indicate *mathematical dependence* — the destination block requires the source block to be evaluated first. Together, the four conservation laws and the conic section equation uniquely determine the orbit.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET863]`
**State Kepler's three laws of planetary motion and write the mathematical form of the third law.**

**Model Answer:**

*Kepler's three laws describe orbital motion under an inverse-square central force:*

1. **Law of Ellipses:** *Every planet moves in an elliptical orbit with the Sun at one focus. The same applies to any satellite orbiting a central body.*

2. **Law of Equal Areas:** *The radius vector from the central body to the orbiting body sweeps out equal areas in equal times.* Mathematically:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{h}{2} = \text{constant}$$

3. **Law of Harmonies:** *The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.* The mathematical form is:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET863]`
**What is meant by "two-body problem" in orbital mechanics? List the four quantities conserved in this problem.**

**Model Answer:**

The **two-body problem** is the idealized physical model in which two point masses interact solely through their mutual gravitational attraction, with no other forces (drag, thrust, radiation pressure) acting on the system. It is the foundational model for predicting satellite and planetary motion.

The four conserved quantities are:

1. **Total Mechanical Energy** $E$ (sum of kinetic and gravitational potential energy)
2. **Total Angular Momentum** $\vec{L}$ (vector quantity, both magnitude and direction)
3. **Total Linear Momentum** $\vec{p}$ (the center of mass moves at constant velocity)
4. **Laplace-Runge-Lenz Vector** $\vec{A}$ (advanced, rarely tested at KTU B.Tech level)

Conservation of angular momentum implies the orbit is planar (lies in a fixed plane).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 2 contributes a 14-mark question. The internal choice pattern (OR) is followed: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each main question has two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET863]`

**A (a)** Derive the expression for the **circular orbital velocity** of a satellite orbiting Earth at a height $h$ above the surface. Also calculate the orbital velocity for the **International Space Station** orbiting at an altitude of $h = 400$ km. Take $\mu_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² and $R_E = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set Up the Force Balance:**
For a satellite in a circular orbit of radius $r$, the gravitational force must provide the centripetal force:

$$\frac{\mu m}{r^2} = \frac{m v_c^2}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_c$:**

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

**Step 3 — Express $r$ in terms of altitude $h$:**

$$r = R_E + h$$

**Step 4 — Final General Formula:**

$$\boxed{\,v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{R_E + h}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Computation for the ISS:**

$$r = 6.371 \times 10^6 + 0.400 \times 10^6 = 6.771 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$$

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{6.771 \times 10^6}}$$

$$v_c = \sqrt{5.886 \times 10^7} = 7{,}672 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.67 \text{ km/s}$$

**Final Answer:** The ISS travels at approximately **7.67 km/s**, completing about **15.5 orbits per day**.

---

**A (b)** Starting from the principle of **conservation of energy**, derive the **Vis-Viva equation** for an orbiting body. Hence, calculate the speed of a satellite at apogee and perigee for an orbit with $a = 12{,}000$ km and $e = 0.3$. Use $\mu_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the Specific Energy at Any Point:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Energy:**
Since $\varepsilon$ is constant throughout the orbit, evaluate it at periapsis where the conic-section properties are simplest. At periapsis, $r_p = a(1-e)$ and the speed (purely tangential) is $v_p = h/r_p$. Using the conic equation $h^2 = \mu a(1-e^2)$:

$$v_p^2 = \frac{h^2}{r_p^2} = \frac{\mu a(1-e^2)}{a^2(1-e)^2} = \frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\varepsilon$ at Periapsis:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the General Expression:**

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v^2$:**

$$\boxed{\,v^2 = \mu \left( \frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a} \right)\,}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Periapsis and Apoapsis Radii:**

$$r_p = a(1-e) = 12{,}000 \times 0.7 = 8{,}400 \text{ km} = 8.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$$

$$r_a = a(1+e) = 12{,}000 \times 1.3 = 15{,}600 \text{ km} = 1.56 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Periapsis Speed:**

$$v_p^2 = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \left( \frac{2}{8.4 \times 10^6} - \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} \right)$$

$$v_p^2 = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \left( 2.381 \times 10^{-7} - 8.333 \times 10^{-8} \right)$$

$$v_p^2 = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.548 \times 10^{-7} = 6.170 \times 10^7$$

$$v_p = 7{,}855 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.86 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Apoapsis Speed (using $v_p r_p = v_a r_a$):**

$$v_a = v_p \cdot \frac{r_p}{r_a} = 7{,}855 \times \frac{8{,}400}{15{,}600} = 4{,}230 \text{ m/s} \approx 4.23 \text{ km/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $v_p \approx 7.86$ km/s, $v_a \approx 4.23$ km/s.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET863]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**B (a)** Define the **six classical Keplerian orbital elements** and state the physical significance of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The six classical Keplerian elements uniquely specify the size, shape, orientation, and current position of an orbit:

1. **Semi-Major Axis ($a$):** Defines the *size* of the orbit. Determines the orbital energy and period. For circular orbits, $a = r$.

2. **Eccentricity ($e$):** Defines the *shape*. Ranges from $e=0$ (perfect circle) through $0 < e < 1$ (ellipse) to $e = 1$ (parabola) and $e > 1$ (hyperbola).

3. **Inclination ($i$):** Defines the *tilt* of the orbital plane measured from a reference plane (usually the equator for Earth orbits). $i = 0°$ is equatorial; $i = 90°$ is polar; $i > 90°$ is retrograde.

4. **Right Ascension of Ascending Node ($\Omega$):** Defines the *longitude* at which the orbit crosses the equatorial plane going from south to north. Measured in the reference plane from a fixed direction (vernal equinox).

5. **Argument of Perigee ($\omega$):** Defines the *orientation* of the ellipse within the orbital plane, measured from the ascending node to the periapsis direction.

6. **True Anomaly ($\nu$):** Defines the *current position* of the satellite, measured from periapsis to the satellite's position along the orbit.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "S-S-I-O-A-P":** *Size, Shape, Inclination, Orientation, Arg of perigee, Position* — corresponds to $(a, e, i, \Omega, \omega, \nu)$.

---

**B (b)** A satellite is in a circular orbit at altitude $h = 1000$ km. Calculate:
(i) Its orbital velocity $v_c$
(ii) Its orbital period $T$
(iii) Its specific mechanical energy $\varepsilon$

Given: $\mu_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s², $R_E = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Orbital Radius:**

$$r = R_E + h = 6.371 \times 10^6 + 1.0 \times 10^6 = 7.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (i) — Orbital Velocity:**

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.371 \times 10^6}}$$

$$v_c = \sqrt{5.408 \times 10^7} = 7{,}354 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.35 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Orbital Period:**

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.371 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4.004 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{1.005 \times 10^6}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 1002.4 = 6{,}298 \text{ s} \approx 1.75 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 (iii) — Specific Mechanical Energy:**

For a circular orbit, $a = r$, so:

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{\mu}{2a} = -\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{2 \times 7.371 \times 10^6}$$

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{1.4742 \times 10^7} = -2.704 \times 10^7 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $v_c \approx 7.35$ km/s
- $T \approx 6{,}298$ s $\approx 1.75$ h
- $\varepsilon \approx -2.70 \times 10^7$ J/kg (the negative sign indicates a *bound* orbit)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units conversion:** When altitude is given in km, the radius must be in **metres** before substituting into the formula. Mixing km and m in the same expression is the #1 reason for wrong numerical answers.
>
> 2. **Using $R_E$ instead of $R_E + h$:** Many students compute $v_c$ at Earth's surface even when the problem specifies a particular altitude. Always check: the formula $v_c = \sqrt{\mu / r}$ uses the **orbital radius**, not the planetary radius.
>
> 3. **Sign error in Vis-Viva:** The equation is $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$. If $a < 0$ (hyperbolic orbit), do not panic — the formula remains valid. The resulting $v^2$ will be greater than $2\mu/r$, correctly indicating an unbound trajectory.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to write units:** KTU examiners *do* deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical answers. Always finish with **"$\approx 7.67$ km/s"** rather than just **"$7.67$"**.
>
> 5. **Confusing eccentricity values:** $e = 0$ is a *circle*, not $e = 1$. $e = 1$ is a *parabola*. $0 < e < 1$ is an *ellipse*. $e > 1$ is a *hyperbola*.
>
> 6. **Wrong semi-major axis:** For a circular orbit, $a = r$ (the radius), *not* $2r$. For an elliptical orbit, $a = (r_p + r_a)/2$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Orbital mechanics** is the application of Newton's laws + universal gravitation to predict spacecraft motion.
- The **two-body problem** assumes two point masses, gravitational force only, and no other forces.
- **Four conservation laws** are the foundation: energy, angular momentum, linear momentum, and the conic-plane constraint.
- **Kepler's 1st Law** → ellipses (with central body at one focus).
- **Kepler's 2nd Law** → equal areas in equal times → conservation of angular momentum.
- **Kepler's 3rd Law** → $T^2 = (4\pi^2 / \mu) \cdot a^3$ → relation between period and size.
- **Six orbital elements** → $a, e, i, \Omega, \omega, \nu$ fully specify the orbit.
- **Circular orbital velocity** → $v_c = \sqrt{\mu / r} \approx 7.9$ km/s at Earth's surface.
- **Escape velocity** → $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2\mu / r} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_c \approx 11.2$ km/s at Earth's surface.
- **Vis-Viva equation** → $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ — valid at *any* point in *any* conic orbit.
- **Specific orbital energy** → $\varepsilon = -\mu / (2a)$ — depends only on $a$, not on $e$ or position.
- **Periapsis/apoapsis** → $r_p = a(1-e)$, $r_a = a(1+e)$, and $v_p r_p = v_a r_a = h$.
- **Key constants for Earth** → $\mu_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s², $R_E = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m.
- **Practical tip:** Always state the energy conservation form $\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$ as your starting point in derivations — it earns 2–3 marks even before you finish algebra.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Guidance, Navigation and Avionics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Guidance, Navigation and Avionics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Guidance, Navigation and Control (GNC)** is the discipline of engineering concerned with the design, integration and operation of systems that determine the position, attitude and trajectory of a vehicle (aircraft, missile, spacecraft or UAV), and that steer it along a desired path to fulfil a mission objective. The term **Avionics** is the portmanteau of *aviation* and *electronics* and refers to all electronic systems installed aboard an aircraft or aerospace vehicle that perform flight-critical, mission-critical and passenger-comfort functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Guidance = the *command* function (where to go, what to do).
> Navigation = the *sense* function (where am I, what is my state?).
> Control = the *actuate* function (move control surfaces / thrust vector to follow guidance commands).
> Avionics = the *enabling electronic hardware/software* (sensors, computers, displays, buses, FMS) that implements GNC.

### 1.2 The Three Functional Pillars

| Pillar | Core Question | Primary Output | Key Subsystems |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Guidance** | "Where do I need to go?" | Desired trajectory / steering commands | Mission planner, waypoint generator, guidance law (PN, APN, ZEM) |
| **Navigation** | "Where am I, how am I oriented, how fast?" | Position, velocity, attitude (PVA) | INS, GPS, VOR/DME, ADIRS, air data system |
| **Control** | "How do I move the vehicle to follow the guidance?" | Actuator commands (deflections, throttle) | Autopilot, FBW, control laws, AFS |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (The "Taxi to the Airport" Analogy)

Imagine you are taking a taxi to a destination you have never visited before:

- **Navigation** corresponds to the *GPS on your phone* that continuously tells the driver: *"You are currently on MG Road, 2.4 km north of the destination, moving at 40 km/h east."* It answers "where am I?".
- **Guidance** corresponds to the *Google Maps route* that says: *"Take the second left, then continue straight for 3 km."* It answers "what should I do next?".
- **Control** corresponds to the *driver's hands and feet* — turning the steering wheel, pressing the accelerator, braking. It executes the guidance commands using the vehicle's actuators.
- **Avionics** is the *whole car dashboard* — the speedometer, fuel gauge, GPS screen, engine sensors — the electronic suite that gives the driver the information and means to act.

In a missile, the seeker head is the *eye*, the inertial measurement unit is the *inner ear*, the autopilot is the *brain-hands*, and the airframe plus fins are the *body*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Aerospace Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants):**
> - Standard sea-level gravity: **g = 9.80665 m/s²**
> - Standard sea-level pressure: **P₀ = 101325 Pa**
> - Earth rotational rate: **Ω = 7.292115 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s**
> - WGS-84 Earth semi-major axis: **a = 6378137.0 m**
> - WGS-84 flattening: **1/f = 298.257223563**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The GNC Functional Chain (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **State Estimation (Navigation)**
   The navigation filter (typically a Kalman filter or complementary filter) fuses measurements from heterogeneous sensors:
   - Inertial sensors (gyroscopes, accelerometers) — *high bandwidth, drift-prone*.
   - GNSS receivers (GPS / GLONASS / Galileo / IRNSS) — *drift-free, lower bandwidth, vulnerable to jamming*.
   - Air data sensors (pitot-static, AoA) — *barometric altitude, airspeed*.
   - Magnetic and optical aids.
   The output is the *best estimate* of position, velocity and attitude (PVA).

2. **Guidance Law Computation**
   The current state is compared to a *reference trajectory* (pre-planned waypoints or a continuously computed optimal path). A guidance law — e.g. **Proportional Navigation (PN)**, **Augmented PN**, **Pure Pursuit**, **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** — produces a commanded acceleration vector **a_c**.
   - PN law: **a_c = N · V_c · λ̇**, where N is the navigation constant (typically 3–5), V_c is closing velocity, λ̇ is the LOS rate.
   - LOS guidance for cruise: **a_c = N · V · (η − η_des)**, where η is the path-tangential heading error.

3. **Control Law Execution**
   The commanded acceleration is transformed into body-axis torque and thrust-vector demands. Classical / modern control (PID, LQR, H-infinity, sliding mode) drives actuators.

4. **Actuation**
   Hydraulic / electro-mechanical / electro-hydraulic actuators move control surfaces (aileron, elevator, rudder, canard, thrust-vectoring nozzle) to produce the commanded moments.

5. **Feedback Closure**
   The new sensor measurements (closing the loop) are fed back to step 1, completing the cycle at rates from **50 Hz** (commercial avionics) up to **1 kHz** (high-end missiles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the order matter?** The three functions form a *cascade* — a poor navigation estimate (e.g. a 100 m GPS error) cannot be corrected by even the best control law, because the guidance command itself becomes wrong. This is the classic **"GIGO"** (Garbage-In Garbage-Out) hierarchy of GNC.

### 2.2 Avionics Architecture Layers (Conceptual Stack)

| Layer | Function | Example Equipment | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Sensors** | Measure physical state | IMU, GPS antenna, pitot tube, radar altimeter, magnetometer | High |
| **L2 — Data Acquisition & Bus** | Acquire, format and transport signals | ARINC 429, ARINC 664 (AFDX), MIL-STD-1553B, CAN-aerospace | High |
| **L3 — Processing** | Compute navigation solution, guidance & control laws | FMS computer, FCC, mission computer, ADC | Medium |
| **L4 — Display & HMI** | Present information to crew | PFD, ND, MFD, HUD, EICAS, MFDS | Medium |
| **L5 — Actuation** | Drive control effectors | Autopilot servos, FBW actuators, thrust reversers | Medium |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory equations to remember for PEAET863 Module 2:**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{a}_c = N \cdot \vec{V}_c \times \vec{\dot{\lambda}}$ | **Proportional Navigation (PN) law** | $N$ = nav. constant (–); $V_c$ = closing velocity [m/s]; $\dot{\lambda}$ = LOS rate [rad/s] |
| 2 | $\ddot{\vec{r}}^{n} = \vec{C}_{b}^{n}\,\vec{f}^{b} - (2\,\vec{\Omega}_{ie}^{n} + \vec{\Omega}_{en}^{n}) \times \vec{v}^{n} + \vec{g}^{n}$ | **Inertial navigation (mechanisation)** | $\vec{f}^{b}$ = specific force [m/s²]; $\vec{C}_{b}^{n}$ = body→nav DCM; $\vec{\Omega}$ = Earth / transport-rate vectors |
| 3 | $R = c \cdot \tau \,/\, 2$ | **Radar / Radio ranging** | $R$ = slant range [m]; $c \approx 3\times10^{8}$ m/s; $\tau$ = two-way time delay [s] |
| 4 | $\dot{\psi}_{LOS} = \dfrac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R}$ | **Line-of-sight rate (pursuer–target)** | $V_T, V_M$ = target / missile speeds; $\eta$ = off-boresight angles |
| 5 | $\phi = \phi_0 + \int \omega\,dt$, $\theta = \int q\,dt$, $\psi = \int r\,dt$ | **Euler integration of attitude** | $p,q,r$ = body angular rates [rad/s] |
| 6 | $P = P_0\left(1 - \dfrac{L\,h}{T_0}\right)^{\tfrac{gM}{RL}}$ | **Barometric (ISA) altitude** | $L$ = lapse rate; $T_0$ = 288.15 K; $h$ = altitude [m] |
| 7 | $V_{TAS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,(p_{t} - p_{s})}{\rho}}$ | **True airspeed from pitot-static** | $p_t$ = total / $p_s$ = static pressure; $\rho$ = air density [kg/m³] |
| 8 | $a_{Coriolis} = 2\,\vec{\Omega}_{ie} \times \vec{v}^{n}$ | **Coriolis acceleration on INS** | $\vec{\Omega}_{ie}$ = Earth-rate vector; $\vec{v}$ = velocity |

> *Note:* In all KTU answer scripts, **always state units in the final numerical box** — the KTU board examiner explicitly deducts marks if units are missing.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Aerospace

- **Civil aviation**: GNC + avionics enable *Required Navigation Performance (RNP)* approaches down to **RNP 0.1** (≈ 0.1 NM accuracy), CAT-III autolands in zero visibility, and *Future Air Navigation System (FANS)* oceanic operations.
- **Military aircraft**: Terrain-Following/Terrain-Avoidance (TF/TA) radars coupled with autopilot keep fighters at **≤ 60 m AGL** at 900 km/h; fire-control radars feed PN guidance into missiles like the AIM-120 AMRAAM.
- **Launch vehicles**: Closed-loop guidance (e.g. *iterative guidance mode* / Q-law) corrects dispersions during ascent; GNC ensures payload placement within meters of the target orbit insertion point.
- **Spacecraft**: Star trackers, sun sensors and GNSS provide attitude + orbit determination; reaction wheels and thrusters execute the control half.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section works out the **most frequently asked derivation** in PEAET863 Module 2 — the classical *Proportional Navigation (PN) guidance law* — and the *Line-of-Sight (LOS) rate kinematics*. Both appear in nearly every KTU previous-year paper.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Proportional Navigation (PN) Guidance Law

**Statement:** In a pursuer–target engagement in 2-D, the commanded lateral acceleration of the pursuer is proportional to the line-of-sight (LOS) rate, $\dot{\lambda}$, and to the closing velocity, $V_c$.

**Step 1 — Define the engagement geometry.**

Let $M$ = missile position, $T$ = target position, $R$ = instantaneous range, $\lambda$ = LOS angle measured in the inertial frame.

$$
\vec{r}_{MT} = \vec{r}_T - \vec{r}_M, \qquad R = \vert \vec{r}_{MT} \vert, \qquad \lambda = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y_T - y_M}{x_T - x_M}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the position vector to get the relative velocity.**

$$
\dot{\vec{r}}_{MT} = \vec{V}_T - \vec{V}_M
$$

Decompose this velocity along the LOS ($u_R$) and perpendicular to the LOS ($u_\lambda$):

$$
\dot{\vec{r}}_{MT} = \dot{R}\,\hat{\imath}_R + R\,\dot{\lambda}\,\hat{\imath}_\lambda
$$

Equating components in the rotating LOS frame:

$$
\dot{R} = \vec{V}_T \cos(\eta_T) - \vec{V}_M \cos(\eta_M) = -V_c
$$

where the *closing velocity* is $V_c = -\dot{R}$ (positive when the range is decreasing), and the off-boresight angles are $\eta_T = \lambda - \psi_T$, $\eta_M = \lambda - \psi_M$.

**Step 3 — Take the time-derivative of the LOS angle to obtain the LOS rate.**

Differentiating $\lambda = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y_T - y_M}{x_T - x_M}\right)$:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \dfrac{(x_T - x_M)(\dot{y}_T - \dot{y}_M) - (y_T - y_M)(\dot{x}_T - \dot{x}_M)}{R^2}
$$

In the LOS-aligned polar form, this reduces to the standard textbook expression:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \dfrac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate again to get the angular acceleration of the LOS.**

$$
\ddot{\lambda} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left[\dfrac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R}\right]
$$

Under the standard assumption $V_T, V_M, \eta_T, \eta_M$ vary slowly (typical for tactical engagement times of a few seconds), the dominant term is:

$$
\ddot{\lambda} \approx -\dfrac{2\,\dot{R}}{R}\,\dot{\lambda} = \dfrac{2\,V_c}{R}\,\dot{\lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate the pursuer's lateral acceleration to the perpendicular component of the relative motion.**

The pursuer's lateral (normal-to-velocity) acceleration $a_M$ contributes, in the LOS frame, a centripetal-like term on $\dot{\lambda}$:

$$
a_M \cos(\eta_M) \approx R\,\ddot{\lambda} + 2\,\dot{R}\,\dot{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the PN law.**

The PN hypothesis is to *nullify* the LOS rate as fast as possible by demanding:

$$
a_{c} = N\,V_c\,\dot{\lambda}
$$

where $N$ is the *effective navigation ratio* (dimensionless), typically 3 ≤ N ≤ 5 for optimal homing performance against a non-maneuvering target.

**Step 7 — Verify by substitution.**

Inserting $a_c$ into the previous equation and using $\ddot{\lambda} \approx (2V_c/R)\dot{\lambda}$:

$$
N\,V_c\,\dot{\lambda}\cos(\eta_M) = 2\,V_c\,\dot{\lambda} + R\,\ddot{\lambda}
$$

For small $\eta_M$ (homing phase), $\cos(\eta_M) \approx 1$, and $\dot{\lambda}$ cancels, giving the design recommendation:

$$
N \;\ge\; 2 \quad \text{for stability} \qquad \text{and typically} \quad N = 3 \text{ to } 5
$$

**Hence, derived.** ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** PN tries to keep the LOS rate *zero* by rotating the missile velocity vector at a rate $N$ times faster than the LOS rotation. Larger $N$ → faster convergence, but more sensitivity to seeker noise.

### 3.2 Derivation of Radar Range Equation (Monostatic)

A frequently-asked 7-mark derivation. The classical **radar range equation** in free space:

$$
\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_t\,G^2\,\lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3\,R^4\,k\,T_s\,B\,L_s}
$$

**Step 1** — Transmitter radiated power density at range $R$ is $\dfrac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$.

**Step 2** — Target intercepts and re-radiates a power $P_t G \sigma / (4\pi R^2)$, where $\sigma$ is the *radar cross-section* [m²].

**Step 3** — The receiving antenna captures a fraction $A_e / (4\pi R^2)$, with effective aperture $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$.

**Step 4** — Received power becomes $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$. This is the fundamental **R⁻⁴ dependence** of radar, which is why long-range radars need huge antennas or high power.

**Step 5** — Receiver noise floor is $N = k T_s B$ (k = Boltzmann constant, $T_s$ = system noise temperature, $B$ = bandwidth), modified by system losses $L_s$. Hence the final SNR expression above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key result to remember:** Doubling the range requires a **16×** increase in transmitted power (or **6 dB** antenna gain, or a **4×** larger target RCS). KTU loves to set numerical substitution problems on this equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic GNC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensor1[IMU Gyros and Accel] --> navfilt
    sensor2[GNSS Receiver] --> navfilt
    sensor3[Air Data Computer] --> navfilt
    sensor4[Magnetometer and Star Tracker] --> navfilt
    navfilt[Navigation Filter Kalman] --> stateest
    stateest[State Estimate PVA]
    stateest --> guidcomp
    waypts[Mission Planner Waypoints] --> guidcomp
    guidcomp[Guidance Law PN or LOS] --> gcmd
    gcmd[Guidance Command Acc Vector] --> ctrlcomp
    stateest --> ctrlcomp
    ctrlcomp[Control Law PID or LQR] --> actcmd
    actcmd[Actuator Commands] --> act1
    actcmd --> act2
    actcmd --> act3
    act1[Aileron and Elevon] --> dyn
    act2[Rudder] --> dyn
    act3[Throttle and TVC] --> dyn
    dyn[Aerospace Vehicle Dynamics] --> sensor1
    dyn --> sensor2
    dyn --> sensor3
    dyn --> sensor4
    dyn --> pay[Payload Camera SAR Weapon]
```

> *Caption:* Closed-loop GNC chain. Note the **two feedback paths** — fast (inner) from sensors to navigation, and slow (outer) through the mission planner.

### 4.2 Avionics Architecture (Civil Transport, ARINC-653 / IMA Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1A[ADIRU] --> busA
    L1B[GPS MMR] --> busA
    L1C[Radar Alt] --> busA
    L1D[Air Data] --> busA
    busA[ARINC 664 AFDX Backbone] --> fmsc
    busA --> fccA
    busA --> fccB
    busA --> dmcA
    busA --> dmcB
    fmsc[FMS Computer] --> dmcA
    fccA[Primary FCC] --> act1
    fccB[Secondary FCC] --> act2
    dmcA[Display Mgmt Computer] --> pfd
    dmcB[Display Mgmt Computer] --> mfd
    dmcA --> hud[Head Up Display]
    act1[Actuator Channel A] --> sur[Aileron Elevator Spoiler]
    act2[Actuator Channel B] --> sur
    pfd[Primary Flight Display] --> pilot[Pilot]
    mfd[Multifunction Display] --> pilot
    hud --> pilot
    pilot --> stick[Control Column]
    stick --> fccA
    stick --> fccB
```

> *Caption:* Modern Integrated Modular Avionics (IMA) with dual-redundant Flight Control Computers (FCC) and Display Management Computers (DMC), as found on an Airbus A350 or Boeing 787.

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Avionics Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AV[Avionics Systems] --> COM[Communication]
    AV --> NAV[Navigation]
    AV --> SURV[Surveillance]
    AV --> FMS2[Flight Management]
    AV --> GFC[Guidance and Flight Control]
    AV --> MIS[Mission Avionics]
    AV --> HMI[Display and HMI]
    COM --> VHF[VHF and HF Radio]
    COM --> SAT[SATCOM]
    COM --> Datalink[ACARS CPDLC]
    NAV --> INS2[INS IRS]
    NAV --> GNSS2[GNSS GPS Galileo]
    NAV --> RADNAV[VOR DME ILS]
    SURV --> TCAS[TCAS]
    SURV --> ADSB[ADS B In and Out]
    SURV --> RADAR[Weather Radar]
    FMS2 --> FMC[FMC]
    FMS2 --> PERF[Performance DB]
    GFC --> AP[Autopilot]
    GFC --> FBW[Fly By Wire]
    GFC --> AFS[Auto Throttle]
    MIS --> FCR[Fire Control Radar]
    MIS --> EO[EO and IR Sensors]
    MIS --> EW[Electronic Warfare]
    HMI --> EFIS[EFIS PFD ND]
    HMI --> EICAS[EICAS]
    HMI --> HUD2[HUD SVS]
```

> *Caption:* Standard civil/military avionics taxonomy — heavily tested in KTU short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between **Guidance**, **Navigation** and **Control** in an aerospace vehicle. Give one example instrument for each function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Function | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Navigation** | Determines the vehicle's *current* position, velocity and attitude using sensors and filters. | Inertial Navigation System (INS) / GPS receiver |
| **Guidance** | Computes the *desired* trajectory and steering commands to reach a target. | Fire-control computer running a *Proportional Navigation* law |
| **Control** | Converts the guidance command into *actuator* deflections (aileron, elevator, throttle) to follow the path. | Autopilot with PID control law driving a hydraulic actuator |

*(1 mark each)*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is **avionics**? List any four major subsystems of a modern civil-aircraft avionics suite.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Avionics is the combined term for all **avi**ation **electronics** systems on board an aircraft, performing communication, navigation, surveillance, flight management, guidance/control and display functions.

Four major subsystems (½ mark each, plus 1 mark for definition):

1. **Communication system** — VHF/HF radio, SATCOM, ACARS/CPDLC datalink.
2. **Navigation system** — INS/IRS, GPS/MMR, VOR/DME/ILS.
3. **Flight Management System (FMS)** — FMC + performance database + autothrottle.
4. **Display & HMI** — EFIS (PFD, ND), EICAS, HUD, SVS.
   *(Acceptable alternatives: Surveillance — TCAS/ADS-B; Guidance & Flight Control — Autopilot / FBW.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2] — 7 marks**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an integrated **Guidance, Navigation and Control (GNC)** system for a tactical missile. Describe the role of each block.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A tactical missile GNC system consists of the following cascaded blocks (block diagram — 2 marks; explanation of each block — 5 × 1 mark):

1. **Seeker (sensor layer):** Detects the target (radar / IR / laser / TV). Provides LOS angle $\lambda$ and range $R$ — used by guidance.
2. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** Three gyros + three accelerometers. Gives body angular rates $(p,q,r)$ and specific force $\vec{f}^b$ — used by navigation.
3. **Navigation Filter (Kalman / EKF):** Fuses seeker + IMU + GPS (if present) to produce an *estimate* of the missile's position, velocity and attitude in the chosen navigation frame.
4. **Guidance Law (e.g. Proportional Navigation):** Computes the *commanded lateral acceleration* $a_c = N V_c \dot{\lambda}$ required to nullify the LOS rate.
5. **Autopilot / Control Law:** Converts $a_c$ into body-axis *rate / acceleration* demands and then into *fin deflection* commands.
6. **Actuators (electro-mechanical or electro-hydraulic):** Move the four control canards/fins.
7. **Airframe (controlled object):** The fin deflections produce aerodynamic moments and forces that change the missile's trajectory — closing the loop.

> **Common marking scheme (KTU):** 2 marks for block diagram; 1 mark each for 5 blocks explained (any 5). Maximum 7.

---

**Q3(b) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2] — 7 marks**
*Derive the **Proportional Navigation (PN) guidance law** for a 2-D pursuer–target engagement. State clearly the assumptions made.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Assume a *planar engagement* in the X-Y inertial plane with missile $M$ and target $T$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the relative range and LOS angle.

$$
R = \sqrt{(x_T-x_M)^2+(y_T-y_M)^2},\qquad \lambda = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y_T-y_M}{x_T-x_M}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate to obtain the LOS rate.

$$
\dot{\lambda} = \dfrac{V_T \sin(\eta_T) - V_M \sin(\eta_M)}{R}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate again to obtain $\ddot{\lambda} \approx (2V_c/R)\dot{\lambda}$, where $V_c = -\dot R$ is the closing speed.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Write the pursuer's lateral acceleration contribution: $a_M \approx R\,\ddot{\lambda} + 2\,\dot R\,\dot{\lambda}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Equate and solve for the missile acceleration, defining the *navigation constant* $N$:

$$
a_{c} = N\,V_c\,\dot{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the design condition $N \ge 2$ for stability, and $3 \le N \le 5$ as the *optimal* range.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** List the assumptions — (i) constant missile and target speeds, (ii) small off-boresight angle $\eta_M$, (iii) planar engagement, (iv) seeker measures $\lambda$ and $\dot{\lambda}$ without noise.

**Final box-statement:** *The PN guidance law demands a lateral missile acceleration proportional to the LOS rate and to the closing velocity, with a dimensionless gain N typically chosen between 3 and 5.*

---

**Question 3 — INTERNAL CHOICE — alternative selection (also for 14 marks total):**

**Q4(a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2] — 7 marks**
*Explain the **inertial navigation mechanisation equation** in the local-level north-east-down (NED) frame. List the major error sources of a strapdown INS.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The fundamental strapdown INS mechanisation in the NED frame (navigation frame 'n' = local NED) is (write the equation, 1 mark):

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{\vec{v}}^{n} = \vec{C}_{b}^{n}\,\vec{f}^{b} - (2\,\vec{\Omega}_{ie}^{n} + \vec{\Omega}_{en}^{n}) \times \vec{v}^{n} + \vec{g}^{n}\;}
$$

*Where:*
- $\vec{C}_{b}^{n}$ — direction-cosine matrix from body to navigation frame, updated by integrating body gyros: $\dot{\vec{C}}_{b}^{n} = \vec{C}_{b}^{n}\,[\vec{\omega}_{ib}^{b} \times]$.
- $\vec{f}^{b}$ — specific force measured by accelerometers [m/s²].
- $\vec{\Omega}_{ie}^{n}$ — Earth-rate vector projected into NED $[0, \Omega\cos\phi, -\Omega\sin\phi]^T$ where $\phi$ is latitude.
- $\vec{\Omega}_{en}^{n}$ — transport rate $= [v_E/(R_e+h), -v_N/(R_n+h), -v_E\tan\phi/(R_e+h)]^T$.
- $\vec{g}^{n}$ — local gravity vector $[0,0,g]^T$ (positive down).

**Error sources of strapdown INS (1 mark each, list any 5):**
1. Gyro bias drift (constant and random-walk).
2. Accelerometer bias and scale-factor error.
3. Initial alignment error (initial attitude uncertainty).
4. Computational / quantisation errors in the DCM update.
5. Vibration-induced rectification (g-sensitive drift).
6. Uncompensated Coriolis and centripetal accelerations.

**Closing (½ mark):** Pure INS drifts unbounded; in practice, **GNSS/INS integration** via Kalman filter is mandatory for long-endurance aerospace platforms.

---

**Q4(b) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2] — 7 marks**
*Derive the **radar range equation** for a monostatic pulsed radar. Explain the physical meaning of each term and the well-known $R^{-4}$ dependence.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The one-way power density at range $R$ from an isotropic radiator of power $P_t$ is $P_t/(4\pi R^2)$. With a directive antenna of gain $G$, the density becomes $P_t G/(4\pi R^2)$ (1 mark).

The target intercepts a portion of this density with effective area $\sigma$ (RCS) and re-radiates isotropically: scattered power = $P_t G \sigma / (4\pi R^2)$ (1 mark).

The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ captures a fraction $A_e/(4\pi R^2)$ of the re-radiated power (1 mark).

Therefore, the received echo power is (1 mark):

$$
P_r = \dfrac{P_t\,G^2\,\lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3\,R^4}
$$

Dividing by the receiver noise $N = k T_s B$ and including system losses $L_s$ gives the SNR form (1 mark):

$$
\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4\,k\,T_s\,B\,L_s}
$$

**Physical meaning of $R^{-4}$ (2 marks):**
- **$1/R^2$** — spherical spreading loss on the *transmit* path.
- **$1/R^2$** — spherical spreading of the *re-radiated* echo back to the radar.
- Combined → **$1/R^4$** round-trip two-way loss. This is why long-range radars need either very high power, very large antennas (high $G$), or very large targets ($\sigma$). **Doubling the range requires 16× the transmitted power** (or 12 dB more power-aperture product).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the *minus* sign in the closing velocity** (write $V_c = -\dot R$, *not* $V_c = \dot R$). ½–1 mark lost.
> 2. **Not stating units explicitly** in the final numerical answer. KTU board explicitly deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Omitting the body-to-nav DCM $\vec{C}_{b}^{n}$** in the INS mechanisation equation — students often write $f^b$ alone. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing *seeker* with *sensor* in the GNC block diagram** — the seeker is one *type* of sensor, not a separate block from IMU/GPS. Use the *layer* of "sensors" instead of drawing the seeker as the top block.
> 5. **Not listing assumptions before a derivation** — KTU valuation key reserves 1 mark for "assumptions stated clearly".
> 6. **In PN derivation, using $a = N V \dot\lambda$ directly without deriving it** — KTU treats the law as the *result*, not the *assumption*. The 7 marks are for the derivation, not the formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — PEAET863 Module 2**

- [x] **GNC vs. Avionics** — GNC is the *function*; Avionics is the *electronic hardware* that implements it.
- [x] **G = command, N = sense, C = actuate** (memorise this triad).
- [x] **PN law**: $a_c = N V_c \dot\lambda$; optimal $N = 3$–$5$; stability condition $N \ge 2$.
- [x] **INS mechanisation**: $\dot{\vec v}^n = \vec C_b^n \vec f^b - (2\vec\Omega_{ie}^n + \vec\Omega_{en}^n) \times \vec v^n + \vec g^n$ — *write from memory in the exam*.
- [x] **Coriolis term**: $2\vec\Omega_{ie}\times\vec v^n$ — always appears in Earth-fixed or local-level frames.
- [x] **Radar range equation**: $P_r \propto 1/R^4$ — *round-trip* two-way propagation.
- [x] **Avionics layers (5)**: Sensors → Bus → Processing → Display → Actuation.
- [x] **Common data buses**: ARINC 429 (civil, low speed), ARINC 664 / AFDX (civil, high speed), MIL-STD-1553B (military).
- [x] **Civil displays**: PFD + ND (EFIS), EICAS (engine), HUD, SVS.
- [x] **Key sensors**: IMU, GPS/MMR, pitot-static (TAS, baro alt), radar altimeter, magnetometer, star tracker, DME/VOR/ILS.
- [x] **Standard constants**: $g = 9.80665$ m/s², $c = 3\times10^8$ m/s, $\Omega = 7.2921\times10^{-5}$ rad/s, $a = 6378137$ m.
- [x] **Always state assumptions and units** in derivations.
- [x] **Block diagram** carries 2 of 7 marks in any 14-mark question — *draw it neatly with arrows* in the answer.
- [x] **PN vs LOS guidance**: PN is for *homing missiles*; LOS is for *cruise / waypoint following* in aircraft and UAVs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atmospheric Measurements - Standard Atmosphere
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Atmospheric Measurements - Standard Atmosphere # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Standard Atmosphere (SA)** is a hypothetical vertical distribution of atmospheric properties (temperature, pressure, density, and derived quantities such as speed of sound and viscosity) that closely approximates the real, year-averaged, mid-latitude atmosphere. It is defined by rigidly specified boundary conditions at sea level and piecewise linear temperature profiles in the vertical direction. For aerospace engineering calculations, the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)**, codified by **ISO 2533:1975** and adopted universally by **ICAO**, **FAA**, and **EASA**, is the canonical reference model. It provides a single deterministic environment against which aircraft performance, altimeter calibrations, wind-tunnel data reduction, and flight-control simulations are referenced. The fundamental sea-level reference constants (anchor point) of the ISA are: | Property | Symbol | Sea-Level Value (h = 0) | |----------|--------|--------------------------| | Temperature | $T_0$ | **288.15 K (15.00 °C)** | | Pressure | $P_0$ | **101,325 Pa (1013.25 hPa)** | | Density | $\rho_0$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g_0$ | **9.80665 m/s²** | | Specific gas constant (dry air) | $R$ | **287.05287 J/(kg·K)** | | Ratio of specific heats | $\gamma$ | **1.400** | | Speed of sound | $a_0$ | **340.294 m/s** | | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu_0$ | **1.7894 × 10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s)** | | Universal gas constant | $R^*$ | **8314.32 J/(kmol·K)** | | Molecular weight of air | $M$ | **28.9644 g/mol** | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — "The Atmospheric Blueprint":** > Imagine a tall office building (Earth's atmosphere) where every floor is assigned a *fixed, contractual* temperature, pressure, and "air weight" by the building's architect. The architect doesn't measure each floor dynamically — instead, he publishes a *standard tenant's manual* (the Standard Atmosphere) that says, "On Floor 0, the temperature is 15 °C; on Floor 11, the temperature is –56.5 °C; on Floor 20, hold the temperature constant…" > A pilot walking into this building can *predict* exactly how heavy the air will be, how fast sound will travel, and how his altimeter will read — even before he takes a single real measurement. This is precisely why the ISA exists: it is a **contract between reality and mathematics**, allowing sensors (altimeters, air-data computers, flight-management systems) to be calibrated against a universal truth. The atmosphere is divided into discrete **layers (regions)** separated by *pause* boundaries, each characterized by a constant **lapse rate** $L = -dT/dz$ (the rate at which temperature falls with altitude in K/km). When $L$ is positive, temperature *increases* with altitude (inversion layer); when $L$ is zero, the layer is *isothermal*. The seven canonical layers of the ISA model (up to ~86 km) are: | Region | Altitude Range (km) | Lapse Rate L (K/km) | Base Temperature (K) | Name of Upper Boundary | |--------|---------------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------------| | 0 | 0 – 11 | –6.5 | 288.15 | Tropopause | | 1 | 11 – 20 | 0.0 | 216.65 | (Stratopause 1) | | 2 | 20 – 32 | +1.0 | 216.65 | Stratopause | | 3 | 32 – 47 | –2.8 | 228.65 | (Stratopause 2) | | 4 | 47 – 51 | 0.0 | 270.65 | (Stratopause 3) | | 5 | 51 – 71 | –2.8 | 270.65 | Mesopause | | 6 | 71 – 84.852 | –1.0 | 214.65 | Upper boundary | > [!NOTE] > The **troposphere (0 – 11 km)** is the regime of all civil aviation; the **lower stratosphere (11 – 20 km)** is the regime of high-altitude cruise and supersonic transport. For most KTU-level problems and aerospace-instrumentation derivations, the *0 – 32 km envelope* is sufficient. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Governing Physics — Three Foundational Equations The ISA is constructed from the marriage of **three** fundamental physical laws. Each is unpacked below. ### (a) The Hydrostatic Equation A vertical column of air in static equilibrium yields a balance between the weight of the column and the pressure gradient: $$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho \, g$$ This says: as one ascends ($dz > 0$), pressure drops ($dP < 0$) because there is *less air* above. The proportionality factor is the *local weight density* $\rho g$. ### (b) The Ideal Gas Law Dry atmospheric air, for all practical flight altitudes, obeys the ideal-gas equation of state: $$P = \rho \, R \, T$$ where $R = R^*/M$ is the **specific gas constant for dry air**. This relates the three thermodynamic properties at every point. ### (c) The Lapse-Rate Definition The vertical temperature structure is *defined* by a piecewise linear lapse rate: $$L \equiv -\frac{dT}{dz} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T(z) = T_b - L \, (z - z_b)$$ where subscript $b$ denotes the base of the current layer. This is the *signature* of the Standard Atmosphere — it is **prescribed**, not derived. ## 2.2 Logical Step-Wise Development **Step 1 — Eliminate density.** Substitute $\rho = P/(RT)$ from (b) into (a): $$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P \, g}{R \, T}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables.** Multiply both sides by $dz/P$: $$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R} \cdot \frac{dz}{T}$$ **Step 3 — Introduce the temperature profile.** With $T(z) = T_b - L(z - z_b)$, $dz = -dT/L$, the integral becomes tractable. Two sub-cases follow: - **Case A — Isothermal layer ($L = 0$):** $T = T_b$ is constant. Direct integration gives the **barometric (isothermal) formula**: $$P(z) = P_b \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{g(z - z_b)}{R \, T_b}\right]$$ - **Case B — Gradient layer ($L \neq 0$):** Integration yields the **power-law pressure equation**: $$\frac{P}{P_b} = \left(\frac{T}{T_b}\right)^{\!g/(R \, L)} \quad ; \quad \frac{\rho}{\rho_b} = \left(\frac{T}{T_b}\right)^{\!g/(R \, L) - 1}$$ **Step 4 — Define the derived exponent.** The dimensionless exponent is denoted $n$: $$n \equiv \frac{g}{R \, L}$$ For the standard troposphere ($L = -0.0065$ K/m, $g = 9.80665$ m/s², $R = 287.05287$ J/kg·K): $$n = \frac{9.80665}{287.05287 \times (-0.0065)} \approx -5.2559$$ So in the troposphere, $\dfrac{P}{P_b} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_b}\right)^{-5.2559}$ and $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_b} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_b}\right)^{-6.2559}$. **Step 5 — Compute the speed of sound.** A critical derived quantity for high-speed aircraft and instrumentation (Mach meters): $$a = \sqrt{\gamma \, R \, T}$$ Since $T$ falls with altitude, $a$ *also* falls. At the tropopause (T = 216.65 K), $a \approx 295.07$ m/s — almost 14 % lower than at sea level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the ISA so important to aerospace *instrumentation*?** > Every pressure-based instrument — altimeter, airspeed indicator, Mach meter, vertical-speed indicator, and total-air-temperature probe — *inverts* the ISA relations to deduce a flight parameter from a static-pressure reading. Without a standard reference, the altimeter needle would have no defined zero, and airspeed would be undefined. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Exam-Critical Equations — memorize the structure, not just the symbols.** The constant $g$ is held at $9.80665$ m/s² throughout the ISA (gravity is *not* varied with altitude in the standard — a simplifying assumption for KTU-level work). | # | Equation | Form | Use / Region | |---|----------|------|----------------| | 1 | $T = T_b - L(z - z_b)$ | Linear | All gradient layers | | 2 | $P = \rho R T$ | Ideal gas | Everywhere | | 3 | $dP/dz = -\rho g$ | Hydrostatic | Everywhere | | 4 | $P = P_b \exp[-g(z-z_b)/(R T_b)]$ | Barometric | Isothermal layers ($L=0$) | | 5 | $P/P_b = (T/T_b)^{g/(RL)}$ | Power law | Gradient layers ($L \neq 0$) | | 6 | $\rho/\rho_b = (T/T_b)^{g/(RL) - 1}$ | Power law | Gradient layers ($L \neq 0$) | | 7 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound | Everywhere | | 8 | $H_p = R T / g$ | Pressure scale height | Diagnostic | | 9 | $P = P_0 (1 - L z / T_0)^{g/(RL)}$ | Explicit (troposphere) | $0 \le z \le 11$ km | | 10 | $\Delta T_{ISA} = T_{actual} - T_{ISA}$ | Temperature offset | ISA-deviation problems | ### Typical Numerical Values at Key Altitudes (ISA Reference) | Altitude z (km) | T (K) | P (hPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | a (m/s) | |------------------|--------|----------|------------------|----------| | 0 | 288.15 | 1013.25 | 1.225 | 340.29 | | 1 | 281.65 | 898.76 | 1.1116 | 336.43 | | 5 | 255.65 | 540.20 | 0.7364 | 320.55 | | 10 | 223.15 | 264.99 | 0.4135 | 299.46 | | 11 (Tropopause) | 216.65 | 226.32 | 0.3639 | 295.07 | | 15 | 216.65 | 120.45 | 0.1947 | 295.07 | | 20 | 216.65 | 54.74 | 0.0889 | 295.07 | | 25 | 221.65 | 25.49 | 0.0401 | 297.95 | | 32 (Stratopause) | 228.65 | 8.68 | 0.0132 | 302.61 | > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake to avoid in KTU answers:** In the lower stratosphere (11 – 20 km), the temperature is *constant* (T = 216.65 K). Many students erroneously continue the –6.5 K/km gradient beyond 11 km. This is a top frequent mark-losing error.
