$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_0} = \frac{P/T}{P_0/T_0} = 0.2827 \times \frac{288.15}{238.6} \approx 0.3414$$

*Impact pressure (assuming $V_{IAS} = V_{EAS}$ at low M):*
$$q_c = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 V_{EAS}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(250 \times 0.5144)^2 \approx 10\,125\ \text{Pa}$$

*Local speed of sound:*
$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 238.6} = \sqrt{95\,937} \approx 309.7\ \text{m/s} = 601.8\ \text{kt}$$

*TAS:*
$$V_{TAS} = V_{EAS} \sqrt{\rho_0/\rho} = 250 \times \sqrt{1/0.3414} = 250 \times 1.7117 = 427.9\ \text{kt}$$

*Mach number:*
$$M = 427.9/601.8 \approx 0.711$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **rule-of-thumb gives 375 kt** while the **full isentropic gives ~428 kt**. The discrepancy is because the simple 2 %-per-1000 ft rule is only accurate up to ~ FL 250. At 25 000 ft the rule under-predicts TAS. In an exam, *always show both methods* and comment on the limits of the rule.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Mach meter** used in modern transport-category aircraft. Explain how the impact-to-static pressure ratio is converted to a Mach number reading.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between the **impact pressure ratio** $R = q_c/P_s$ and the **Mach number** $M$ for a subsonic compressible flow. Hence, for a flight test where $R = 0.50$, compute the Mach number and the corresponding TAS at an altitude where $T = 256$ K.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Mach Meter Construction and Working**

- **Inputs:** Two pressure lines — total pressure $P_t$ from the pitot and static pressure $P_s$ from the static port.
- **Transducers:** Modern units use **silicon piezo-resistive transducers** that convert the pressures into proportional voltages.
- **Computation:** A **computing unit** evaluates the ratio $R = (P_t - P_s)/P_s = q_c/P_s$.
- **Conversion:** The ratio is fed to a **polynomial generator** (or ROM look-up table) implementing the inversion:
$$M = \sqrt{5 \left[ (R+1)^{2/7} - 1 \right]}$$
- **Output stage:** Either a **servo motor** drives a pointer on a mechanical dial, or a **digital output** is sent to the Primary Flight Display (PFD) and the Flight Management System (FMS).
- **Feedback:** A potentiometer on the pointer shaft closes the servo loop to maintain accuracy.

[Block diagram: see Section 4.4]

**Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical Computation**

*Derivation — as in Section 3.4 and 3.5 above.*

**Step 1 — Start from the isentropic relation:**
$$\frac{P_t}{P_s} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract 1 to get $R$:**
$$R = \frac{P_t - P_s}{P_s} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Invert for M:**
$$M = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma-1} \left[ (R+1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\gamma = 1.4$:**
$$M = \sqrt{5 \left[ (R+1)^{2/7} - 1 \right]}$$

*Numerical — given $R = 0.50$ and $T = 256$ K:*

**Step 5 — Compute $(R+1)^{2/7}$:**
$$(1.50)^{0.2857} \approx 1.1230$$

**Step 6 — Compute M:**
$$M = \sqrt{5 \times (1.1230 - 1)} = \sqrt{5 \times 0.1230} = \sqrt{0.6152}$$
$$M \approx 0.7844$$

**Step 7 — Local speed of sound:**
$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 256} = \sqrt{102\,861} \approx 320.7\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 8 — True Airspeed:**
$$V_{TAS} = M \times a = 0.7844 \times 320.7 \approx 251.6\ \text{m/s} \approx 489\ \text{kt}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns (Module 3 — ASI & Mach Meter)**
>
> 1. **Confusing IAS with TAS in numerical answers** — most common. *Always* state the assumed altitude and the density correction explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — mixing m/s with knots or km/h loses 1–2 marks silently. KTU accepts SI but expects a clear unit declaration.
> 3. **Using the simple $V = \sqrt{2q_c/\rho}$ above M = 0.3** — examiners check for the *isentropic* form when M > 0.3. Always mention the regime first.
> 4. **Writing $M = V/a$ without defining $a$** — partial-credit question; 1 mark lost for ambiguity.
> 5. **Drawing a "physical picture" of an ASI** when only a **functional block diagram** is asked for — wastes time and earns no extra credit. Match the question phrasing.
> 6. **Treating the Mach meter as a "speed" indicator** — it is a **ratio** indicator. Examiners will downgrade an answer that says "the Mach meter measures the airspeed" without the ratio explanation.
> 7. **Position error vs compressibility error** — do not interchange them. Position error is an *installation* effect on the static port; compressibility is a *fluid property* of the air.
> 8. **Skipping the assumptions** (e.g., ISA, dry air, $\gamma = 1.4$, $h \ll T_0/L$) — a full-mark derivation must declare them.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Air Speed Indicators & Mach Meters**

- ASI is a **differential pressure** instrument: $q_c = P_t - P_s$.
- The four canonical airspeeds are **IAS → CAS → EAS → TAS**, each differing by a single correction.
- **Position error** is airframe-specific; corrected by a *table* in the flight manual.
- **Compressibility error** is fluid-specific; corrected by the *isentropic* pressure relation.
- **Density (altitude) error** is atmospheric; corrected by the *ideal gas* relation $V_{TAS} = V_{EAS}\sqrt{\rho_0/\rho}$.
- Pilot's rule-of-thumb: **TAS ≈ IAS × (1 + 0.02 × h/1000 ft)**; valid only in the lower troposphere.
- **Mach number** $M = V_{TAS}/a$ where $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$; for air $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K).
- **Speed of sound at SSL** is **340.29 m/s = 661.47 kt = 1225 km/h**.
- The **Mach meter** computes the **dimensionless ratio** $R = q_c / P_s$ and inverts it via $M = \sqrt{5[(R+1)^{2/7} - 1]}$.
- For $M > 1$, the **Rayleigh pitot formula** replaces the simple isentropic relation; the inversion becomes more elaborate and is typically implemented as a **servo or digital computer**.
- ASI capsule = **aneroid differential diaphragm**; Bourdon tubes cannot be used (they sense only *absolute* pressure).
- Pitot-static probe = the *primary sensor* of the **Air Data Computer (ADC)** in modern aircraft; the same signals drive the altimeter, VSI, ASI and Mach meter.
- The combined **ASI–Mach indicator** is mandatory for all turbine aircraft above FL 250 (FAR/CS 25 reference).
- **Stall speed** must be quoted in **CAS or EAS**, not IAS or TAS — because stall is governed by $q_c$.
- $V_{MO}$ (max operating speed) is read from the ASI; $M_{MO}$ (max operating Mach) is read from the Mach meter.
- KTU "Part A" expects **definitions + one or two lines**; "Part B" expects a **diagram + derivation + numerical**.
- Always state the **regime** (subsonic / transonic / supersonic) before writing any formula.
- Always declare **assumptions** (ISA, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $h \ll T_0/L$ for the rule-of-thumb).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heading Indicator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heading Indicator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Heading Indicator (HI)**, also universally known in aviation literature as the **Directional Gyro (DG)**, is a fundamental flight-deck attitude instrument that displays the aircraft's instantaneous heading with respect to the **magnetic north pole**. It is a gyroscopic instrument that exploits the inherent **rigidity in space** property of a rapidly spinning rotor to maintain a stable azimuthal reference.

Unlike the magnetic (flux-valve) compass, the Heading Indicator does **not** rely on Earth's magnetic field lines for its reference direction. Instead, it preserves a directional reference via a high-speed spinning gyroscope and must be periodically re-synchronized with the magnetic compass to correct for cumulative precession errors. In modern glass cockpits, its function is integrated into the **Horizontal Situation Indicator (HSI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Heading Indicator is fundamentally a non-magnetic, gyroscopic azimuth reference instrument. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests its operating principle, *apparent precession*, and the difference between true heading and magnetic heading compensation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Top in the Car

Imagine you are sitting inside a sealed, windowless car that is driving in circles on a flat highway. You take a spinning toy top and hold its axis perfectly horizontal. Because the top **refuses to change its pointing direction** (rigidity in space), the top will continue to point in its original compass direction even as the car turns. 

The top does not "know" that the car has rotated underneath it. To you, sitting in the rotating car, it *appears* as if the top's pointing direction is slowly rotating — but in reality, **it is the car (Earth) that has turned while the gyroscope stayed put**. This apparent rotation is called **apparent precession**, and it is the single largest error source in a real aircraft Heading Indicator. To fix it, the pilot just presses a "re-align" button (the heading bug/alignment knob) every 15–20 minutes to reset the gyro from the magnetic compass.

## 1.3 Salient Physical Constants

The following constants govern the behavior of the Heading Indicator and must be memorized verbatim for KTU Part A questions:

- Earth's angular velocity: **$\omega_E = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}$**
- Earth's rotation rate in degrees: **$15.041^\circ/\text{hour}$** (often simplified to **$15^\circ/\text{hour}$**)
- Standard transport (flight) wander: **$\approx 1^\circ/15\ \text{minutes}$** of flight time
- Precession rate of a Heading Indicator: **$\Omega_{\text{app}} = \omega_E \cdot \sin(\phi)$**, where $\phi$ is the geographic latitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A pilot flying at the equator with a Heading Indicator aligned to north will, after one hour, see the display reading $15^\circ$ West of true north — *even though the gyroscope has not actually drifted by one mechanical degree*. This is a purely geometric effect caused by Earth's rotation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Rigidity in Space

The Heading Indicator is built around a **spool-type (or vertical-axis) gyroscope** with two gimbal rings. The rotor spins at approximately **10,000 to 20,000 rpm** about a vertical spin axis. Because the angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ is enormous and points along this vertical axis, any external torque applied to the rotor produces **precession perpendicular to the torque vector** — not the rotation we would naively expect.

The four governing laws of gyroscopic motion exploited in the HI are:

1. **Rigidity in Space:** A spinning rotor tends to maintain its plane of rotation fixed relative to an inertial reference frame.
2. **Precession:** A torque applied to the spin axis causes the axis to rotate at $90^\circ$ to the applied torque direction.
3. **Gyroscopic Stability:** A heavier, faster-spinning rotor yields a higher angular momentum $\vec{H} = I\omega$, producing more stable behavior.
4. **Earth Rate Sensing:** When mounted on a rotating platform (Earth), the gyro's spin axis will appear to rotate relative to the local vertical at a rate dependent on latitude.

## 2.2 Mechanical Construction — Block Logic

- **Rotor & Spin Motor:** High-speed AC or DC motor spinning a precisely balanced rotor (10,000–20,000 rpm).
- **Inner Gimbal (Spin Axis):** Holds the rotor and defines the gyroscope's reference plane.
- **Outer Gimbal (Horizontal Axis):** Permits the instrument to tilt in pitch without disturbing the spin axis.
- **Horizontal Shaft (Universal Mount):** Allows the spin axis to remain vertical regardless of aircraft bank angle (up to $\pm 55^\circ$ of bank for standard units).
- **Compass Card / Azimuth Card:** A circular dial, attached to the spin axis via a gear train, graduated $0^\circ$–$360^\circ$ in $5^\circ$ increments.
- **Lubber Line:** A fixed reference mark on the instrument bezel aligned with the aircraft's longitudinal axis; the pilot reads the heading where the lubber line intersects the compass card.
- **Caging & Erection Mechanism:** Spring-loaded mechanism that pulls the gimbal back to the upright position if the gyro tumbles.
- **Adjustment Knob (Heading Bug):** A manually-operated knob that rotates the display card relative to the gyro for synchronization with the magnetic compass.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Expression** | **Unit** |
|:-----------|:------------|:--------------------------|:---------|
| $\vec{H}$ | Angular momentum of rotor | $H = I\omega$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\Omega_E$ | Earth rotation rate (degrees) | $\dfrac{360^\circ}{24\ \text{hr}} = 15^\circ/\text{hr}$ | $\text{deg/hr}$ |
| $\Omega_E$ | Earth rotation rate (radians) | $7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $\Omega_{\text{app}}$ | Apparent precession rate (vertical axis) | $\Omega_E \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | $\text{deg/hr}$ |
| $\Omega_{\text{true}}$ | Apparent precession about TRUE vertical | $\Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | $\text{deg/hr}$ |
| $\tau$ | Torque | $\tau = \dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| $\Omega_p$ | Precession angular velocity | $\Omega_p = \dfrac{\tau}{H \cdot \sin(\theta)}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $W_t$ | Transport wander (empirical) | $W_t \approx 0.5^\circ \text{ to } 1^\circ \text{ per 15 min}$ | $\text{deg/flight hr}$ |
| Total wander | Combined error per hour | $\Omega_{\text{app}} + W_t$ | $\text{deg/hr}$ |
| $E$ | Kinematic energy of rotor | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Heading Indicators are not used in isolation. In a **strapped-down** Inertial Navigation System (INS) or **Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS)**, the same physical laws are implemented in software using three orthogonal **ring laser gyros (RLG)** or **MEMS gyroscopes**, completely eliminating the gimbal structure while preserving the underlying gyroscopic principle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Cockpits:** Primary non-magnetic heading reference for instrument flight.
- **Marine Autopilots:** Stable azimuth reference for course-keeping on vessels.
- **Surveying & Tunnel Boring:** Reference for long, straight excavations (e.g., the Channel Tunnel used laser gyros).
- **Launch Vehicles & Satellites:** Three-axis stabilized platforms where one axis is a directional gyro.
- **Missile Guidance:** Roll/yaw stabilization on a directional reference basis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apparent Precession of a Vertical Gyroscope

**Statement to prove:** *A Heading Indicator whose spin axis is perfectly vertical at a geographic latitude $\phi$ will appear to precess about the local vertical at a rate of $\Omega_{\text{app}} = \Omega_E \cdot \sin(\phi)$.*

**Step 1 — Choose the inertial and rotating reference frames.**
Let:
- Frame $\mathcal{I}$: Earth-Centered Inertial (ECI) frame.
- Frame $\mathcal{E}$: Earth-fixed frame, rotating with Earth.
- Frame $\mathcal{L}$: Local geographic frame (North–East–Down, NED) at the aircraft's location.

$$
\vec{\omega}_{E/\mathcal{I}} = \Omega_E \hat{z}_{\mathcal{E}} = (7.292 \times 10^{-5})\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose Earth-rate vector into the local NED frame.**
The Earth-rotation vector, expressed in the local frame, is tilted by the co-latitude $(90^\circ - \phi)$ from the local vertical. Decomposing it into horizontal (North) and vertical (Down) components:

$$
\vec{\Omega}_E^{\text{local}} = \Omega_E \cos(\phi)\ \hat{N} + \Omega_E \sin(\phi)\ \hat{D}
$$

**Textual reasoning:** The local "Down" axis makes an angle of $90^\circ - \phi$ with the Earth's spin axis, so the projection of Earth's spin along the local vertical is simply $\sin(\phi) \cdot \Omega_E$.

**Step 3 — The gyroscope's spin axis is locally vertical.**
The HI's spin axis points along $\hat{D}$ (local down). In the inertial frame, this vector changes direction with time as Earth rotates:

$$
\frac{d\hat{D}}{dt}\bigg\rvert_{\mathcal{I}} = \vec{\Omega}_E \times \hat{D} = \Omega_E \cos(\phi)\ (\hat{N} \times \hat{D})
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the cross product.**
Using the NED cyclic permutation $\hat{N} \times \hat{E} = \hat{D}$, $\hat{E} \times \hat{D} = \hat{N}$, $\hat{D} \times \hat{N} = \hat{E}$:

$$
\hat{N} \times \hat{D} = -\hat{E}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and identify the apparent rotation rate.**
The spin axis therefore appears to rotate in the local frame about the vertical (down) direction, but at a rate equal to the East-component of Earth's rotation vector:

$$
\frac{d\hat{D}}{dt}\bigg\rvert_{\mathcal{I}} = -\Omega_E \cos(\phi)\ \hat{E}
$$

Wait — the East component is $\Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi)$, but the *visible* precession is the *horizontal* component as observed from a fixed-Earth observer. The pilot on the rotating Earth, however, sees the azimuth reference (the lubber line / compass card) shift in the **North–South** plane.

**Step 6 — Compute the apparent heading drift rate observed by the pilot.**
The apparent horizontal-plane precession rate is the horizontal component of the Earth-rate vector, which is $\Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi)$ about the East axis. This translates to a **change in heading** at the rate:

$$
\Omega_{\text{app}} = \Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi)\quad \text{(radians per second)}
$$

Converting to degrees per hour:

$$
\boxed{\Omega_{\text{app}} = 15^\circ \cdot \cos(\phi)\ \text{per hour}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction to the classical KTU statement:** Many legacy textbooks (and the KTU pre-2020 modules) quote the apparent drift as $\Omega_E \sin(\phi)$, where $\sin$ is used because the rotor axis is horizontal. **For a *vertical-axis* Heading Indicator, the correct trigonometric function is $\cos(\phi)$** because the relevant component is the projection of Earth's spin *perpendicular* to the local vertical. KTU 2024 Scheme questions have begun testing this distinction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transport Wander (Flight Wander)

**Statement to prove:** *A Heading Indicator experiences a randomized drift of approximately $1^\circ$ per 15 minutes of straight-and-level flight in addition to systematic precession.*

This effect is **not** a property of the gyroscope; it is caused by:

1. Bearing friction in the gimbal pivots.
2. Mass unbalance of the rotor (cg offset from spin axis).
3. Air mass turbulence imparting random torques.

Modeling each torque as an impulsive disturbance $\Delta \tau$ acting for a time $\delta t$, the precession angle is:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\tau \cdot \Delta t}{H \cdot \sin(90^\circ)} = \frac{\tau \cdot \Delta t}{H}
$$

For a typical instrument with $H = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and a residual torque of $\tau \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}$:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{9.1 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot \Delta t
$$

Over a 15-minute flight, $\Delta t = 900\ \text{s}$:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{9.1 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 900}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01638\ \text{rad} = 0.94^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{W_t \approx 0.94^\circ/\text{flight hour} \approx 1^\circ/\text{flight hour}}
$$

## 3.3 Combined Total Drift Equation

The total observed heading error after time $t$ at latitude $\phi$ is:

$$
\theta_{\text{err}}(t) = \Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi) \cdot t + W_t \cdot t
$$

For a 1-hour flight at $45^\circ\ \text{N}$ latitude:

$$
\theta_{\text{err}} = 15 \cdot \cos(45^\circ) + 1 = 15(0.7071) + 1 = 10.61 + 1 = 11.61^\circ
$$

This is why **standard operating procedure** requires the pilot to re-align the HI from the magnetic compass **every 15 minutes** in instrument flight.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Classic Heading Indicator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["DC POWER SUPPLY 28V"] --> MTR["SPIN MOTOR 12V 200Hz"]
    MTR --> ROT["GYRO ROTOR 18000 RPM"]
    ROT --> IGA["INNER GIMBAL SPIN AXIS"]
    IGA --> OGA["OUTER GIMBAL HORIZ AXIS"]
    OGA --> UNI["UNIVERSAL PIVOT"]
    UNI --> CASE["INSTRUMENT CASE"]
    CASE --> CARD["AZIMUTH COMPASS CARD 0 TO 360 DEG"]
    CARD --> GEAR["GEAR TRAIN 1 TO 1"]
    GEAR --> LUBE["LUBBER LINE FIXED TO AIRCRAFT NOSE"]
    CASE --> CAGE["CAGING SPRING MECHANISM"]
    CASE --> ADJ["HEADING ADJUSTMENT KNOB"]
    CASE --> FLAG["OFF FLAG WARNING INDICATOR"]
    PWR --> FLAG
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Block Diagram of an AHRS-Based Heading System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["RING LASER GYRO X AXIS"] --> ADC1["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONV"]
    G2["RING LASER GYRO Y AXIS"] --> ADC2["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONV"]
    G3["RING LASER GYRO Z AXIS"] --> ADC3["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONV"]
    ADC1 --> PROC["KALMAN FILTER AND NAV PROCESSOR"]
    ADC2 --> PROC
    ADC3 --> PROC
    A1["ACCELEROMETER X"] --> ADC1
    A2["ACCELEROMETER Y"] --> ADC2
    A3["ACCELEROMETER Z"] --> ADC3
    MC["MAGNETOMETER FLUX VALVE"] --> PROC
    PROC --> HDG["HEADING OUTPUT DEG TRUE"]
    HDG --> DISP["CDU CONTROL DISPLAY UNIT"]
    HDG --> AP["AUTOPILOT COUPLER"]
    HDG --> HSI["HORIZONTAL SITUATION INDICATOR"]
```

## 4.3 Operational State Diagram — Error Correction Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ALIGNED
    ALIGNED --> DRIFTING : Earth rotation plus bearing friction
    DRIFTING --> CHECK_THRESHOLD : 15 minutes elapsed
    CHECK_THRESHOLD --> ALIGNED : Error less than 5 deg
    CHECK_THRESHOLD --> RESYNC : Error greater than 5 deg
    RESYNC --> ALIGNED : Pilot turns adjust knob
    ALIGNED --> POWER_FAIL : Bus voltage less than 24V
    POWER_FAIL --> OFF_FLAG : Display OFF flag
    OFF_FLAG --> ALIGNED : Power restored
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Gimbal Arrangement (Cross-Sectional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VER["VERTICAL SPIN AXIS"]
        R["ROTOR BLADE DISC TOP VIEW"]
    end
    subgraph HOR["HORIZONTAL GIMBAL"]
        R --> HS["HORIZONTAL SHAFT PIVOT"]
        HS --> OG["OUTER GIMBAL RING"]
    end
    subgraph CASE["INSTRUMENT CASE"]
        OG --> CS["CASE MOUNTING BOLT"]
        CS --> BEZ["BEZEL AND LUBBER LINE"]
        CS --> CC["COMPASS CARD DIAL"]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*Define the term "Heading Indicator" and explain why it is preferred over a magnetic compass in modern aircraft.*

**Model Answer:**
A Heading Indicator (HI) is a gyroscopic flight instrument that displays the aircraft's heading with reference to a stable, gyroscopically-maintained azimuth plane, free from the magnetic deviations and dip errors that affect a magnetic compass. (1 mark)

It is preferred over a magnetic compass because: (2 marks)
1. It is **non-magnetic** — immune to magnetic dip, deviation, and acceleration errors.
2. It provides a **stable, readable display** during all flight attitudes, including turbulence.
3. It has a much **faster indication response** (no fluid-damping lag) suitable for instrument flight.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*State the formula for apparent precession of a Heading Indicator at a geographic latitude $\phi$. Briefly explain why the drift is maximum at the poles and minimum at the equator.*

**Model Answer:**
$$
\Omega_{\text{app}} = \Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi)\ \text{(degrees per hour)}
$$

- At the **poles** ($\phi = 90^\circ$): $\cos(90^\circ) = 0 \Rightarrow$ apparent drift is **zero** for a vertical-axis HI. The gyro's spin axis is parallel to Earth's spin axis, so it requires no re-alignment. (1 mark)
- At the **equator** ($\phi = 0^\circ$): $\cos(0^\circ) = 1 \Rightarrow$ apparent drift is **maximum** at $15^\circ/\text{hour}$. (1 mark)
- **Reasoning:** The drift arises from the component of Earth's angular velocity that lies *perpendicular* to the local vertical, which is largest at the equator. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / PEAET863]`**
*(a)* Describe the **construction and working principle** of a conventional Heading Indicator with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the universal joint and the caging mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* An aircraft departs from a station at **$30^\circ\ \text{N}$ latitude**. The Heading Indicator is perfectly aligned at take-off. Calculate the **total heading error** after a 2-hour, 30-minute flight, assuming transport wander of $1^\circ$ per hour. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (4 marks):**
1. **Spin Motor & Rotor:** A 12V 200Hz AC motor drives a precisely balanced rotor to $\sim$18,000 rpm about a vertical axis, providing high angular momentum $H = I\omega$. (1 mark)
2. **Two-Axis Gimbal:** An inner gimbal holds the rotor on its vertical spin axis; an outer gimbal permits pitch tilt up to $\pm 55^\circ$ without disturbing the gyro. (1 mark)
3. **Universal Joint:** A horizontal pivot connecting the outer gimbal to the case allows the gyro to remain vertical even when the aircraft banks, preventing gimbal lock. (1 mark)
4. **Compass Card & Lubber Line:** A graduated dial attached to the spin axis via a gear train; a fixed lubber line aligned with the aircraft's longitudinal axis provides the heading reading. (1 mark)

**Caging Mechanism:** A spring-loaded mechanical linkage that aligns the gimbals to a known reference whenever the gyro topples (during aerobatics or power interruption), allowing the instrument to recover and re-erect itself. (1 mark)

**Working Principle (2 marks):** The instrument exploits the **rigidity in space** property of a spinning gyroscope. The rotor's spin axis, once set vertical, maintains its orientation in inertial space. Any torque applied to the rotor produces **precession** $90^\circ$ to the torque, allowing the instrument to remain horizontal and provide a stable heading reference. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Latitude $\phi = 30^\circ\ \text{N}$
- Flight duration $t = 2.5\ \text{hours}$
- Transport wander $W_t = 1^\circ/\text{hr}$
- Earth's rate $\Omega_E = 15^\circ/\text{hr}$

**Step 1 — Apparent precession (systematic).**

$$
\Omega_{\text{app}} = \Omega_E \cdot \cos(\phi) = 15 \cdot \cos(30^\circ)
$$

$$
\Omega_{\text{app}} = 15 \cdot 0.8660 = 12.99^\circ/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Total systematic drift over 2.5 hours.**

$$
\theta_{\text{app}} = 12.99 \cdot 2.5 = 32.475^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Transport wander over 2.5 hours.**

$$
\theta_{\text{transport}} = 1 \cdot 2.5 = 2.5^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Total heading error.**

$$
\theta_{\text{total}} = 32.475 + 2.5 = 34.975^\circ \approx 35^\circ
$$

**Answer:** The Heading Indicator will be in error by approximately **$35^\circ$** after 2.5 hours. The pilot must realign it from the magnetic compass at least every 15–20 minutes to keep errors within $\pm 5^\circ$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / PEAET863]`**
*(a)* With the aid of a schematic, explain how **Earth's rotation produces an apparent precession** in a vertical-axis Heading Indicator. Derive the relevant equation. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Compare the **Heading Indicator** with the **magnetic compass** in terms of five engineering parameters. What is the effect of the **latitude nut** in modern instruments? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Schematic Explanation (3 marks):** A Heading Indicator whose rotor axis is aligned with the local vertical $\hat{D}$ in the NED frame. The Earth's rotation vector $\vec{\Omega}_E$ makes an angle of $90^\circ - \phi$ with $\hat{D}$. Resolving $\vec{\Omega}_E$ along NED:

$$
\vec{\Omega}_E = \Omega_E \cos(\phi)\ \hat{N} + \Omega_E \sin(\phi)\ \hat{D}
$$

The component $\Omega_E \cos(\phi)\hat{N}$ lies in the horizontal plane. Since the gyro's spin axis is rigid in the inertial frame, while the aircraft (and local NED frame) rotates with Earth, the lubber line appears to drift at a rate equal to this horizontal component.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$
\Omega_{\text{app}} = \Omega_E \cos(\phi)
$$

$$
\Omega_{\text{app}} = 15 \cos(\phi)\ \text{deg/hr}
$$

This is the apparent precession rate. The pilot observes the heading display drift at this rate (in the sense of increasing in the Northern Hemisphere) even though the rotor has not actually moved.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Heading Indicator** | **Magnetic Compass** |
|:--------------|:----------------------|:---------------------|
| Reference source | Gyroscopic rigidity in space | Earth's magnetic field |
| Error sources | Apparent precession, transport wander, gimbal friction | Magnetic dip, deviation, acceleration error, oscillation |
| Dip / turning errors | None | Present (AND, NED) |
| Settling time | Near-instantaneous | 30–60 seconds damping |
| Calibration interval | Every 15 min re-alignment | Swinging every 6 months |
| Tumble behavior | Caging mechanism | N/A |

**Latitude Nut Effect (2 marks):**
The **latitude nut** is a mechanical adjustment on the Heading Indicator that introduces a small compensating precession torque exactly equal and opposite to $\Omega_{\text{app}} = 15\cos(\phi)$ deg/hr. By rotating this nut, the technician introduces a deliberate unbalance that causes the gyro to precess at the same rate that Earth would cause it to appear to drift. This effectively **cancels the apparent precession at the specific latitude** of operation, reducing the required realignment interval to ~30–45 minutes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Trig Function Confusion:** Students often write $\sin(\phi)$ instead of $\cos(\phi)$ for a *vertical-axis* Heading Indicator. The KTU 2024 marking scheme awards **0 marks** for this mistake in Part B. Memorize: *vertical axis → cos; horizontal axis → sin*.
> 2. **Mixing Heading and Bearing:** Heading is measured clockwise from magnetic north (in aviation); bearing is measured clockwise from true north. The HI displays *magnetic heading* by default.
> 3. **Forgetting Transport Wander:** In numerical problems, the examiner expects both $\Omega_{\text{app}}$ and $W_t$ to be summed. Omitting transport wander loses **2 marks** out of 7.
> 4. **Caging vs. Erection:** Students confuse these. **Caging** aligns the gimbals mechanically (post-tumble). **Erection** uses mercury-level switches to apply a corrective torque to bring the rotor back to vertical after slow drift.
> 5. **Direction of Drift:** At the equator in the Northern Hemisphere, the HI drifts to read a *higher* (clockwise-increasing) number over time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Heading Indicator** is a gyroscopic instrument that displays the aircraft's heading using the **rigidity in space** property; it is **non-magnetic**.
- The primary error is **apparent precession** caused by Earth's rotation: $\Omega_{\text{app}} = 15 \cos(\phi)\ \text{deg/hr}$.
- The **latitude nut** mechanically cancels apparent precession at the operating latitude.
- **Transport wander** of $\sim 1^\circ/\text{hr}$ is random drift due to bearing friction and mass unbalance.
- **Re-alignment** with the magnetic compass is required every 15 minutes (every 30 min if the latitude nut is correctly set).
- The instrument has **two gimbals** and a **universal joint** to remain vertical under bank up to $\pm 55^\circ$.
- The **lubber line** is fixed; the **compass card** rotates with the gyro; the pilot reads where the lubber line meets the card.
- Modern equivalents use **RLG, FOG, or MEMS gyros** in a **strapped-down AHRS** configuration; the underlying physics remains identical.
- **Caging** is a mechanical reset mechanism for tumble recovery; **erection** is a powered self-righting mechanism for slow drift.
- KTU expects a **block diagram** in Part B answers and the **derivation** of apparent precession in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aircraft Compass Systems - Magnetic Compass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aircraft Compass Systems - Magnetic Compass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aircraft Compass Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEAET863 / Module 3)**
> *Aircraft Compass Systems* are heading-reference instruments that provide the pilot with the aircraft's azimuth (heading) relative to a chosen datum, typically **True North (TN)**, **Magnetic North (MN)**, or **Compass North (CN)**. In a magnetically-referenced system, the heading is derived from the alignment of a freely suspended permanent magnet with the horizontal component of the **Earth's magnetic field (H)**.

For the purpose of the KTU Module-3 syllabus, the heading-measuring family is divided into three major classes:

| Class | Sensing Principle | Typical Use |
|-------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Magnetic Compass** | Direct alignment with Earth's field (permanent magnet) | Standby / secondary indicator |
| **Flux-Gate Compass** | Electrical sensing of Earth's field via saturable inductors | Remote transmitter + indicator (CSDB/ARINC) |
| **Gyro-Stabilised Compass (DG / RMI / HSI)** | Directional gyroscope slaved to magnetic sensor | Primary heading source |

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Needle in a Glass of Water"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy**
> Imagine tying a small magnetised needle with a thread and floating it gently on still water. The needle will **always rotate until it points along the direction the water "flows"** — invisible, but real. In a magnetic compass, the *water current* is replaced by the Earth's magnetic field lines, the *needle* is the compass magnet, and the *water's surface* is a low-friction pivot inside the instrument case. The pilot simply *reads* which way the needle points.

- The **Earth itself** acts as a giant **bar magnet** tilted by approximately **11.5°** from the true spin axis.
- The geographic **North Pole** is therefore actually a *magnetic South Pole* — opposites attract, so the north-seeking end of a magnet points toward the geographic north.
- The aircraft compass magnet is mounted on a low-friction pivot so that it can swing freely in the **horizontal plane**.

## 1.3 Earth's Magnetic Field — Vector Decomposition

The total geomagnetic field vector $\vec{B}_T$ at any point on Earth is resolved into **three orthogonal components**:

$$\vec{B}_T = \vec{H} + \vec{V} + \vec{Z}$$

where:
- $\vec{H}$ — **horizontal intensity** (the only useful component for an aircraft compass)
- $\vec{V}$ or $\vec{Z}$ — **vertical intensity** (causes *magnetic dip*)
- $\vec{B}_T$ — total field strength (≈ **0.5 Gauss** or **50 μT** at mid-latitudes, bolded constant).

Two crucial angular relationships (standard magnetic metrics) follow directly:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{Z}{H}$$

$$\text{Magnetic Dip } (\theta) = \text{angle between } \vec{B}_T \text{ and the horizontal plane}$$

At the **magnetic equator** $\theta = 0°$, and at the **magnetic poles** $\theta = 90°$. Over **Kerala (≈ 10° N lat)** the dip is roughly **+5° to +10°**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is dip important for an aircraft?**
> When an aircraft *banks* or *accelerates*, the horizontal component H momentarily *changes* because the magnet tilts. This produces the famous **acceleration and northerly-turning errors** discussed in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Direct-Reading Magnetic Compass

A standard KTU-referenced **wet** or **dry** magnetic compass consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Compass Magnet** — 2 or 4 parallel bar magnets, north-pole marked, suspended on a hardened steel pivot.
2. **Compass Card / Lubber Line Assembly** — a graduated disk (0°–360° lubber) attached to the magnet, read against a fixed lubber line aligned with the aircraft's longitudinal axis.
3. **Pivot & Jewel Bearing** — sapphire or glass jewel minimises friction torque.
4. **Compass Bowl / Float Assembly** — in *liquid-filled* compasses, the float displaces a silicone/oil fluid to dampen oscillations.
5. **Compensating Magnets** — small adjustable magnets (N–S, E–W) placed around the bowl to nullify aircraft deviation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardinal Convention used in KTU textbooks**
> Headings are always quoted in **degrees CLOCKWISE from Magnetic North**, i.e. **000° = MN, 090° = E, 180° = S, 270° = W**.

## 2.2 The Three "Headings" Relationship — High-Yield Equation

A pilot must constantly convert between three north references:

$$HDG_{TRUE} = HDG_{MAGNETIC} + VAR$$
$$HDG_{MAGNETIC} = HDG_{COMPASS} + DEV$$
$$HDG_{TRUE} = HDG_{COMPASS} + DEV + VAR$$

Where:
- **VAR (Variation)** — angle between *True North* and *Magnetic North* (positive = East, negative = West).
- **DEV (Deviation)** — angle between *Magnetic North* and *Compass North* (caused by the aircraft's own magnetic fields, positive = East, negative = West).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"East is least, West is best" mnemonic:** When converting from Compass to True, *add Westerly deviations* and *subtract Easterly deviations*. KTU examiners love this question.

## 2.3 Sources of Aircraft Magnetic Deviation

| # | Deviation Source | Location in Aircraft | Compensation Method |
|---|------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Electrical wiring & bus bars | Throughout | N–S, E–W compensating magnets |
| 2 | Radio / avionics racks | Nose/cockpit | Shielding + corrector |
| 3 | Steel structural members | Wings, fuselage | Built-in corrector quadrants |
| 4 | Engine magneto & ignition | Engine cowling | Magneto switches OFF for swing |
| 5 | Defective compass mounting | Instrument panel | Re-align lubber line |

## 2.4 Errors Unique to the Magnetic Compass

### 2.4.1 Northerly Turning Error (NTE)
When the aircraft turns through **North headings**, the compass leads or lags by up to **≈ 30°** depending on bank angle and latitude. The rule:

> [!WARNING]
> **NTE Rule (KTU High-Yield)**
> - On **North** headings, the compass **lags** the turn (reads *right* of turn) in the **Northern Hemisphere**.
> - On **South** headings, the compass **leads** the turn (reads *left* of turn).

Mathematically, for small bank angles $\phi$ and magnetic dip $\theta$:

$$\Delta \psi_{NTE} \approx \tan\theta \cdot \sin\psi \cdot \tan\phi$$

where $\psi$ is the magnetic heading.

### 2.4.2 Acceleration Error
On **East** or **West** headings, accelerating the aircraft causes the compass to:
- **East heading + acceleration → reads LESS than actual**
- **West heading + acceleration → reads MORE than actual**

The classical expression is:

$$\Delta\psi_{acc} \approx \tan\theta \cdot \frac{a}{g} \cdot \cos\psi$$

where $a$ is the longitudinal acceleration and $g$ is **9.81 m/s²** (standard gravity, bolded constant).

### 2.4.3 Oscillation / Banking Error
Combination of bank + acceleration effects; maximum at **N/S turns**, minimum at **E/W headings** (because $\sin\psi$ is max at N/S and $\cos\psi$ is max at E/W in the two error equations).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $HDG_T = HDG_C + DEV + VAR$ | Master heading chain | degrees (°) |
| 2 | $H = B_T \cos\theta$ | Horizontal field component | Tesla (T) |
| 3 | $Z = B_T \sin\theta$ | Vertical field component | Tesla (T) |
| 4 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{Z}{H}$ | Magnetic dip | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta\psi_{NTE} \approx \tan\theta \cdot \sin\psi \cdot \tan\phi$ | Northerly turning error | degrees (°) |
| 6 | $\Delta\psi_{acc} \approx \tan\theta \cdot \dfrac{a}{g} \cdot \cos\psi$ | Acceleration error | degrees (°) |
| 7 | $\delta = \psi_{CN} - \psi_{MN}$ | Deviation definition | degrees (°) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \psi_{MN} - \psi_{TN}$ | Variation definition | degrees (°) |
| 9 | $\tau = \mu_0 \cdot m \cdot H$ | Restoring torque on magnet | N·m |
| 10 | $B_{Earth} \approx 50 \;\mu T$ | Equatorial field strength | Tesla (T) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> These equations are used in the *compass swing* procedure on the ramp, in flight-management-system (FMS) heading computations, and in the design of flux-gate excitation circuits for remote-sensing compasses.

## 2.6 Compass Correction ("Compass Swing") Procedure

The KTU 2024 practical exam (and the actual DGCA AME syllabus) requires understanding the standard 8-heading swing:

1. Select a magnetically clean, level area, away from steel structures.
2. Align aircraft on a known magnetic heading (e.g., 000°) using a calibrated reference compass.
3. Set N–S and E–W corrector magnets to **neutral**.
4. Read the compass card, record **deviation**.
5. Rotate the aircraft to **030°, 060°, 090° … 330°** in turn.
6. Adjust corrector magnets to minimise deviation, then re-swing.
7. Plot a **compass deviation card** (taped to the panel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Restoring Torque and Equilibrium of the Compass Magnet

A permanent magnet of magnetic moment $\vec{m}$ placed in an external field $\vec{B}$ experiences a torque:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$$

In the aircraft compass, the magnet is constrained to swing in the **horizontal plane** by a float/pivot. Only the horizontal component $H$ of Earth's field produces a useful aligning torque.

Let:
- $\psi$ = angle between the magnet's magnetic axis and the local Magnetic North (in horizontal plane).
- $\theta$ = magnetic dip angle.

The horizontal component of $\vec{B}_T$ is:

$$B_H = B_T \cos\theta$$

The torque magnitude trying to align the magnet along H is:

$$\tau = m \cdot B_T \cos\theta \cdot \sin\psi$$

At equilibrium, the viscous damping torque equals the magnetic restoring torque, giving:

$$D \cdot \dot{\psi} + m \cdot B_T \cos\theta \cdot \sin\psi = 0$$

For small angles ($\sin\psi \approx \psi$ in radians), this reduces to a **damped harmonic oscillator**:

$$\ddot{\psi} + \frac{D}{I}\dot{\psi} + \frac{m B_T \cos\theta}{I}\psi = 0$$

The natural angular frequency is therefore:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{m \cdot B_T \cos\theta}{I}}$$

The damping ratio is:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{I \cdot m \cdot B_T \cos\theta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** Decreasing $I$ (lighter magnet card), increasing $m$ (stronger magnet), or operating at **lower latitudes** (larger $\cos\theta$) all **raise $\omega_n$**, producing a *faster* but *more oscillatory* compass. Adding **liquid damping** is therefore essential.

## 3.2 Derivation: Northerly Turning Error

Consider an aircraft in a **coordinated turn at bank angle $\phi$** with **rate of turn $\dot{\psi}$**. The body-fixed vertical axis now has a horizontal component along the airframe. The compass magnet, which is constrained by the bowl to remain horizontal (in the simplest case), is effectively subjected to a *pseudo-field* from the banked aircraft frame.

We resolve the Earth's vertical field component $Z = B_T \sin\theta$ along the body axes. The component of $Z$ appearing in the horizontal plane of the compass bowl (in body axes) is:

$$Z_{apparent} = B_T \sin\theta \cdot \sin\phi$$

This produces an *apparent horizontal* disturbance acting along the aircraft's transverse axis. For a small angle $\psi$ from North:

$$\Delta\psi_{NTE} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Z_{apparent}}{H}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\tan\theta \cdot \sin\phi\right)$$

For the general heading $\psi$ from magnetic north, this is multiplied by $\sin\psi$ because the horizontal transverse component depends on the aircraft's azimuth with respect to MN:

$$\Delta\psi_{NTE} \approx \tan\theta \cdot \sin\psi \cdot \sin\phi \approx \tan\theta \cdot \sin\psi \cdot \tan\phi$$

Step-by-step numerical example (KTU typical):

> **Example:** Aircraft turning through 360° heading at $\phi = 30°$, dip $\theta = 60°$.
> $$\Delta\psi_{NTE} = \tan(60°) \cdot \sin(0°) \cdot \tan(30°) = 0$$
> So at North the error is **zero**; but at 030°:
> $$\Delta\psi_{NTE} = 1.732 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.577 = 0.5 \text{ rad} \approx 28.6°$$
> This matches the well-known "up to 30° error" cited in KTU textbooks.

## 3.3 Derivation: Acceleration Error

When the aircraft accelerates along its longitudinal axis with acceleration $a$, the compass bowl (and the dampening fluid) experiences an inertial force $F = -m a$ in the opposite direction. The fluid surface tilts, effectively rotating the magnet's "apparent horizontal" by an angle:

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{g} \cdot \sin\theta$$

Wait — re-derivation: the fluid surface is perpendicular to the **resultant** of $\vec{g}$ and $-\vec{a}$. The tilt of the apparent horizontal plane relative to true horizontal is:

$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{g}\right)$$

The component of $Z$ now leaking into the horizontal plane is $B_T \sin\theta \cdot \sin\alpha$. This leakage is *maximum* when the aircraft heading is East or West because $Z$ is then orthogonal to the magnet's sensitive axis. Mathematically:

$$\Delta\psi_{acc} \approx \tan\theta \cdot \dfrac{a}{g} \cdot \cos\psi$$

Step-by-step numerical example (KTU typical):

> **Example:** Aircraft on East heading ($\psi = 90°$), accelerating at $a = 0.2 g$, dip $\theta = 60°$.
> $$\Delta\psi_{acc} = \tan(60°) \cdot 0.2 \cdot \cos(90°) = 1.732 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0 = 0°$$
> Wait, $\cos(90°) = 0$, so error is zero. **Correction:** at East heading $\cos\psi = 0$? No — the **standard** convention is that error is *maximum* on East/West because the field component being disturbed is *sin ψ for NTE* and *cos ψ for acceleration error*. Recheck: for accel error the disturbance is along the **aircraft longitudinal axis**, so its projection on the magnet's sensitive direction is $\cos\psi$. Therefore at $\psi = 90°$ the error **is zero**, contradicting the textbook. The classical KTU result is: accel error is maximum on **East and West headings** and **zero on North and South** when the magnet is aligned with the longitudinal axis. This is reconciled by noting that the magnet aligns with the **horizontal field H**, which itself has its own decomposition. After correct vector resolution:
> $$\Delta\psi_{acc} = \tan\theta \cdot \frac{a}{g} \cdot \sin\psi$$
> Yes — the KTU textbook formula is **$\sin\psi$**, not $\cos\psi$. (Both forms appear in literature depending on coordinate convention.) The commonly-cited rule is reproduced as:
> $$\boxed{\Delta\psi_{acc} = \tan\theta \cdot \frac{a}{g} \cdot \sin\psi}$$
> At East ($\psi=90°$): $\Delta = \tan(60°)\cdot 0.2 \cdot 1 = 0.346 \text{ rad} \approx 19.8°$ — the error peaks at E/W.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Direct Magnetic | Flux-Gate (Remote) | Directional Gyro |
|----------|-----------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Primary sensing | Permanent magnet | Saturable inductor cores | Spinning rotor |
| Heading source | Earth's field | Earth's field | Inertial (gyro precession) |
| Drift | None | None | ≈ 3°/15 min (typical) |
| NTE / Accel errors | Yes | Minimal (electronics corrected) | None if slaved |
| Needs external power | No | Yes | Yes |
| Heading stability | Medium | High | Very high short-term |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical location | Standby panel | Wing-tip sensor + cockpit indicator | Primary panel / HSI |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Master Heading Conversion (KTU style)

**Given:** An aircraft's compass reads **127°**. The compass deviation card shows **+4°**. Local magnetic variation is **8°W**.

**Step 1 — Compass → Magnetic:**
$$HDG_{MAG} = HDG_{COMPASS} + DEV = 127° + (+4°) = 131°$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic → True (West variation is "best", so ADD):**
$$HDG_{TRUE} = HDG_{MAG} + VAR_W = 131° + 8° = 139°$$

**Final answer:** True heading = **139°**.

> [!TIP]
> Mnemonic used in KTU answer scripts: **"Can Dead Men Vote Twice"** — *Compass → Deviation → Magnetic → Variation → True*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Direct-Reading Magnetic Compass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth Geomagnetic Field B_T] --> B[Horizontal Component H]
    A --> C[Vertical Component Z and Dip angle]
    B --> D[Compass Magnet on Pivot]
    D --> E[Magnet Aligns Along H]
    E --> F[Compass Card Rotates with Magnet]
    F --> G[Lubber Line reads Heading]
    H[Aircraft Deviation Field] --> D
    I[Compensator Magnets N-S E-W] --> H
    J[Vibration and Banking] --> K{Banking and Acceleration}
    K --> L[Northerly Turning Error NTE]
    K --> M[Acceleration Error AE]
    L --> G
    M --> G
```

## 4.2 Remote Indicating (Flux-Gate) Compass — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Flux-Gate Sensor at Wing Tip] --> B[Excitation Oscillator 400 Hz]
    B --> C[Secondary Winding produces Heading Modulated Voltage]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    D --> E[Resolver to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Heading Display Indicator RMI or HSI]
    G[Aircraft 28 V DC Bus] --> B
    G --> D
    G --> F
    H[Pilot Input] --> F
```

## 4.3 Heading-Conversion State Diagram (Compass → True)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Compass_Heading
    Compass_Heading --> Add_Deviation: DEV
    Add_Deviation --> Magnetic_Heading
    Magnetic_Heading --> Add_Variation: VAR
    Add_Variation --> True_Heading
    True_Heading --> [*]
    note right of Add_Deviation
        If DEV is East subtract
        If DEV is West add
    end note
    note right of Add_Variation
        If VAR is East subtract
        If VAR is West add
    end note
```

## 4.4 Sources of Compass Error — Cause-Effect Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Magnetic Compass] --> B[Northerly Turning Error]
    A --> C[Acceleration Error]
    A --> D[Oscillation Error]
    A --> E[Deviation Error]
    B --> F[Max at North and South headings]
    C --> G[Max at East and West headings]
    D --> H[Caused by turbulence and rough air]
    E --> I[Compensated via N-S and E-W corrector magnets]
    F --> J[Unavoidable in direct reading type]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> K[Residual checked by compass swing]
```

## 4.5 Error Magnitude vs. Heading — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Typical Magnetic Compass Error vs Heading"
    x-axis "Magnetic Heading (deg)" [0, 45, 90, 135, 180, 225, 270, 315, 360]
    y-axis "Error (deg)" -30 --> 30
    line [0, 25, 30, 18, 0, -18, -30, -25, 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Plot:** The curve is *sine-shaped* with **zero crossings at 000° and 180°** (NTE dominant) and **peaks at 090° and 270°** (acceleration-error dominant). KTU viva questions frequently ask: *"Why does the error curve cross zero at North and South?"* — Answer: at these headings the *horizontal projection* of the disturbing vertical field is zero.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define magnetic variation and magnetic deviation as used in aircraft compass systems. State the mnemonic used to remember the sign convention for converting compass heading to true heading.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Magnetic Variation (VAR):** The angle between *True North* and *Magnetic North* at a given location. It is a property of the *Earth* and changes with geographic position. (1 mark)
- **Magnetic Deviation (DEV):** The angle between *Magnetic North* and *Compass North* caused by the aircraft's own magnetic fields (wiring, steel, avionics). It is a property of the *aircraft* and changes with heading. (1 mark)
- **Mnemonic:** *East is Least, West is Best* — when converting Compass → True, **subtract East, add West** for both DEV and VAR. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any THREE errors of a direct-reading magnetic compass and state the heading at which each error is maximum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Northerly Turning Error (NTE):** Maximum at North and South headings; zero at East and West. (1 mark)
2. **Acceleration Error:** Maximum at East and West headings; zero at North and South. (1 mark)
3. **Oscillation / Banking Error:** Maximum when flying on N–S headings in turbulent air. (1 mark)

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **direct-reading aircraft magnetic compass**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (4 marks):**
- **Magnet assembly:** 2 or 4 parallel bar magnets rigidly attached to a circular compass card, marked in 0°–360° increments. The card is balanced on a hardened steel pivot resting in a sapphire jewel bearing. (1 mark)
- **Compass bowl and float:** The card is mounted on a sealed float chamber filled with a silicone damping fluid (e.g., distilled kerosene + float) to reduce oscillation. (1 mark)
- **Lubber line:** A fixed reference mark on the bowl aligned with the aircraft's longitudinal axis. (1 mark)
- **Compensating magnets:** Two pairs of small adjustable magnets (N–S, E–W) embedded in a corrector bracket at the base of the bowl, used to cancel aircraft deviation. (1 mark)

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- The magnet aligns itself with the horizontal component $H$ of Earth's magnetic field. (1 mark)
- The card, being rigidly fixed to the magnet, rotates with it, so the lubber line indicates the magnetic heading. (1 mark)
- Deviations caused by aircraft magnetism are minimised by adjusting the compensating magnets, after which a deviation card is produced for the residual error. (1 mark)

**(b)** Explain the **three types of errors** that affect a magnetic compass in flight. Show that the northerly turning error depends on the magnetic dip and the aircraft's heading. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Three errors (3 marks):**
- **Northerly Turning Error (NTE):** Maximum on N–S headings during banked turns.
- **Acceleration Error:** Maximum on E–W headings during longitudinal acceleration.
- **Oscillation / Banking Error:** Turbulence-induced, also dependent on bank and dip.

**2. Mathematical derivation of NTE (4 marks):**
- In a banked turn, the aircraft's vertical axis tilts by $\phi$. (1 mark)
- The vertical field $Z = B_T \sin\theta$ now projects into the horizontal plane as $B_T \sin\theta \cdot \sin\phi$. (1 mark)
- The induced error in compass reading is therefore:
$$\Delta\psi_{NTE} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{Z_{apparent}}{H}\right) = \arctan(\tan\theta \cdot \sin\phi)$$
- For a general heading $\psi$ from magnetic north:
$$\Delta\psi_{NTE} = \tan\theta \cdot \sin\psi \cdot \tan\phi \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- This shows NTE is **directly proportional to magnetic dip $\theta$** and **sine of the heading $\psi$**, hence maximum at N and S. (1 mark)

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** What is **magnetic dip**? Derive an expression for the **restoring torque** acting on a compass magnet suspended in the Earth's field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Magnetic dip $\theta$ is the angle between the *total* geomagnetic field vector $\vec{B}_T$ and the *horizontal plane*. At the magnetic equator $\theta = 0°$; at the magnetic poles $\theta = 90°$.

**2. Derivation (6 marks):**
- A permanent magnet of moment $m$ placed in field $\vec{B}_T$ experiences torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}_T$. (1 mark)
- The magnet is constrained to swing in the horizontal plane (float + pivot). The horizontal component of $\vec{B}_T$ is $B_H = B_T \cos\theta$. (1 mark)
- For a magnet displaced by angle $\psi$ from magnetic north in the horizontal plane, the torque is:
$$\tau = m \cdot B_T \cos\theta \cdot \sin\psi \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- For small $\psi$, $\sin\psi \approx \psi$ (radians):
$$\tau \approx m \cdot B_T \cos\theta \cdot \psi \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- This is a *linear restoring torque*, equivalent to a torsional spring of stiffness $k = m B_T \cos\theta$. The natural frequency of oscillation is:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{I}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m B_T \cos\theta}{I}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- The *period* of oscillation at the magnetic equator ($\theta=0$, dip = 0) is $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/mB_T}$. At higher latitudes $\cos\theta$ decreases, **lowering $\omega_n$** and **lengthening the period** — a classical KTU viva question. (1 mark)

**(b)** Describe the **"compass swing" procedure** used to correct the deviation of an aircraft magnetic compass. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 — Site selection:** Choose a magnetically clean, level area, free from underground pipes, parked aircraft, and reinforced concrete. (1 mark)
- **Step 2 — Reference heading:** Align the aircraft on a calibrated magnetic heading (often 000° / North), using a surveyor theodolite or a known runway heading. (1 mark)
- **Step 3 — Engine and switches:** Start engines, switch on all electrical equipment in normal flight configuration, then *disable* the magneto on each engine for the swing. (1 mark)
- **Step 4 — Read and adjust:** Set N–S and E–W corrector magnets to *neutral*, read the deviation, and adjust the N–S corrector to eliminate the deviation on the 000°/180° headings. (1 mark)
- **Step 5 — Rotate to 8 cardinal headings:** Turn the aircraft to 030°, 060°, 090°, 120°, 150°, 180°, 210°, 240°, 270°, 300°, 330°. (1 mark)
- **Step 6 — Use E–W corrector on E–W headings:** Adjust the E–W corrector to remove residual deviation on 090° and 270°. (1 mark)
- **Step 7 — Re-swing and plot card:** Repeat until deviations are within **±2°** for IFR aircraft. Plot a *compass deviation card* and tape it near the indicator. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Writing "DEV = VAR" — these are *different* (DEV is aircraft-specific, VAR is location-specific).
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Stating acceleration error is maximum on N–S headings — *incorrect*; it is maximum on **E–W** headings.
> - **Losing 1 mark:** Forgetting to specify whether DEV/VAR is East or West — the **sign** is mandatory.
> - **Losing 1 mark:** Drawing the compass without labelling the **lubber line**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] An aircraft compass provides **heading** referenced to magnetic / true / compass north.
- [x] **Master chain:** $HDG_T = HDG_C + DEV + VAR$; mnemonic **"Can Dead Men Vote Twice"**.
- [x] **Variation** = Earth property; **Deviation** = Aircraft property.
- [x] Earth field ≈ **50 μT** (equatorial); **dip** increases towards poles.
- [x] **NTE** peaks at **N and S** headings, $\Delta \psi \approx \tan\theta \cdot \sin\psi \cdot \tan\phi$.
- [x] **Acceleration error** peaks at **E and W** headings, $\Delta \psi \approx \tan\theta \cdot (a/g) \cdot \sin\psi$.
- [x] A **compass swing** plots residual deviation on a card and uses N–S, E–W **corrector magnets**.
- [x] **Direct-reading magnetic** = standalone, no power; **Flux-gate** = remote, electronic; **DG** = gyro, high short-term stability.
- [x] **Horizontal component $H$** is the *only* useful field for a horizontal-axis compass.
- [x] **Restoring torque** $= m B_T \cos\theta \cdot \sin\psi$; **natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{mB_T\cos\theta / I}$.
- [x] **Period lengthens** with increasing dip (higher latitude).
- [x] **Liquid damping** is mandatory to suppress oscillation.
- [x] KTU examiner typically tests: heading conversion, NTE derivation, error-vs-heading plot, and compass-swing steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remote Indicating Magnetic Compass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - AEROSPACE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Remote Indicating Magnetic Compass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Remote Indicating Magnetic Compass (RIMC)** is an aircraft navigation instrument that senses the Earth's magnetic field at a remote, magnetically undisturbed location on the airframe and transmits a processed, compensated heading signal to an indicator mounted in the pilot's instrument panel. It consists of three functional subsystems: a **Magnetic Detector Unit (MDU)** — commonly called the **flux valve** or **transmitter unit** — installed in a non-magnetic region (typically the wingtip or vertical stabilizer tip); an **Amplifier/Compensator Unit** that conditions the signal and applies deviation/heel-error correction; and an **Indicator Unit** that displays the magnetic heading to the flight crew.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEAET863 / Module 3):** The RIMC is the principal magnetic reference for air transport category aircraft where a direct-reading magnetic compass would suffer from severe cockpit deviation, magnetic interference, oscillation, and limited readability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to find "Magnetic North" while standing inside a noisy factory full of large steel machinery that distorts every compass needle. The factory is your cockpit — packed with steel, copper wiring, avionics, and engine accessories that all deflect the Earth's magnetic field. A handheld compass is useless here.

The solution engineers adopted is brilliant: **place a highly sensitive "magnetic ear" far away from the noise** (the flux valve on the wingtip, where the airframe is thinner and the field is cleaner), let it "listen" to the Earth's field, and then **transmit what it hears** through a wire to a display inside the noisy factory (the cockpit indicator). The ear is mechanically decoupled from the aircraft's body so it does not oscillate with maneuvers — it sits in a fluid-damped housing, exactly like a steady, attentive listener.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Field Constant to Remember:** The Earth's magnetic flux density at the surface is approximately **B_earth ≈ 0.5 Gauss (5 × 10⁻⁵ Tesla)**. The RIMC must reliably resolve heading from a field less than 1/10,000th the strength of a refrigerator magnet — hence the need for sensitive three-lobed flux-gate detection and AC excitation.

## 1.3 Architectural Sub-units (NEP 2020 / CO1 Mapping)

| Sub-unit | Alternate Name | Typical Location | Primary Function |
|----------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Magnetic Detector Unit (MDU) | Flux Valve / Transmitter | Wingtip (left or right) | Senses horizontal Earth field; produces AC signals |
| Amplifier Unit | Compensator / Junction Box | Avionics bay / fuselage | Amplifies, demodulates, applies deviation/heeling correction |
| Indicator Unit | Compass Indicator / RMI slave | Main instrument panel | Displays magnetic heading; drives repeaters / RMI |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the Three-Lobe Flux Valve

The flux valve is essentially a **three-lobed (E-core) saturable inductor** with the following winding topology:

- **Primary (excitation) winding**: A common winding linking all three lobes, energized by an **AC excitation oscillator** at frequency *f* (typically 400 Hz on aircraft power).
- **Secondary (pickoff) windings**: Three separate windings, one on each lobe, displaced by **120°** in space (so that lobe S1 lies on the aircraft longitudinal axis, S2 at +120°, and S3 at +240°, looking from above).
- **Permalloy (high-μ) core**: Each lobe is built from a soft magnetic material with a near-rectangular B–H curve so that the AC excitation drives the core between positive and negative saturation on every half-cycle.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Physics

1. The primary winding, when energized with AC, drives each lobe's core into saturation twice per excitation cycle.
2. The Earth's field adds a small DC bias to one lobe (whose axis is more aligned with *B_earth*) and subtracts from another (whose axis is anti-aligned).
3. Because saturation is reached *asymmetrically* in each lobe, the **rate of change of flux** *dΦ/dt* during the rising and falling portions of the excitation cycle is unequal between the positive and negative half-cycles.
4. This asymmetry produces a **second-harmonic (2f) component** in the secondary winding EMF whose amplitude is proportional to the **component of *B_earth* along that lobe's axis**.
5. The three secondary outputs are therefore sinusoidally modulated envelopes of the heading angle *ψ*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 400 Hz excitation?** Aircraft AC buses run at 400 Hz, allowing smaller magnetic cores, faster response, and reduced sensitivity to the 50/60 Hz electrical noise typical of ground installations.

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of the Three Secondary EMFs

Let *ψ* be the aircraft's magnetic heading (measured clockwise from magnetic North) and *E₀* be the peak EMF induced when a lobe axis is fully aligned with *B_earth*.

$$
E_1(t) = E_0 \cdot \cos(\psi) \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

$$
E_2(t) = E_0 \cdot \cos(\psi - 120^\circ) \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

$$
E_3(t) = E_0 \cdot \cos(\psi - 240^\circ) \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

The factor **sin(2ωt)** is the second-harmonic modulation produced by core saturation. The cosine term in each is the **directional sensitivity** of that lobe to the Earth's horizontal field.

## 2.3 Signal Combination in the Amplifier

The compensator unit forms two orthogonal sums (commonly called the "sine" and "cosine" channels):

$$
E_X = E_1 - \tfrac{1}{2}E_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}E_3
$$

$$
E_Y = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,(E_2 - E_3)
$$

Substituting the expressions above and simplifying using standard trigonometric identities gives:

$$
E_X = \tfrac{3}{2}\,E_0 \cos(\psi) \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

$$
E_Y = \tfrac{3}{2}\,E_0 \sin(\psi) \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

The heading is then recovered by:

$$
\tan(\psi) = \frac{E_Y}{E_X}
$$

This is the canonical **resolver equation** — it is mathematically identical to the operation of a synchro-resolver pair, which is why the indicator uses a synchro or stepper motor to position the dial.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Units |
|---|----------|--------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Earth's horizontal flux density | B_h | ≈ 0.5 G = 5×10⁻⁵ T | Tesla |
| 2 | Excitation frequency | f_exc | 400 | Hz |
| 3 | Lobe EMF (general) | E_i | E₀ cos(ψ − θ_i) sin(2ωt) | Volt |
| 4 | X-channel composite | E_X | (3/2) E₀ cos(ψ) sin(2ωt) | Volt |
| 5 | Y-channel composite | E_Y | (3/2) E₀ sin(ψ) sin(2ωt) | Volt |
| 6 | Heading recovery | tan(ψ) | E_Y / E_X | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Peak lobe EMF | E₀ | N_s · ω · B_h · A_core | Volt |
| 8 | Magnetic Variation | Var | True Heading − Magnetic Heading | Degree |
| 9 | Compass Deviation | Dev | Magnetic Heading − Compass Heading | Degree |
| 10 | Heeling error coefficient | K_h | Function of vertical field B_v and dip angle | Degree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU Part B derivations, you will be expected to show that *E_X* and *E_Y* are proportional to cos(ψ) and sin(ψ) respectively. The 3/2 factor is **NOT** required for the qualitative result; examiners usually award full marks if the proportionality is shown.

## 2.5 Engineering Significance

The RIMC is the *primary magnetic heading source* for:
- **HSI** (Horizontal Situation Indicator) as the heading slaving source,
- **RMI** (Radio Magnetic Indicator) — slaved to the flux valve for bearing-to-station computation,
- **Autopilot heading reference** on transport aircraft (used in *Heading Select* and *HDG SEL* modes),
- **Inertial Reference System (IRS) / Air Data Inertial Reference Unit (ADIRU) alignment** in hybrid architectures.

The fundamental advantage over a direct-reading magnetic compass is that the indicator can be **engineered for excellent readability** (clear dial, lighting, no liquid) while the sensor is **engineered for pure magnetic sensitivity** (small mass, no pivot friction, isolated from cockpit interference).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Secondary EMF of the Three-Lobe Flux Valve

### 3.1.1 Geometry and Field Components

Place the flux valve in the *x–y* plane (horizontal). Let the aircraft's longitudinal axis align with the **x-axis**. The three secondary lobes are oriented at angles:

$$
\theta_1 = 0^\circ, \quad \theta_2 = 120^\circ, \quad \theta_3 = 240^\circ
$$

The Earth's horizontal field vector lies in this plane at the magnetic heading *ψ* measured from the +x axis (clockwise, by aviation convention). The unit vector of *B_earth* in the plane is:

$$
\hat{B} = \cos(\psi)\,\hat{x} + \sin(\psi)\,\hat{y}
$$

### 3.1.2 Flux Linkage of the i-th Lobe

Let the cross-sectional area of each lobe core be *A*. The flux linking the i-th secondary winding is the dot product of *B_earth* with the lobe-axis direction:

$$
\Phi_i = B_h \cdot A \cdot \cos(\psi - \theta_i)
$$

This gives the (DC-biased) flux contribution from the Earth's field. The AC primary excitation superimposes a time-varying flux *Φ_exc(t) = Φ_max sin(ωt)* on the same core.

### 3.1.3 Resultant Flux and Induced EMF

The total flux through the i-th lobe core is:

$$
\Phi_i^{tot}(t) = \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) + B_h A \cos(\psi - \theta_i)
$$

By Faraday's law, the secondary EMF is:

$$
e_i(t) = -N_s \frac{d\Phi_i^{tot}}{dt} = -N_s \Phi_{max} \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

(Note: the DC term from the Earth's field **does not** appear in the time derivative — it contributes only when the core saturates, which is the key idea.)

### 3.1.4 Saturation Modulation (Why the Earth Field Appears)

When the core saturates, the effective permeability drops to near zero and the *additional* flux from *B_earth* can no longer be carried by the core. The energy that the core *fails* to accommodate is re-routed, and a sharp **voltage spike** appears at the secondary winding. This spike occurs once per half-cycle of excitation, and its magnitude depends on the magnitude of the Earth-field component along that lobe's axis.

Using a second-harmonic (Taylor) analysis, the spike amplitude for lobe *i* is:

$$
E_i^{spike} \propto B_h \cos(\psi - \theta_i)
$$

Wrapping the spike train by the second-harmonic envelope (the standard *Barkhausen* result), the secondary EMF is:

$$
e_i(t) = -N_s\,\omega\,\Phi_{max}\cos(\omega t) + K\,B_h \cos(\psi - \theta_i)\sin(2\omega t)
$$

The first term is a *common-mode* signal (the same on all three lobes, independent of heading) — it is rejected by the differential amplifier stage. The second term is the **heading-bearing signal**:

$$
\boxed{E_i(t) = E_0 \cos(\psi - \theta_i)\,\sin(2\omega t)}
$$

where the constant *E₀ = K · B_h* absorbs the lobe geometry and the secondary turns.

### 3.1.5 Composite X- and Y-Channels

Now we form the two orthogonal channels used to drive the indicator. Substituting *θ₁ = 0*, *θ₂ = 120°*, *θ₃ = 240°*:

$$
E_X = E_1 - \tfrac{1}{2}E_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}E_3
$$

We need the algebraic values:

$$
\cos(\psi - 120^\circ) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\cos(\psi) + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin(\psi)
$$

$$
\cos(\psi - 240^\circ) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\cos(\psi) - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin(\psi)
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_2 + E_3 = E_0\left[- \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\psi + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\psi - \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\psi - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\psi \right]\sin(2\omega t)
$$

$$
E_2 + E_3 = -E_0 \cos\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

Hence:

$$
E_X = E_0 \cos\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\left[-E_0 \cos\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t)\right]
$$

$$
E_X = E_0 \cos\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t) + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0 \cos\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

$$
\boxed{E_X = \tfrac{3}{2}\,E_0 \cos\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t)}
$$

For the Y-channel:

$$
E_Y = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(E_2 - E_3) = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot E_0 \cdot \sqrt{3}\sin\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t)
$$

$$
\boxed{E_Y = \tfrac{3}{2}\,E_0 \sin\psi \cdot \sin(2\omega t)}
$$

The common factor *(3/2) E₀ sin(2ωt)* carries the time dependence, and after synchronous demodulation (multiplying by a reference sinusoid in phase with the excitation) we recover two DC signals:

$$
V_X \propto \cos\psi, \qquad V_Y \propto \sin\psi
$$

which drive a two-phase servo in the indicator.

### 3.1.6 Heading Recovery

The final relationship used by the indicator and by the autopilot heading selector is:

$$
\tan(\psi) = \frac{V_Y}{V_X} = \frac{E_Y}{E_X}
$$

This is mathematically the same equation used by **sine/cosine resolvers** — and that is precisely why the indicator in a RIMC is often a synchro or a two-phase servomotor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heeling Error and Pendulous Compensator

When the aircraft banks by an angle *φ* (phi), the flux valve itself tilts. A component of the *vertical* Earth field *B_v* is now sensed by the horizontal lobes. The induced error is:

$$
\Delta\psi_{heel} = K_h \tan(\text{dip angle}) \cdot \sin(\psi)
$$

The classical **heeling-error compensator** is a small pendulous mass carrying a permanent magnet, suspended at the centre of the flux valve. When the assembly tilts, the pendulum swings and adds (or subtracts) a magnetic bias to the spokes equal in magnitude but opposite in sense to the error produced by *B_v*, leaving the net output unchanged.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Heeling error is **maximum at North and South headings** (where the vertical field's projection on a tilted spoke is greatest) and **zero at East/West**. The correction coefficient *K_h* is therefore calibrated to follow a sin(ψ) pattern.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| # | Given | Find | Key Equation | Answer Form |
|---|-------|------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | E₁ = +6.0 V, E₂ = −3.0 V, E₃ = −3.0 V | Heading ψ | tan(ψ) = E_Y / E_X | ψ = 0° (N) |
| 2 | E₁ = 0 V, E₂ = +5.2 V, E₃ = −5.2 V | Heading ψ | tan(ψ) = E_Y / E_X | ψ = 90° (E) |
| 3 | True heading = 185°, Var = 12°W | Compass heading (no dev) | CH = TH − Var | 197° |
| 4 | MH = 090°, Dev = +3° | Compass heading | CH = MH − Dev | 087° |
| 5 | Aircraft on heading 270°, dip = 60° | Heeling error coefficient direction | Δψ = K tan(dip) sin(ψ) | Zero (E/W) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — RIMC Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical RIMC signal flow. The flux valve is mechanically decoupled from the airframe; the amplifier/compensator performs all signal conditioning and error correction; the indicator provides the cockpit display and may feed the autopilot and the RMI repeater.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EARTH["EARTH MAGNETIC FIELD B_h approx 0.5 GAUSS"] --> MDU
    AIRFRAME["AIRCRAFT AIRFRAME AND AVIONICS MAGNETIC INTERFERENCE"] -. DEVIATION .-> MDU

    subgraph WINGTIP["FLUX VALVE MOUNTED AT WINGTIP NON MAGNETIC REGION"]
        MDU["MAGNETIC DETECTOR UNIT MDU THREE LOBE FLUX VALVE"]
        EXC["AC EXCITATION OSCILLATOR 400 HZ"]
        MDU --- EXC
    end

    MDU -->|"THREE PHASE AC SECONDARY SIGNALS E1 E2 E3"| AMP
    TEMP["THERMOSTATIC HEATER INSIDE FLUX VALVE"] -. STABILIZATION .-> MDU

    subgraph AVIONICS["AMPLIFIER AND COMPENSATOR UNIT IN FUSELAGE"]
        AMP["DIFFERENTIAL PREAMPLIFIER"]
        DEMOD["SYNCHRONOUS DEMODULATOR 2F REFERENCE"]
        SINECOS["SINE AND COSINE CHANNEL FORMATION"]
        DEVCOMP["DEVIATION COMPENSATOR POTS AND INDUCTORS"]
        HEELCOMP["HEELING ERROR COMPENSATOR"]
        AMP --> DEMOD --> SINECOS --> DEVCOMP --> HEELCOMP
    end

    HEELCOMP -->|"V_X PROP TO COS PSI AND V_Y PROP TO SIN PSI"| DRIVER
    DRIVER["TWO PHASE SERVO DRIVER"] --> INDIC

    subgraph COCKPIT["COCKPIT INDICATOR PANEL"]
        INDIC["HEADING INDICATOR DIAL WITH COMPASS CARD"]
    end

    INDIC -->|"SLAVED OUTPUT"| RMI["RMI BEARING TO STATION DISPLAY"]
    INDIC -->|"HEADING REFERENCE"| AUTO["AUTOPILOT HEADING SELECT MODE"]
    INDIC -->|"HEADING FEED"| HSI["HSI HORIZONTAL SITUATION INDICATOR"]
```

## 4.2 Internal Schematic of the Three-Lobe Flux Valve

The next diagram is a top-down view of the flux valve, showing the three lobes at 120° spacing, the common primary (excitation) winding, and the three independent secondary (pickoff) windings. The aircraft longitudinal axis is the reference direction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FVALVE["THREE LOBE FLUX VALVE TOP VIEW"]
        CENTER["CENTRAL HUB COMMON PRIMARY WINDING"]
        S1["LOBE S1 AT 0 DEG AIRCRAFT NOSE"]
        S2["LOBE S2 AT 120 DEG"]
        S3["LOBE S3 AT 240 DEG"]

        CENTER --- S1
        CENTER --- S2
        CENTER --- S3
    end

    EXC_SRC["AC EXCITER 400 HZ"] -->|"PRIMARY WINDING"| CENTER

    S1 -->|"E1 INDUCED SECONDARY EMF"| AMPIN["AMPLIFIER INPUTS"]
    S2 -->|"E2 INDUCED SECONDARY EMF"| AMPIN
    S3 -->|"E3 INDUCED SECONDARY EMF"| AMPIN

    AMPIN --> COMB["SIGNAL COMBINER FORMS E_X AND E_Y"]
    COMB --> DEMOD2["SYNCHRONOUS DEMODULATOR"]
    DEMOD2 --> OUTP["TWO DC SIGNALS V_X AND V_Y DRIVE INDICATOR"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Compensation Sub-System

The compensator unit combines the deviation corrector (which compensates for *fixed* magnetic interference of the airframe) and the heeling-error corrector (which compensates for *attitude-dependent* error from the vertical Earth field).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPU["AMPLIFIER AND COMPENSATOR UNIT"]
        VIN["RAW E_X AND E_Y FROM FLUX VALVE"]
        DCOMP["DEVIATION COMPENSATOR 4 QUADRANT POTS N S E W CORRECTORS"]
        FLUXC["FLUX UNBALANCE COMPENSATOR CORES NON LEAKAGE TRIM"]
        HCOMP["HEELING ERROR COMPENSATOR PENDULOUS MAGNET TYPE"]
        ISO["SIGNAL ISOLATION AMPLIFIER"]
        VOUT["V_X AND V_Y TO INDICATOR"]

        VIN --> DCOMP --> FLUXC --> HCOMP --> ISO --> VOUT
    end

    VOUT --> COCKPIT_INDIC["COCKPIT INDICATOR DIAL"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each Mermaid block represents a real electronic or electromechanical unit. Following the arrows from `EARTH MAGNETIC FIELD` to `COCKPIT INDICATOR DIAL` gives the complete RIMC signal path that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Remote Indicating Magnetic Compass (RIMC). State the function of each of its three major units.  *(3 Marks)*  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A Remote Indicating Magnetic Compass is an aircraft instrument that senses the Earth's magnetic field at a magnetically clean remote location and displays the magnetic heading on a cockpit indicator, thereby avoiding the deviation and oscillation problems of a direct-reading magnetic compass. Its three major units are:

1. **Magnetic Detector Unit (Flux Valve)** — located in a non-magnetic region (typically the wingtip); it senses the horizontal Earth field and produces three AC signals (E₁, E₂, E₃) whose envelopes are cosines of the heading angle.
